advent-01-a.logline: Advent 1/4 — The new church year opens with a jolt: stay awake, because the Lord is coming when you least expect it.
advent-01-a.trajectory: Advent A follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and readiness, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = Signs of the Messiah (Gaudete), Week 4 = Joseph's dream and the Annunciation fulfilled.

first.synopsis: Isaiah paints a grand vision of the future: all nations streaming toward the Lord's mountain to learn his ways. The famous line appears — "They shall beat their swords into plowshares." It ends with an invitation: "O house of Jacob, come, let us walk in the light of the Lord!" This sets the Advent tone of hopeful longing for a world that doesn't exist yet but is promised.

second.synopsis: Paul delivers a wake-up call: "It is the hour now for you to awake from sleep." He frames the Christian life as a transition from night to day — throw off the works of darkness, put on the armor of light. The climactic instruction is striking: "Put on the Lord Jesus Christ." This is one of the few second readings that directly matches the Advent 1 theme of alertness and urgency.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus compares his return to the days of Noah — people eating, drinking, and going about their business, completely unaware until the flood swept them away. Two people in a field, one taken, one left. The core command: "Stay awake! For you do not know on which day your Lord will come." He uses the image of a thief breaking in at night to drive home the point: readiness is everything.

advent-01-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to wake up. The church year begins not with a lullaby but with an alarm clock. Isaiah's vision of universal peace — swords into plowshares — is the destination, but Jesus warns that we can sleepwalk right past it. The first reading and gospel are perfectly paired: one shows where we're headed, the other insists we pay attention to the journey. Paul's second reading reinforces the theme unusually well, urging us to "put on the armor of light." The tone is urgent but hopeful — not fear-based, but an invitation to live awake.

advent-01-b.logline: Advent 1/4 — A potter cries out for his clay to hold its shape, while Jesus warns: Watch, because you don't know the hour.
advent-01-b.trajectory: Advent B follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and longing, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = John testifies to the light (Gaudete), Week 4 = The Annunciation to Mary.

first.synopsis: This is a raw, desperate prayer. Isaiah cries out: "Oh, that you would rend the heavens and come down!" The people confess they've withered like leaves, their guilt carrying them away like the wind. But threaded through the lament is trust: "You, Lord, are our father; we are the clay and you the potter: we are all the work of your hands." It's one of the most emotionally vulnerable readings in the lectionary.

second.synopsis: Paul thanks God for the gifts given to the Corinthians — enriched in every way, not lacking in any spiritual gift — as they wait for Christ's revelation. He assures them that God "will keep you firm to the end" and that "God is faithful." This reading follows its own track, not directly tied to the watchfulness theme, but its emphasis on God's faithfulness complements the first reading's cry for God to show up.

gospel.synopsis: Mark's version of the watchfulness parable is compact and punchy. A man travels abroad, leaves his servants in charge, and orders the gatekeeper to watch. Jesus hammers the point: you don't know if the master comes in the evening, at midnight, at cockcrow, or in the morning. The final line lands like a command to the whole church: "What I say to you, I say to all: Watch!"

advent-01-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to long for God honestly. Isaiah's plea — "rend the heavens and come down!" — is one of the most powerful Advent prayers in scripture, the cry of a people who know they've drifted and desperately want God to close the distance. Jesus matches that longing with a practical instruction: stay alert, keep watching. The emotional arc moves from vulnerability to vigilance. The second reading runs on a parallel track, but Paul's reminder that "God is faithful" answers Isaiah's anguished question. The tone is aching and expectant.

advent-01-c.logline: Advent 1/4 — Cosmic signs shake the heavens, but Jesus says: stand up and raise your heads, because your redemption is near.
advent-01-c.trajectory: Advent C follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and cosmic signs, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = John's practical instructions (Gaudete), Week 4 = The Visitation of Mary and Elizabeth.

first.synopsis: Jeremiah delivers a short, direct promise: God will fulfill what he promised to Israel and Judah. A "just shoot" will rise from David's line and do what is right. Judah will be safe, Jerusalem will be secure, and the city will be called "The Lord our justice." It's brief but loaded — a compact prophecy of the Messiah as a king who brings safety and righteousness.

second.synopsis: Paul prays that the Thessalonians' love will "increase and abound" for one another, strengthening their hearts to be "blameless in holiness" at the coming of Christ. He then gently exhorts them to keep living as they've been taught — and to do so "even more." It's pastoral encouragement, not directly tied to the apocalyptic theme of the other readings, but it grounds the cosmic drama in everyday faithfulness.

gospel.synopsis: Luke's apocalyptic passage is dramatic: signs in the sun, moon, and stars; nations in dismay; the sea roaring. People will die of fright. But then comes the pivot — "when these signs begin to happen, stand erect and raise your heads, because your redemption is at hand." Jesus warns against letting hearts become "drowsy from carousing and drunkenness and the anxieties of daily life." Be vigilant. Pray for strength.

advent-01-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to lift our heads in hope even when the world feels like it's shaking apart. Jeremiah's promise of a just ruler from David's line is the anchor, and Luke's apocalyptic gospel is the storm — but the remarkable thing is that Jesus tells us the storm itself is a sign of rescue. "Stand erect and raise your heads, because your redemption is at hand." The second reading runs on its own track but quietly reinforces the message: grow in love, stay faithful, don't get drowsy. The tone is dramatic yet ultimately hopeful — not doom, but dawn.

advent-02-a.logline: Advent 2/4 — John the Baptist bursts onto the scene with fire and fury, calling for repentance as the kingdom draws near.
advent-02-a.trajectory: Advent A follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and readiness, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = Signs of the Messiah (Gaudete), Week 4 = Joseph's dream and the Annunciation fulfilled.

first.synopsis: One of the most beautiful messianic prophecies in all of scripture. A shoot sprouts from the stump of Jesse, bearing the spirit of wisdom, understanding, counsel, and strength. This ruler will judge with justice for the poor. Then the imagery turns wild and gorgeous: "The wolf shall be a guest of the lamb... a little child to guide them." The earth will be filled with knowledge of the Lord "as water covers the sea." This is the dream of creation restored.

second.synopsis: Paul urges the Roman community to live in harmony, welcoming one another as Christ welcomed them. He connects the Scriptures to endurance and hope, and emphasizes that Christ came for both Jews and Gentiles — so that all might "with one voice glorify God." This reading connects well to the first reading's universal vision of all nations finding peace.

gospel.synopsis: John the Baptist appears in the desert — camel hair, leather belt, locusts and wild honey — preaching: "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand!" He's Isaiah's "voice crying in the desert." When Pharisees and Sadducees show up, he calls them a "brood of vipers" and warns that "the ax lies at the root of the trees." He contrasts his water baptism with the one coming after him, who will baptize "with the Holy Spirit and fire."

advent-02-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hold two images together: Isaiah's peaceable kingdom where wolves lie down with lambs, and John the Baptist's fierce demand for repentance in the desert. The first reading is the vision; the gospel is the preparation required to get there. You can't have the lamb-and-wolf harmony without first letting the ax fall at the root of whatever isn't bearing fruit. Paul's second reading reinforces the theme of unity — Christ welcoming all. The tone shifts between tenderness and urgency, dream and demand.

advent-02-b.logline: Advent 2/4 — "Comfort, give comfort to my people" — Isaiah's tender words meet John the Baptist's desert cry to prepare the way.
advent-02-b.trajectory: Advent B follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and longing, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = John testifies to the light (Gaudete), Week 4 = The Annunciation to Mary.

first.synopsis: This is Handel's Messiah territory. "Comfort, give comfort to my people." A voice cries out to prepare a highway in the desert for God — every valley filled, every mountain made low. Then the tone shifts to a shepherd: God gathers the lambs in his arms, carries them in his bosom, leads the ewes with care. The passage moves from cosmic road-building to intimate tenderness, and both images matter.

second.synopsis: Peter reminds his readers that God's sense of time isn't ours — "one day is like a thousand years." The Lord isn't delaying; he's being patient, "not wishing that any should perish." But the day of the Lord will come "like a thief," and the heavens will pass away. Since everything is to be dissolved, Peter asks: "What sort of persons ought you to be?" He urges holiness as we await "new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells."

gospel.synopsis: Mark opens his entire gospel here — "The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ the Son of God" — and immediately introduces John the Baptist as the fulfillment of Isaiah's voice in the desert. John appears in the wilderness, baptizing and preaching repentance. All of Jerusalem and Judea come out to him. He points beyond himself: "One mightier than I is coming after me... He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit."

advent-02-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to prepare the way — both the landscape and the heart. Isaiah's famous "Comfort, give comfort" opens with tenderness, then calls for radical leveling: valleys filled, mountains brought low, a highway built for God. Mark's gospel shows John the Baptist doing exactly that work, announcing one greater who is coming. The pairing is seamless — prophet and fulfillment. Peter's second reading runs on its own eschatological track but asks a question worth sitting with: given that everything is passing away, what kind of people should we be? The tone is both consoling and challenging.

advent-02-c.logline: Advent 2/4 — Baruch dresses Jerusalem in glory while Luke anchors John the Baptist in real history: Tiberius, Pilate, the desert, the voice.
advent-02-c.trajectory: Advent C follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and cosmic signs, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = John's practical instructions (Gaudete), Week 4 = The Visitation of Mary and Elizabeth.

first.synopsis: Baruch tells Jerusalem to take off her mourning clothes and put on the splendor of God's glory. Her scattered children are coming home, "borne aloft in glory as on royal thrones." Mountains will be made low, gorges filled to level ground — echoing Isaiah's highway imagery. God is "leading Israel in joy by the light of his glory, with his mercy and justice for company." It's a victory parade vision, full of color and movement.

second.synopsis: Paul prays for the Philippians with deep affection, expressing confidence that "the one who began a good work in you will continue to complete it until the day of Christ Jesus." He asks that their love may "increase ever more and more in knowledge and every kind of perception." This reading follows its own track — it's not about John the Baptist — but the idea of God completing what he started fits the Advent theme of fulfillment drawing near.

gospel.synopsis: Luke the historian carefully dates the moment: the fifteenth year of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate governing Judea. Into this politically specific world, "the word of God came to John the son of Zechariah in the desert." John preaches repentance and quotes Isaiah at length: every valley filled, every mountain made low, winding roads made straight. Luke's version ends with the universal promise: "all flesh shall see the salvation of God."

advent-02-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see God's salvation as something concrete, not abstract. Baruch dresses Jerusalem for a homecoming — mourning clothes off, glory on — while Luke plants John the Baptist in a specific moment in Roman imperial history. God's word doesn't arrive in a vacuum; it arrives during Tiberius's reign, in a real desert, to a real man. Both readings share Isaiah's highway imagery: mountains lowered, valleys filled. The second reading is on its own track, but Paul's confidence that God finishes what he starts reinforces the Advent hope. The tone is joyful and grounded.

advent-03-a.logline: Gaudete Sunday (Advent 3/4) — John the Baptist asks from prison: "Are you the one?" Jesus answers by pointing to what the blind, deaf, and lame are experiencing.
advent-03-a.trajectory: Advent A follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and readiness, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = Signs of the Messiah (Gaudete), Week 4 = Joseph's dream and the Annunciation fulfilled.

first.synopsis: Isaiah promises that the desert will bloom abundantly, that the weak will be strengthened, and the frightened will be told: "Be strong, fear not! Here is your God, he comes to save you." Then the signs: the blind see, the deaf hear, the lame leap like a stag, the mute sing. Those the Lord has ransomed will "enter Zion singing, crowned with everlasting joy." This is the checklist Jesus will later use to answer John's question.

second.synopsis: James urges patience: be like the farmer who waits for rain and harvest. "Make your hearts firm, because the coming of the Lord is at hand." Don't complain about each other — the Judge is at the gates. Look to the prophets as examples of patience in hardship. This reading is somewhat independent of the other two but captures the Gaudete Sunday posture: patient, firm-hearted, expectant.

gospel.synopsis: John the Baptist, now in prison, sends his disciples to ask Jesus: "Are you the one who is to come, or should we look for another?" Jesus doesn't answer directly. Instead he says: look at the evidence — the blind see, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor hear good news. Then he praises John as more than a prophet, the greatest born of women, while hinting that the kingdom redefines greatness entirely.

advent-03-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to look for evidence. It's Gaudete Sunday — rejoice — but the joy here isn't sentimental. John the Baptist is sitting in prison asking the hardest question a believer can ask: Is this really it? Are you really the one? Jesus answers not with theology but with results: the blind see, the lame walk, the poor hear good news. Isaiah's prophecy from the first reading is the exact checklist Jesus points to. The second reading counsels patience. The tone is honest, searching, and ultimately reassuring — joy rooted in what God is actually doing, not wishful thinking.

advent-03-b.logline: Gaudete Sunday (Advent 3/4) — "Rejoice always," Paul says, while John the Baptist insists he's not the light but points to one hidden among us.
advent-03-b.trajectory: Advent B follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and longing, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = John testifies to the light (Gaudete), Week 4 = The Annunciation to Mary.

first.synopsis: The spirit of the Lord is upon the prophet — anointed to bring glad tidings to the poor, heal the brokenhearted, proclaim liberty to captives. Then the tone shifts to pure joy: "I rejoice heartily in the Lord, in my God is the joy of my soul." God has clothed the speaker in a "robe of salvation" and a "mantle of justice," like a bridegroom with a crown, like a bride adorned with jewels. Justice and praise will spring up before all nations.

second.synopsis: Paul delivers one of his most quotable passages: "Rejoice always. Pray without ceasing. In all circumstances give thanks." He adds: "Do not quench the Spirit. Do not despise prophetic utterances. Test everything; retain what is good." Then he prays that God will make them "perfectly holy" — spirit, soul, and body — for the coming of Christ. This reading is a Gaudete Sunday perfect fit, full of joy, prayer, and trust in God's faithfulness.

gospel.synopsis: John the Evangelist introduces John the Baptist as a man "sent from God" who came to "testify to the light" — but emphatically was not the light. When priests and Levites interrogate him — Are you the Christ? Elijah? The Prophet? — he answers no, no, no. He's just "the voice of one crying out in the desert." His most striking line: "There is one among you whom you do not recognize," whose sandal strap he's not worthy to untie.

advent-03-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to rejoice because the one we're waiting for is already closer than we think. Isaiah's anointed herald brings glad tidings to the poor and wears joy like a wedding garment. John the Baptist, questioned by religious authorities, insists he's not the main character — "there is one among you whom you do not recognize." The Messiah is hidden in plain sight. Paul's second reading is the emotional soundtrack: "Rejoice always. Pray without ceasing." All three readings converge on Gaudete Sunday's central claim: joy isn't premature. The tone is luminous and expectant.

advent-03-c.logline: Gaudete Sunday (Advent 3/4) — "What should we do?" the crowds ask John, and he gives surprisingly practical answers: share your cloak, stop cheating, be fair.
advent-03-c.trajectory: Advent C follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and cosmic signs, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = John's practical instructions (Gaudete), Week 4 = The Visitation of Mary and Elizabeth.

first.synopsis: Zephaniah erupts with joy: "Shout for joy, O daughter Zion! Sing joyfully, O Israel!" The Lord has removed judgment, turned away enemies, and stands in Jerusalem's midst as a mighty savior. The most striking image: God himself "will rejoice over you with gladness, and renew you in his love, he will sing joyfully because of you." God singing over his people — that's a rare and beautiful image in scripture.

second.synopsis: Paul's Gaudete anthem: "Rejoice in the Lord always. I shall say it again: rejoice!" The Lord is near. Have no anxiety — instead, bring everything to God in prayer with thanksgiving. "Then the peace of God that surpasses all understanding will guard your hearts and minds in Christ Jesus." This is among the most frequently quoted Advent passages, and it connects directly to the day's theme of joy.

gospel.synopsis: The crowds come to John the Baptist and ask the simplest, most human question: "What should we do?" His answers are disarmingly practical — share your extra cloak, stop overtaxing people, don't extort anyone, be content with your wages. When people wonder if he might be the Christ, John redirects: one mightier is coming who will baptize with the Holy Spirit and fire, separating wheat from chaff. Luke notes that John was "preaching good news to the people."

advent-03-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let joy reshape how we live. Zephaniah offers one of scripture's most tender images — God singing joyfully over his people — and Paul commands us to rejoice, twice, insisting the Lord is near. But Luke's gospel grounds the celebration in ethics: when the crowds ask John "What should we do?" he doesn't give mystical answers. Share your coat. Stop cheating. Be fair. The second reading is perfectly matched to Gaudete Sunday. The overall tone is warm, joyful, and surprisingly earthy — real joy produces real generosity.

advent-04-a.logline: Advent 4/4 — Joseph discovers Mary is with child, wrestles with what to do, and receives an angel's message in a dream: "Emmanuel — God is with us."
advent-04-a.trajectory: Advent A follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and readiness, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = Signs of the Messiah (Gaudete), Week 4 = Joseph's dream and the Annunciation fulfilled.

first.synopsis: God offers King Ahaz any sign he wants — as deep as the netherworld or high as the sky — but Ahaz refuses with false piety. Isaiah, frustrated, announces the sign anyway: "The virgin shall conceive, and bear a son, and shall name him Emmanuel." The name means "God is with us," and it becomes the theological hinge of the entire Advent season, connecting Old Testament prophecy directly to the Christmas event.

second.synopsis: Paul introduces himself and the gospel: Jesus Christ, "descended from David according to the flesh, but established as Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness through resurrection from the dead." This is essentially Paul's thesis statement — the entire story of Jesus in one sentence. It connects to the first reading and gospel by grounding the incarnation in David's lineage and divine identity.

gospel.synopsis: Matthew tells the story from Joseph's perspective. Mary is found with child before they've lived together. Joseph, a righteous man, decides to divorce her quietly rather than expose her to shame. But an angel appears in a dream: "Do not be afraid to take Mary your wife into your home. For it is through the Holy Spirit that this child has been conceived in her." The child will be named Jesus — "he will save his people from their sins." Matthew connects it to Isaiah: Emmanuel, "God is with us." Joseph wakes up and obeys.

advent-04-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust what we can't fully understand. The season pivots from cosmic expectation to intimate incarnation. Isaiah's Emmanuel prophecy — spoken to a reluctant king — finds its fulfillment in Joseph's quiet bedroom, where an angel speaks through a dream. Joseph's faith is remarkable: he doesn't get a burning bush or a parted sea, just a dream and a command, and he obeys. Paul's second reading connects Jesus to David's line and to divine power. The tone is intimate, human, and hushed — we've moved from desert prophets and apocalyptic skies to a man lying in the dark, choosing to say yes.

advent-04-b.logline: Advent 4/4 — God reverses the script: David wants to build God a house, but God will build David a dynasty — and Gabriel tells Mary she will bear its fulfillment.
advent-04-b.trajectory: Advent B follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and longing, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = John testifies to the light (Gaudete), Week 4 = The Annunciation to Mary.

first.synopsis: David, settled in his cedar palace, feels guilty that the Ark of God sits in a tent. He wants to build God a house. But God flips the script through the prophet Nathan: I don't need a house — I will build you a house. God recounts David's journey from shepherd to king, then makes the great promise: "I will raise up your heir after you... I will be a father to him, and he shall be a son to me. Your house and your kingdom shall endure forever." This is the Davidic covenant — the foundation for messianic expectation.

second.synopsis: Paul closes Romans with a doxology praising God who can strengthen believers "according to the revelation of the mystery kept secret for long ages but now manifested." This mystery, now made known to all nations, brings about "the obedience of faith." It's brief but perfectly placed for Advent 4 — the secret is about to be revealed. The hidden is becoming visible.

gospel.synopsis: The Annunciation. Gabriel appears to Mary in Nazareth: "Hail, full of grace! The Lord is with you." She is troubled. The angel reassures her and delivers the news: she will conceive a son named Jesus who will be called Son of the Most High and receive "the throne of David his father." Mary asks how this is possible. Gabriel explains: the Holy Spirit will overshadow her, and "nothing will be impossible for God." Mary's response is total surrender: "Behold, I am the handmaid of the Lord. May it be done to me according to your word."

advent-04-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let God rewrite our plans. David wanted to build God a house; God told David he would build him a dynasty instead. That dynasty's fulfillment arrives in the most unexpected way — not through military might or royal decree, but through Gabriel's visit to a young woman in Nazareth. Mary's "yes" is the human hinge on which the incarnation turns. Paul's second reading speaks of a mystery "kept secret for long ages" now being revealed. All three readings converge on one idea: God's plan is bigger, stranger, and more intimate than anything we would have designed. The tone is awe-filled and tender.

advent-04-c.logline: Advent 4/4 — Mary travels in haste to Elizabeth, the baby leaps, and two women recognize what God is doing before anyone else catches on.
advent-04-c.trajectory: Advent C follows a 4-week arc: Week 1 = Watchfulness and cosmic signs, Week 2 = John the Baptist prepares the way, Week 3 = John's practical instructions (Gaudete), Week 4 = The Visitation of Mary and Elizabeth.

first.synopsis: Micah points to Bethlehem — "too small to be among the clans of Judah" — as the birthplace of a ruler whose origin is "from of old, from ancient times." God will wait until "she who is to give birth has borne," and then this ruler will shepherd his flock by the strength of the Lord. "He shall be peace." The prophecy emphasizes smallness and hiddenness: God's great plan unfolds in the least impressive place.

second.synopsis: The author of Hebrews puts words from Psalm 40 into Christ's mouth: "Sacrifice and offering you did not desire, but a body you prepared for me." Christ comes not to offer more animal sacrifices but to do God's will with his own body. "By this will, we have been consecrated through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all." This reading connects to the incarnation theme — God taking on a body — though it points ahead to the cross.

gospel.synopsis: Mary travels "in haste" to the hill country to visit her pregnant relative Elizabeth. When Elizabeth hears Mary's greeting, the infant John leaps in her womb. Elizabeth, filled with the Holy Spirit, cries out: "Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb." She marvels: "How does this happen to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?" She recognizes Mary's faith: "Blessed are you who believed that what was spoken to you by the Lord would be fulfilled."

advent-04-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to recognize God at work in what seems small and hidden. Micah points to tiny Bethlehem; Luke shows two pregnant women in a hill-country house, and between them the entire story of salvation is unfolding. Elizabeth's recognition — the baby leaping, her Spirit-filled cry — is one of the first acts of faith in the gospel. Mary believed what was spoken to her, and she moved "in haste" toward the people who would understand. The second reading deepens the incarnation theme: God prepared a body. The tone is intimate, joyful, and profoundly human — the cosmic has become personal.

nativity-vigil.logline: God's long genealogy of promise reaches its fulfillment in the birth of Emmanuel, God-with-us.
nativity-vigil.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: Isaiah refuses to keep silent until Jerusalem's vindication blazes like a torch for all nations to see. The city once called "Forsaken" will be renamed "My Delight," and God will rejoice over his people the way a bridegroom rejoices over his bride. The imagery is intimate and celebratory — a love poem between God and his people on the eve of their greatest gift.

second.synopsis: Paul stands in a synagogue in Pisidia and traces salvation history from the Exodus through David to Jesus. He quotes God's testimony about David — "a man after my own heart" — then draws a straight line from David's descendants to Jesus. John the Baptist appears as the final herald, insisting he is not the one but that someone greater follows. It is a compact summary of the whole biblical story arriving at its destination.

gospel.synopsis: Matthew opens with the genealogy of Jesus — forty-two generations from Abraham through David through the Babylonian exile to Joseph and Mary. It is a roll call of saints and sinners, including four women with unconventional stories (Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba). The narrative then shifts to Joseph's crisis: Mary is pregnant, he plans to divorce quietly, but an angel tells him in a dream, "Do not be afraid." The child is conceived by the Holy Spirit, and his name — Emmanuel — means "God is with us."

nativity-vigil.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to stand at the threshold of the greatest story ever told and trace the long, winding road God traveled to reach us. Isaiah's promise that the "Forsaken" would become "My Delight" finds its answer in Joseph's courageous yes to a plan he did not fully understand. We are invited to trust that God's promises — however long they take — arrive exactly when they should.

nativity-midnight.logline: Into the deepest darkness, a child is born — and the angels cannot contain the news.
nativity-midnight.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: "The people who walked in darkness have seen a great light." Isaiah announces the birth of a royal child whose titles are staggering: Wonder-Counselor, God-Hero, Father-Forever, Prince of Peace. The yoke of oppression is smashed, the boots and bloodied cloaks of war are burned. This is the great messianic prophecy — a child whose dominion is vast and forever peaceful, sustained by justice from David's throne.

second.synopsis: Paul tells Titus that "the grace of God has appeared, saving all." This grace trains us to reject godless ways and live with temperance, justice, and devotion while we wait for the "blessed hope" — the full appearance of Christ's glory. Jesus gave himself to deliver us from lawlessness and to form a people eager to do good. It is a compact theology of Christmas: grace appeared, grace transforms, grace promises more.

gospel.synopsis: Luke sets the scene with a census under Caesar Augustus that sends Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem. There, in the most ordinary of circumstances — no room at the inn — Mary gives birth, wraps the baby in swaddling clothes, and lays him in a manger. Shepherds keeping watch in the fields are terrified by an angel who proclaims "good news of great joy" — a savior born in the city of David. Then the sky fills with angels singing, "Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace to those on whom his favor rests."

nativity-midnight.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to see the contrast at the heart of Christmas: the almighty God entering the world as a vulnerable infant, laid in a feeding trough. Isaiah's promise of a great light piercing the darkness is fulfilled not in a palace but in a stable, announced not to kings but to shepherds. We are invited to find God in the small, overlooked, and humble places — and to join the angels in singing glory.

nativity-dawn.logline: The shepherds rush to Bethlehem and find exactly what the angel promised — and Mary treasures it all.
nativity-dawn.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: A short, urgent proclamation to the ends of the earth: "Your savior comes!" The people will be called "the holy people, the redeemed of the Lord," and Jerusalem will no longer be forsaken but "Frequented" — a city alive with God's presence. It is a brief burst of joy that matches the first light of Christmas morning.

second.synopsis: Paul declares that God saved us not because of anything we did right, but purely "because of his mercy." Salvation comes through "the bath of rebirth and renewal by the Holy Spirit," poured out richly through Jesus Christ. The goal: that we might be "justified by his grace and become heirs in hope of eternal life." It is a summary of the Christmas gift — mercy we did not earn, poured out lavishly.

gospel.synopsis: The angels return to heaven, and the shepherds say to one another, "Let us go, then, to Bethlehem." They hurry and find Mary, Joseph, and the infant in the manger — exactly as the angel described. They spread the news, and everyone who hears it is amazed. But the most striking detail: "Mary kept all these things, reflecting on them in her heart." The shepherds return home glorifying and praising God.

nativity-dawn.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to do what the shepherds did — go in haste to encounter what God has revealed and then tell others about it. Isaiah's proclamation that the savior comes is confirmed by the shepherds' own eyes. We are invited to be like Mary, who pondered these extraordinary events in her heart, letting them sink in before rushing to explain them.

nativity-day.logline: The Word that was with God from the beginning becomes flesh and dwells among us, full of grace and truth.
nativity-day.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: "How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings glad tidings." Isaiah pictures a messenger running toward Jerusalem with the news: "Your God is King!" The sentinels see it and shout for joy. The ruins of Jerusalem break into song because God has comforted his people. And the scope is universal — "all the ends of the earth will behold the salvation of our God." This is Christmas at its most cosmic.

second.synopsis: The Letter to the Hebrews opens with a sweeping declaration: God, who spoke in the past through prophets, has now spoken through his Son. This Son is "the refulgence of his glory, the very imprint of his being," the one through whom the universe was created. After accomplishing purification from sins, he sits at God's right hand, far superior to the angels. The Father himself declares: "You are my son; this day I have begotten you."

gospel.synopsis: John's Prologue is the theological summit of Christmas. "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." All things were made through him; his life is the light of the human race, and "the darkness has not overcome it." He came to his own, but his own did not accept him — yet to those who did, he gave power to become children of God. The climactic line: "The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us." From his fullness, we receive "grace in place of grace."

nativity-day.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to zoom out from the manger and grasp the cosmic scale of what happened at Christmas. Isaiah's vision of salvation reaching the ends of the earth meets John's declaration that the eternal Word — present at creation itself — has chosen to take on flesh and live among us. We are invited to marvel at the audacity of the Incarnation and to receive from Christ's fullness, grace upon grace.

holy-family-a.logline: Joseph protects the child and his mother through danger, exile, and an uncertain return — guided only by dreams.
holy-family-a.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: Sirach teaches that honoring your father atones for sins and revering your mother stores up riches. The passage gets tender and specific: care for your father when he is old, be patient even if his mind fails, and never revile him. "Kindness to a father will not be forgotten, firmly planted against the debt of your sins." It is a practical, grounded vision of family holiness rooted in daily acts of respect and patience.

second.synopsis: Paul lays out the wardrobe of Christian community life: "Put on heartfelt compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness, and patience." Over all of it, put on love — "the bond of perfection." Let the peace of Christ control your hearts. Teach and encourage one another with "psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs." The passage then addresses family roles directly: mutual love, obedience, and the warning to fathers not to provoke their children into discouragement.

gospel.synopsis: After the Magi leave, an angel warns Joseph in a dream: Herod wants to kill the child. Joseph rises immediately, takes Mary and Jesus by night, and flees to Egypt. After Herod dies, another dream sends them back — but hearing that Archelaus rules Judea, Joseph is still afraid. A third dream redirects them to Nazareth. The Holy Family is a refugee family, navigating danger and political instability, trusting fragile guidance one step at a time.

holy-family-a.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to see holiness in the messy, anxious, improvised reality of family life. Sirach's call to honor parents with patience meets Joseph's heroic willingness to uproot his family repeatedly on nothing more than a dream. We are invited to recognize that protecting those we love — and trusting God through uncertainty — is itself a form of worship.

holy-family-b.logline: Simeon and Anna recognize the infant Jesus as the long-awaited salvation, while a sword of sorrow looms.
holy-family-b.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: God tells the childless Abram, "Fear not! I am your shield." Abram pushes back — what good are gifts without an heir? God takes him outside and says, "Look up at the sky and count the stars. Just so shall your descendants be." Abram believes, and God credits it as righteousness. Years later, Sarah bears Isaac in their old age. It is the foundational story of faith rewarded — not quickly, but fully.

second.synopsis: The author of Hebrews holds up Abraham and Sarah as heroes of faith. By faith, Abraham left for an unknown land. By faith, Sarah conceived past the normal age because she trusted the one who promised. From one man "as good as dead" came descendants as numerous as the stars. And by faith, Abraham was willing to offer Isaac, reasoning that God could raise the dead. It is a meditation on trust that defies all evidence.

gospel.synopsis: Mary and Joseph bring the infant Jesus to the Temple for the rite of purification. There they meet Simeon, a devout man who had been promised he would not die before seeing the Messiah. He takes the baby in his arms and prays: "My eyes have seen your salvation — a light for revelation to the Gentiles and glory for your people Israel." But he also warns Mary: "A sword will pierce your own soul." The prophetess Anna, 84 years old, gives thanks and speaks about the child to all who await redemption. The family returns to Nazareth, where the child grows strong, "filled with wisdom."

holy-family-b.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to see that the life of faith means waiting — sometimes for decades — and trusting that God's promises will be fulfilled. Abraham and Sarah waited a lifetime for Isaac; Simeon waited his whole life to hold the Messiah. We are invited to hold both the joy of fulfillment and the warning of the sword, recognizing that the gift of this child will cost something of everyone who loves him.

holy-family-c.logline: The twelve-year-old Jesus stays behind in the Temple, astonishing the teachers and terrifying his parents.
holy-family-c.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: Hannah, who had prayed desperately for a child, gives birth to Samuel. Once he is weaned, she brings him to the temple at Shiloh with offerings and presents him to the priest Eli. Her words are striking: "I prayed for this child, and the Lord granted my request. Now I, in turn, give him to the Lord; as long as he lives, he shall be dedicated to the Lord." Hannah left Samuel there. It is a story of answered prayer and radical letting go.

second.synopsis: "See what love the Father has bestowed on us that we may be called the children of God. And so we are." John marvels at our identity as God's children, acknowledging that what we shall ultimately become has not yet been revealed. He then connects love to obedience: keep God's commandments, believe in the name of Jesus, and love one another. The Spirit confirms that God remains in us. It is about the dignity and responsibility of being part of God's family.

gospel.synopsis: At twelve, Jesus goes with his parents to Jerusalem for Passover. On the way home, Mary and Joseph realize he is not in the caravan. After three anxious days of searching, they find him in the Temple, sitting among the teachers, "listening to them and asking them questions." Everyone is astonished. Mary says, "Son, why have you done this to us?" Jesus responds with his first recorded words: "Did you not know that I must be in my Father's house?" They do not understand — but he goes home with them, obedient, and Mary "kept all these things in her heart."

holy-family-c.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to sit with the tension of a child who belongs to God even more than to his parents. Hannah gave Samuel to the Lord; Mary discovers that Jesus already knows where he truly belongs — in his Father's house. We are invited to recognize that the children in our care are not ultimately ours, and that the journey of faith sometimes means searching anxiously for a God who was never actually lost.

jan-1-mary-mother-of-god-abc.logline: On the eighth day, the child is named Jesus, and Mary quietly ponders the weight of it all.
jan-1-mary-mother-of-god-abc.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: God gives Moses the priestly blessing for Aaron and his sons to speak over Israel: "The Lord bless you and keep you! The Lord let his face shine upon you, and be gracious to you! The Lord look upon you kindly and give you peace!" It is one of the most beautiful and ancient blessings in Scripture — three lines that build from protection to grace to peace. God says, "So shall they invoke my name upon the Israelites, and I will bless them."

second.synopsis: Paul writes that "when the fullness of time had come, God sent his Son, born of a woman, born under the law" — to ransom those under the law so we might receive adoption as God's children. The proof of our adoption: God's Spirit in our hearts, crying "Abba, Father!" We are no longer slaves but sons and daughters — and if children, then heirs. It is a dense, powerful summary of what the Incarnation accomplishes.

gospel.synopsis: The shepherds hurry to Bethlehem and find Mary, Joseph, and the infant in the manger. They share what the angel told them, and everyone is amazed. Mary keeps all these things, reflecting on them in her heart. When eight days are complete, the child is circumcised and named Jesus — "the name given him by the angel before he was conceived in the womb." The naming makes it real and public: this child has entered the covenant.

jan-1-mary-mother-of-god-abc.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to begin the new year under the oldest blessing in Scripture and in the company of Mary, who models what it looks like to receive overwhelming grace and simply hold it. The priestly blessing of Numbers — "The Lord let his face shine upon you" — finds its ultimate expression in the child now named Jesus, the face of God turned toward us. We are invited to let the name of Jesus shape the year ahead.

the-epiphany-of-the-lord-abc.logline: Magi from the East follow a star, bearing gifts, and reveal that this king belongs to the whole world.
the-epiphany-of-the-lord-abc.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: "Rise up in splendor, Jerusalem! Your light has come." While darkness covers the earth and thick clouds cover the peoples, the Lord's glory shines on Jerusalem. Nations walk by its light; kings are drawn to its radiance. Sons and daughters stream in from afar. Caravans of camels arrive from Sheba "bearing gold and frankincense, and proclaiming the praises of the Lord." The imagery is lavish and universal — the whole world drawn to a single light.

second.synopsis: Paul reveals the "mystery" that had been hidden for generations but is now made known by the Spirit: "the Gentiles are coheirs, members of the same body, and copartners in the promise in Christ Jesus through the gospel." It is brief but radical — the walls between insiders and outsiders are down. The promise belongs to everyone. This is the theological meaning of the Magi's visit in a single sentence.

gospel.synopsis: Magi from the East arrive in Jerusalem asking, "Where is the newborn king of the Jews? We saw his star at its rising and have come to do him homage." King Herod is greatly troubled. He secretly summons the Magi, learns the timing, and sends them to Bethlehem with dark intentions. The star leads them to the house where they find the child with Mary. They prostrate themselves, open their treasures — gold, frankincense, and myrrh — and, warned in a dream, leave by another route. The seekers find what the powerful fear.

the-epiphany-of-the-lord-abc.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to see that the light of Christ is not private property — it draws seekers from every nation, culture, and background. Isaiah's vision of nations walking by Jerusalem's light is fulfilled by pagan astrologers who follow a star to a child. We are invited to bring our own gifts to Christ, to follow whatever light we have been given, and to recognize that God often reveals himself to the unexpected outsiders first.

baptism-of-the-lord-a.logline: Jesus steps into the Jordan, the heavens tear open, and the Father declares: "This is my beloved Son."
baptism-of-the-lord-a.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: God introduces his Servant: "Here is my servant whom I uphold, my chosen one with whom I am pleased." This servant will bring justice to the nations — but gently, without shouting in the streets. "A bruised reed he shall not break, and a smoldering wick he shall not quench." God has grasped him by the hand and set him as "a covenant of the people, a light for the nations," to open blind eyes and free prisoners from darkness.

second.synopsis: Peter, speaking in the house of Cornelius, makes a groundbreaking declaration: "God shows no partiality. In every nation whoever fears him and acts uprightly is acceptable to him." He summarizes the gospel — how God anointed Jesus of Nazareth "with the Holy Spirit and power," and how Jesus "went about doing good and healing all those oppressed by the devil, for God was with him." It is a concise mission statement for the one whose ministry is about to begin.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus comes from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John. John protests — "I need to be baptized by you" — but Jesus insists: "Allow it now, for thus it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness." After the baptism, the heavens open, the Spirit of God descends like a dove, and a voice from heaven declares, "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased." The Trinitarian revelation is complete: Father, Son, and Spirit all present at the water's edge.

baptism-of-the-lord-a.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to see Jesus step fully into his mission — the gentle servant of Isaiah who will bring justice without violence, now publicly anointed by the Spirit and claimed by the Father. We are invited to remember our own baptism, when we too were claimed as beloved, and to ask what it means to carry that identity into the work of justice, healing, and mercy.

baptism-of-the-lord-b.logline: The heavens are torn open over the Jordan, and the voice of God names Jesus as beloved Son.
baptism-of-the-lord-b.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: God issues an extravagant invitation: "All you who are thirsty, come to the water! You who have no money, come, receive grain and eat." Why spend your wages on what fails to satisfy? The passage builds to the great declaration: "My thoughts are not your thoughts, nor are your ways my ways." Just as rain and snow water the earth and make it fruitful, so God's word will not return void — it will accomplish what it was sent to do. It is a promise of abundance and a call to seek the Lord while he may be found.

second.synopsis: John writes that everyone who believes Jesus is the Christ is begotten by God, and faith is "the victory that conquers the world." Three things testify to Jesus: the Spirit, the water, and the blood — and these three are of one accord. If we accept human testimony, God's testimony is surely greater. The passage links baptism and belief, pointing to the water of Jesus's baptism and the blood of his cross as one continuous witness.

gospel.synopsis: John the Baptist proclaims that "one mightier than I is coming" — one who will baptize not with water but with the Holy Spirit. Jesus arrives from Nazareth and is baptized in the Jordan. Mark's account is characteristically dramatic: coming up from the water, Jesus sees "the heavens being torn open" and the Spirit descending like a dove. A voice from heaven says, "You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased." Mark's word for the heavens is violent — they are ripped apart. Heaven can no longer be kept separate from earth.

baptism-of-the-lord-b.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to hear God's word go out and not return empty — just as Isaiah promised. The same God who invites the thirsty to come to the water now tears open the heavens over the Jordan to claim his Son. We are invited to come to the water ourselves, to let God's word accomplish its purpose in us, and to trust that the heavens remain open over every baptismal font.

baptism-of-the-lord-c.logline: While Jesus prays after his baptism, heaven opens and the Spirit descends — his public mission begins.
baptism-of-the-lord-c.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: "Comfort, give comfort to my people, says your God." Isaiah announces that Jerusalem's guilt is expiated and her service is at an end. A voice cries out to prepare the way of the Lord in the desert — every valley filled, every mountain made low. Then the image shifts to tenderness: "Like a shepherd he feeds his flock; in his arms he gathers the lambs, carrying them in his bosom." The God who levels mountains also cradles lambs.

second.synopsis: Paul combines two passages from Titus: "The grace of God has appeared, saving all" and training us to live justly while we await Christ's return. Then: "When the kindness and generous love of God our savior appeared" — not because of anything we earned — he saved us through "the bath of rebirth and renewal by the Holy Spirit." The two appearances of grace bookend the Christian life: the grace that appeared at Christmas and the grace that washes us in baptism.

gospel.synopsis: The people wonder whether John might be the Christ, but he sets them straight: "One mightier than I is coming. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire." Luke then gives a unique detail — after his baptism, Jesus is praying when heaven opens, the Spirit descends in bodily form like a dove, and a voice says, "You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased." Only Luke notes the prayer. The heavens open not just at the water but at the moment of communion between Father and Son.

baptism-of-the-lord-c.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to stand at the hinge between Christmas and Ordinary Time, between the hidden life of Nazareth and the public ministry that will change everything. Isaiah's tender shepherd who gathers lambs is the same one now stepping into the Jordan to be anointed with Spirit and fire. We are invited to see our own baptism as the moment God spoke our name and said "beloved" — and to let that identity fuel everything that follows.

2nd-sun-after-christmas-abc-epiphany-rep.logline: Wisdom pitches her tent among God's people, and the eternal Word becomes flesh to dwell among us.
2nd-sun-after-christmas-abc-epiphany-rep.trajectory: The Christmas season moves from the intimacy of the Nativity through the revelation to the nations (Epiphany) to the inauguration of Jesus's public life (Baptism).

first.synopsis: Wisdom speaks in the first person, boasting before the assembly of the Most High. The Creator commanded her to pitch her tent in Jacob and put down roots in Israel. "Before all ages, in the beginning, he created me, and through all ages I shall not cease to be." She ministers in the holy tent and fixes her abode in Zion. This is a portrait of divine Wisdom choosing to dwell with a particular people — a foreshadowing of the Incarnation itself.

second.synopsis: Paul blesses God for blessing us "with every spiritual blessing in the heavens." Before the foundation of the world, God chose us to be holy and destined us for adoption through Jesus Christ. Paul then prays that God would give the community "a Spirit of wisdom and revelation" so that "the eyes of your hearts" may be enlightened. Key imagery: being chosen before creation, adopted as children, and given inner sight to see what that means.

gospel.synopsis: John's Prologue returns on this Sunday as the theological capstone of the Christmas season. "In the beginning was the Word" — present with God, identical with God, the agent of all creation. Life and light entered the world, but the world did not recognize him. To those who accepted him, he gave the power to become children of God. The turning point: "The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us." John the Baptist testifies, and from Christ's fullness we receive "grace in place of grace." No one has seen God, but the Son has revealed him.

2nd-sun-after-christmas-abc-epiphany-rep.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to linger in the mystery of the Incarnation before the season passes. Sirach's Wisdom choosing to dwell among God's people is the Old Testament preview of John's staggering claim: the Word became flesh. We are invited to let Paul's prayer become our own — that the eyes of our hearts would be enlightened to grasp the hope, the riches, and the glory of being chosen and adopted before the world began.

lent-01-a.logline: Lent begins in the desert: Adam and Eve fell to temptation, but Jesus stands firm against the devil's every test.
lent-01-a.trajectory: Lent A follows the great catechetical gospels used for RCIA scrutinies: Temptation → Transfiguration → Woman at the Well → Man Born Blind → Raising of Lazarus. Each week deepens the journey from sin to new life.

first.synopsis: God forms the first human from clay and breathes life into him, planting a garden with every good tree. But the serpent twists God's command, convincing the woman that the forbidden fruit will make them "like gods." They eat, their eyes are opened, and they realize they are naked. The story sets up the entire Lenten arc: humanity's original rupture with God, the shame that follows disobedience, and the need for redemption.

second.synopsis: Paul draws a sweeping contrast between Adam and Christ. Through one man, sin and death entered the world; through one man, grace overflowed for all. Where the transgression brought condemnation, the gift brought acquittal. Where disobedience made the many sinners, obedience will make the many righteous. It is a theological backbone for the season: Lent traces the path from the first Adam's failure to the new Adam's victory.

gospel.synopsis: The Spirit leads Jesus into the desert for forty days of fasting and temptation. The devil tries three angles, each an appeal to self-centeredness: turn stones to bread (provide for yourself), throw yourself from the temple (display yourself), worship me for all the kingdoms (exalt yourself). Each time Jesus answers with Scripture, refusing to grasp what is not his to take. The irony: Jesus will eventually do all three things — feed the hungry, be saved by angels, receive all kingdoms — but for others, not himself. Where Adam and Eve reached for the fruit, Jesus refuses. "Get away, Satan!" The angels come and minister to him.

lent-01-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to step into the desert with Jesus at the very start of Lent. The first reading shows us where we come from — a humanity that reached for the wrong thing and lost its footing. The gospel shows us where we are headed — following someone who faced every temptation and did not break. We begin Lent not with a program of self-improvement but with the honest admission that we are the descendants of Adam and Eve, and the hopeful recognition that Christ has already won the battle we keep losing.

lent-01-b.logline: God sets a rainbow in the clouds as a covenant sign, and the Spirit drives Jesus into the desert — he emerges proclaiming: "Repent, and believe in the gospel."
lent-01-b.trajectory: Lent B traces God's covenant history: Temptation → Transfiguration → Temple Cleansing → God's love for the world (Jn 3) → The hour has come (Jn 12).

first.synopsis: After the flood, God establishes a covenant with Noah and every living creature: never again will floodwaters destroy the earth. The sign is striking: the Hebrew word is not 'rainbow' but 'bow' — as in a weapon. God is hanging his war bow in the sky, transforming an instrument of destruction into a sign of peace. This is God's first formal covenant in Scripture, unconditional and universal — a unilateral disarmament that sets the tone for Year B's Lenten arc tracing God's covenant faithfulness through history.

second.synopsis: Peter connects Noah's flood to baptism. Christ suffered for sins once, the righteous for the unrighteous, to lead us to God. The water that saved Noah's eight passengers prefigures the baptismal water that saves us now — not by washing dirt from the body, but as an appeal to God for a clear conscience through the resurrection of Jesus Christ. A powerful baptismal text for the Lenten journey.

gospel.synopsis: Mark's temptation account is the shortest and starkest of the three synoptics: the Spirit "drove" Jesus into the desert. He was there forty days, tempted by Satan, among wild beasts, and the angels ministered to him. Then, after John's arrest, Jesus comes to Galilee with his core proclamation: "This is the time of fulfillment. The kingdom of God is at hand. Repent, and believe in the gospel." No details of the temptations — just the raw wilderness and the urgent call.

lent-01-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hear God's covenant promise anew as Lent begins. The rainbow after the flood reminds us that God's faithfulness is older than our failures. Mark's spare, urgent account of the temptation strips away everything but the essentials: desert, Satan, wild beasts, angels, and then the call to repent and believe. We enter Lent under the sign of a God who has already committed to us, and who asks us to commit in return.

lent-01-c.logline: Israel confesses its salvation history as an offering of firstfruits, and Jesus defeats the devil by clinging to the word of God alone.
lent-01-c.trajectory: Lent C focuses on repentance and mercy: Temptation → Transfiguration → Repent or perish → Prodigal Son → Woman caught in adultery.

first.synopsis: Moses instructs the people on how to offer their firstfruits: you set the basket before the altar and recite your story. "My father was a wandering Aramean" — then Egypt, slavery, crying out, deliverance, the land flowing with milk and honey. It is a confession of faith through narrative: who we are is the story of what God has done. The offering of firstfruits becomes an act of memory and gratitude.

second.synopsis: Paul says the word of faith is near you, in your mouth and in your heart. Confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord, believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, and you will be saved. There is no distinction between Jew and Greek — the same Lord is Lord of all. "Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved." A text about the simplicity and universality of saving faith.

gospel.synopsis: Luke's account of the temptation follows Jesus, filled with the Holy Spirit, into the desert for forty days. The devil offers bread, power over all kingdoms, and a death-defying leap from the temple parapet. Jesus answers each temptation with Scripture. Luke uniquely ends the scene in Jerusalem (the temple), foreshadowing the city where everything will be resolved. The devil departs "for a time" — he will be back. Key image: the devil quotes Scripture too, but Jesus wields it as a sword.

lent-01-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to begin Lent by confessing who we are — a people with a story of deliverance. The Deuteronomy reading teaches us to offer our lives as firstfruits by remembering what God has done. The gospel shows Jesus doing exactly that: when every comfort and shortcut is offered, he answers from the deep well of God's word. Year C's Lenten journey will focus on repentance and mercy, and it starts here with the fundamental choice: will we trust God's word or reach for something shinier?

lent-02-a.logline: God calls Abraham to leave everything behind, and Jesus is transfigured on the mountain — a flash of glory to sustain the long walk toward the cross.
lent-02-a.trajectory: Lent A follows the great catechetical gospels used for RCIA scrutinies: Temptation → Transfiguration → Woman at the Well → Man Born Blind → Raising of Lazarus. Each week deepens the journey from sin to new life.

first.synopsis: God tells Abram to leave his land, his kinsfolk, and his father's house for an unknown destination. In return: "I will make of you a great nation, and I will bless you." All the communities of the earth will find blessing in him. And Abram went. No argument, no negotiation — just obedience to a staggering promise. This is the call that launches salvation history.

second.synopsis: Paul urges Timothy to bear his share of hardship for the gospel with strength from God. God saved us and called us to a holy life — not because of anything we did, but according to his own design and grace, given in Christ before time began. Christ has destroyed death and brought life and immortality to light. A compact summary of the gospel message for those walking the Lenten road.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a high mountain. He is transfigured: his face shines like the sun, his clothes become white as light. Moses and Elijah appear, conversing with him. Peter offers to build three tents. A bright cloud overshadows them and the Father's voice declares: "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased; listen to him." The disciples fall in terror; Jesus touches them and says, "Rise, and do not be afraid." A preview of Easter glory, given mid-Lent to strengthen the journey.

lent-02-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust a promise we cannot yet see fulfilled. Abraham left everything on God's word alone. The disciples saw Jesus blazing with glory on the mountain — but then had to come back down and keep walking toward Jerusalem. The Transfiguration is not the destination; it is fuel for the road. In the middle of Lent's austerity, we are given a glimpse of what is coming, so we can keep going.

lent-02-b.logline: Abraham raises the knife over Isaac and God provides a ram instead; on the mountain, Jesus is revealed as the beloved Son destined for sacrifice.
lent-02-b.trajectory: Lent B traces God's covenant history: Temptation → Transfiguration → Temple Cleansing → God's love for the world (Jn 3) → The hour has come (Jn 12).

first.synopsis: God tests Abraham: take your son Isaac, your only one, whom you love, and offer him as a sacrifice. Each time God calls, Abraham answers 'Hineni' — 'Here I am' — the same word Moses, Samuel, and Isaiah will use when God calls them. Abraham obeys, builds the altar, raises the knife. At the last moment, the angel stops him: "Do not lay your hand on the boy." A ram caught in the thicket becomes the substitute. Because Abraham did not withhold his beloved son, God swears to bless him abundantly. The typology is unmistakable: a father's beloved son, a mountain, a sacrifice — and a substitute who dies in his place.

second.synopsis: Paul asks the rhetorical question that echoes through all of Lent: if God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own Son but handed him over for us all — how will he not also give us everything else? Who will condemn? Christ Jesus died, was raised, sits at God's right hand, and intercedes for us. The Abraham story finds its fulfillment: God actually does what he asked Abraham to do.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a high mountain. His clothes become dazzling white, "such as no fuller on earth could bleach them." Elijah and Moses appear. Peter, terrified, babbles about building tents. A cloud overshadows them: "This is my beloved Son. Listen to him." Suddenly they see no one but Jesus alone. Coming down the mountain, he orders them to keep silent until the Son of Man has risen from the dead. They kept the matter to themselves, "questioning what rising from the dead meant."

lent-02-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see the cross foreshadowed in the most startling Old Testament story of all. Abraham's willingness to sacrifice his beloved son prefigures what God will actually do on Calvary. The Transfiguration, paired with this reading, reveals the beloved Son who will not be spared. The disciples do not yet understand what "rising from the dead" means — and neither, in some ways, do we. But the Father's voice is clear: listen to him. That is our Lenten work.

lent-02-c.logline: God cuts a covenant with Abraham in fire and darkness, and on the mountain Jesus is transfigured while speaking of his coming "exodus" in Jerusalem.
lent-02-c.trajectory: Lent C focuses on repentance and mercy: Temptation → Transfiguration → Repent or perish → Prodigal Son → Woman caught in adultery.

first.synopsis: God takes Abram outside: count the stars, if you can. So shall your descendants be. Abram believes, and God credits it as righteousness. Then the ancient covenant ritual: split animals, a deep terrifying darkness, and a smoking fire pot with a flaming torch passing between the pieces. God alone walks between the halves, binding himself to the promise. A haunting, primal scene — God staking his own life on the covenant.

second.synopsis: Paul warns against those who are enemies of the cross, whose minds are set on earthly things. But our citizenship is in heaven, and we await a savior who will transform our lowly bodies to conform with his glorified body. Therefore, stand firm. The contrast between earthly-mindedness and heavenly citizenship frames the Transfiguration: we have seen where we are headed, so we live differently now.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus takes Peter, John, and James up the mountain to pray. While praying, his face changes and his clothing becomes dazzling white. Moses and Elijah appear in glory, speaking of his "exodus" — Luke's unique word for what Jesus will accomplish in Jerusalem. The disciples, overcome by sleep, wake to see his glory. A cloud overshadows them; the Father's voice says, "This is my chosen Son; listen to him." Only Luke links the Transfiguration explicitly to Jesus' coming death as an "exodus" — a liberation.

lent-02-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust a God who binds himself to us in the dark. The covenant with Abraham is a terrifying, beautiful scene — God walking through the split animals, pledging his own faithfulness. On the mountain, Jesus is revealed in glory, but the conversation is about his exodus, his death. Luke wants us to know that the glory and the cross are not opposites; they are the same story. Our Lenten journey is an exodus too — from slavery to freedom, from death to life.

lent-03-a-first-scrutiny.logline: Israel thirsts in the desert and God strikes water from the rock; at Jacob's well, Jesus offers the Samaritan woman "living water" that wells up to eternal life.
lent-03-a-first-scrutiny.trajectory: Lent A follows the great catechetical gospels used for RCIA scrutinies: Temptation → Transfiguration → Woman at the Well → Man Born Blind → Raising of Lazarus. Each week deepens the journey from sin to new life.

first.synopsis: The Israelites are dying of thirst in the desert and grumble against Moses: "Why did you bring us out of Egypt just to kill us?" Moses cries out to God. God tells him to strike the rock at Horeb, and water flows out for the people. The place gets named Massah and Meribah — testing and quarreling — because the people asked, "Is the Lord in our midst or not?" The question still echoes.

second.synopsis: Since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God. Hope does not disappoint, because the love of God has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit. While we were still helpless, Christ died for the ungodly. God proves his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. The image of love "poured out" connects to the water imagery of the other readings.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus, tired from his journey, sits at Jacob's well at noon. A Samaritan woman comes to draw water, and a remarkable conversation unfolds. He asks for a drink; she is shocked a Jew would speak to her. He offers "living water" that wells up to eternal life. He reveals her past — five husbands — and she recognizes him as a prophet. The conversation deepens: true worship is in Spirit and truth. She asks about the Messiah; he says, "I am he." She runs to tell the town, leaving her water jar behind. This is the first RCIA scrutiny gospel: an encounter with Christ that exposes our thirst, our past, and our deepest need.

lent-03-a-first-scrutiny.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to bring our thirst to Jesus. In the desert, the Israelites doubted God was even with them. At the well, a woman with a complicated past met someone who knew everything about her and offered her living water anyway. The first scrutiny asks the catechumens — and all of us — to stop drawing from wells that leave us thirsty and to drink from the source that never runs dry. Key images for music: living water, the well, thirst, the encounter at noon.

lent-03-b.logline: God gives the Ten Commandments as the terms of covenant love, and Jesus cleanses the temple, declaring his body the true temple that will be raised in three days.
lent-03-b.trajectory: Lent B traces God's covenant history: Temptation → Transfiguration → Temple Cleansing → God's love for the world (Jn 3) → The hour has come (Jn 12).

first.synopsis: God delivers the Ten Commandments at Sinai. They begin with identity: "I, the Lord, am your God, who brought you out of Egypt." Then the terms of the relationship: no other gods, no idols, honor the sabbath, honor your parents, do not kill, do not commit adultery, do not steal, do not bear false witness, do not covet. These are not arbitrary rules but the contours of a covenant relationship — how a freed people lives with their liberator.

second.synopsis: Jews demand signs and Greeks look for wisdom, but we proclaim Christ crucified — a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles. But to those who are called, Christ is the power of God and the wisdom of God. The foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom; the weakness of God is stronger than human strength. A short, punchy text that reframes everything the world thinks it knows about power.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus enters the temple at Passover and finds it turned into a marketplace. He makes a whip, drives out the merchants, overturns the money changers' tables: "Stop making my Father's house a marketplace!" When challenged for a sign, he says, "Destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up." Note: Jesus deliberately says 'raise up,' not 'rebuild' — this is resurrection language, not construction language. They scoff — the temple took forty-six years to build. But he was speaking of his body. After the resurrection, the disciples remembered. The true temple is not a building; it is Christ himself.

lent-03-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to ask what we have turned into a marketplace. The commandments define the shape of covenant love; the temple cleansing shows what happens when worship gets co-opted by commerce and power. Jesus is not just reforming the temple — he is replacing it. His body, destroyed and raised, becomes the new meeting place between God and humanity. For music ministers: images of zeal, cleansing, holy ground, and the body as temple.

lent-03-c.logline: God reveals his name from the burning bush — "I AM" — and Jesus warns that without repentance, the fruitless fig tree will be cut down.
lent-03-c.trajectory: Lent C focuses on repentance and mercy: Temptation → Transfiguration → Repent or perish → Prodigal Son → Woman caught in adultery.

first.synopsis: Moses sees a bush burning but not consumed and turns aside to look. God calls from the bush: remove your sandals, this is holy ground. God has seen the affliction of his people in Egypt and has come to rescue them. Moses asks for God's name. The answer: "I am who am." Tell them "I AM sent me to you." The burning bush is one of Scripture's most iconic images — God's presence that blazes without destroying, and a name that refuses to be contained.

second.synopsis: Paul warns the Corinthians using Israel's desert history as a cautionary tale. Our ancestors all passed through the sea, all ate spiritual food, all drank from the spiritual rock — and the rock was Christ. Yet God was not pleased with most of them. These things happened as examples for us. Whoever thinks he is standing secure should take care not to fall. A sobering reminder that spiritual privilege is not the same as spiritual faithfulness.

gospel.synopsis: People tell Jesus about Galileans whom Pilate killed at worship. Jesus refuses the logic that suffering equals guilt: those people were not worse sinners than anyone else. "But I tell you, if you do not repent, you will all perish as they did!" Then the parable of the barren fig tree: the owner wants to cut it down after three fruitless years, but the gardener pleads for one more year to cultivate and fertilize it. Urgent mercy — but not infinite patience. The time to bear fruit is now.

lent-03-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to stop making excuses and start bearing fruit. The burning bush reveals a God who sees suffering and acts. The gospel says: do not waste time calculating who deserves what — repent. The fig tree parable is both warning and comfort: the gardener advocates for more time, but the tree must eventually produce. Lent is that "one more year" of patient cultivation. What fruit is God waiting for?

lent-04-a-second-scrutiny.logline: God anoints the overlooked shepherd boy David as king, and Jesus opens the eyes of a man born blind — "I was blind and now I see."
lent-04-a-second-scrutiny.trajectory: Lent A follows the great catechetical gospels used for RCIA scrutinies: Temptation → Transfiguration → Woman at the Well → Man Born Blind → Raising of Lazarus. Each week deepens the journey from sin to new life.

first.synopsis: God sends Samuel to Jesse's house to anoint a new king. Samuel looks at the tall, impressive eldest son and thinks, "Surely this is the one." God corrects him: "Not as man sees does God see, because man sees the appearance but the Lord looks into the heart." Seven sons pass; none is chosen. Then the youngest, David, is brought in from tending sheep — ruddy, handsome, overlooked. "There — anoint him, for this is the one!" The spirit of the Lord rushes upon David.

second.synopsis: You were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord. Live as children of light. Light produces every kind of goodness and righteousness and truth. Take no part in the fruitless works of darkness; instead, expose them. "Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will give you light." A baptismal hymn fragment that connects directly to the gospel's theme of moving from darkness into sight.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus sees a man blind from birth. The disciples ask who sinned. Jesus rejects the premise: this happened so God's works might be visible. He makes clay, anoints the man's eyes, and sends him to wash in the Pool of Siloam. The man comes back seeing. What follows is a dramatic courtroom scene: neighbors, Pharisees, and parents all interrogate him. His testimony grows bolder with each round: "One thing I do know is that I was blind and now I see." The authorities throw him out. But then Jesus actively goes to find him — like the Good Shepherd seeking the lost sheep — and asks, "Do you believe in the Son of Man?" He says, "I do believe, Lord," and worships. The healing is not just physical sight but full social and spiritual reintegration. The second scrutiny gospel: who is really blind?

lent-04-a-second-scrutiny.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let God see us differently than the world does. Samuel looked for a king among the tall and impressive; God chose the forgotten shepherd boy. The man born blind could not see, but the Pharisees who had perfect eyesight were the truly blind ones. The second scrutiny asks: what are we refusing to see? Where have we mistaken appearance for substance? Key images for music: light, sight, anointing, "I was blind and now I see," arising from darkness.

lent-04-b.logline: By the rivers of Babylon the exiles wept, but God's mercy endured; Jesus tells Nicodemus that God so loved the world he gave his only Son.
lent-04-b.trajectory: Lent B traces God's covenant history: Temptation → Transfiguration → Temple Cleansing → God's love for the world (Jn 3) → The hour has come (Jn 12).
