first.synopsis: A sweeping summary of Israel's downfall: priests and people practiced every abomination, polluted the temple, mocked God's messengers, and scoffed at the prophets until there was no remedy. Enemies burned the temple and carried the people to Babylon. But after seventy years, God stirred the heart of Cyrus of Persia to set them free and rebuild the temple. Destruction and exile — then, impossibly, restoration. The covenant survives even catastrophe.

second.synopsis: God, rich in mercy, loved us even when we were dead in our transgressions and brought us to life with Christ. By grace you have been saved through faith — this is not from you, it is the gift of God, not from works, so no one may boast. We are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus for good works prepared in advance. Grace is the engine; works are the fruit, not the cause.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells Nicodemus — who came to him at night, and who will reappear on Good Friday as one of only two people who dare claim Jesus' body — that just as Moses lifted up the serpent in the desert, the Son of Man must be lifted up so that everyone who believes may have eternal life. Then the most quoted verse in Scripture: "God so loved the world that he gave his only Son, so that everyone who believes in him might not perish but might have eternal life." God sent his Son not to condemn but to save. The light has come into the world, but people preferred darkness. Whoever lives the truth comes to the light. Nicodemus's own journey from darkness to light spans the entire gospel of John.

lent-04-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to receive the astonishing news that God's response to a broken world is not condemnation but love. The exile in Babylon looked like the end of the story, but God raised up a pagan king to set his people free. John 3:16 is not a bumper sticker — it is the theological center of the gospel: God gave his Son so that we might live. In the middle of Lent, we are reminded that the whole journey points toward mercy, not punishment. The light is here; will we step into it?

lent-04-c.logline: The manna stops and Israel eats the fruit of the promised land; the prodigal son comes home to a father who was watching the road all along.
lent-04-c.trajectory: Lent C focuses on repentance and mercy: Temptation → Transfiguration → Repent or perish → Prodigal Son → Woman caught in adultery.

first.synopsis: A transitional moment: the Israelites arrive in the promised land and celebrate Passover at Gilgal. The day after Passover, they eat the produce of the land for the first time — unleavened cakes and parched grain. On that same day, the manna ceases. The desert provision stops because the promise has arrived. It is a quiet, powerful image of arrival: God's faithfulness shifts form but never stops.

second.synopsis: Whoever is in Christ is a new creation: the old things have passed away. God has reconciled us to himself through Christ and given us the ministry of reconciliation. We are ambassadors for Christ. "Be reconciled to God." For our sake God made him who did not know sin to be sin, so that we might become the righteousness of God. This is the heart of the Lenten invitation: reconciliation is available, and we are called to carry that message.

gospel.synopsis: The Pharisees grumble because Jesus eats with sinners. So he tells the parable of the prodigal son. The younger son demands his inheritance, squanders it in a far country, and ends up feeding pigs. He comes to his senses and heads home with a rehearsed apology. But the father sees him from a long way off, runs to him, embraces him, and throws a feast: "This son of mine was dead, and has come to life again." The older son is furious at the unfairness. The father pleads: "Everything I have is yours. But we must celebrate." The story is unfinished — we never learn if the older son goes in.

lent-04-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to come home. The prodigal son parable is the greatest story ever told about repentance and mercy — and it is placed right in the middle of Lent for a reason. The father does not wait inside; he is watching the road. The reconciliation Paul describes in the second reading is not a transaction but an embrace. The challenge is for both sons: the one who wandered and the one who stayed but never understood the father's heart. Which one are we?

lent-05-a-third-scrutiny.logline: Ezekiel sees dry bones raised from their graves, and Jesus stands before the tomb of his friend and commands: "Lazarus, come out!"
lent-05-a-third-scrutiny.trajectory: Lent A follows the great catechetical gospels used for RCIA scrutinies: Temptation → Transfiguration → Woman at the Well → Man Born Blind → Raising of Lazarus. Each week deepens the journey from sin to new life.

first.synopsis: God speaks through Ezekiel: "O my people, I will open your graves and have you rise from them." He will put his spirit in them so they may live, and settle them on their land. "I have promised, and I will do it." Originally about Israel's return from exile, this text takes on its fullest meaning in light of the gospel: God is in the business of raising the dead. The image of opened graves echoes forward to Easter morning.

second.synopsis: Those in the flesh cannot please God, but you are in the spirit if the Spirit of God dwells in you. If Christ is in you, the body is dead because of sin but the spirit is alive because of righteousness. If the Spirit of the one who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, that same Spirit will give life to your mortal bodies. The logic is clear: the same power that raised Jesus will raise us.

gospel.synopsis: Lazarus is sick. Jesus delays two days, then tells the disciples, "Lazarus has died. And I am glad for you that I was not there, that you may believe." Martha meets him: "Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died." Jesus declares, "I am the resurrection and the life." She confesses faith. At the tomb, Jesus weeps. He orders the stone removed. Martha protests: "Lord, by now there will be a stench." Jesus prays, then cries out in a loud voice: "Lazarus, come out!" The dead man emerges, still bound in burial cloths. "Untie him and let him go." The third scrutiny gospel is the climax of the Lenten arc: Jesus has power over death itself.

lent-05-a-third-scrutiny.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to believe that what is dead can live again. The scrutiny readings have been building all Lent: thirst quenched (Week 3), blindness healed (Week 4), and now death itself defeated (Week 5). Lazarus is not a metaphor; he is a sign of what is coming at Easter. For those preparing for baptism and for all of us, the question is Martha's: "Do you believe this?" Key images: the opened grave, "Lazarus, come out!", unbinding, the stench of death giving way to the breath of life.

lent-05-b.logline: God promises a new covenant written on the heart, and Jesus declares that unless a grain of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains just a grain.
lent-05-b.trajectory: Lent B traces God's covenant history: Temptation → Transfiguration → Temple Cleansing → God's love for the world (Jn 3) → The hour has come (Jn 12).

first.synopsis: God announces through Jeremiah: the days are coming when I will make a new covenant, unlike the one your ancestors broke. "I will place my law within them and write it upon their hearts. I will be their God, and they shall be my people." No longer will anyone need to teach their neighbor how to know the Lord — all will know him, from least to greatest. "I will forgive their evildoing and remember their sin no more." The most important covenant prophecy in the Old Testament.

second.synopsis: In the days when Christ was in the flesh, he offered prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears to the one who could save him from death. He was heard because of his reverence. Son though he was, he learned obedience from what he suffered. Made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation for all who obey him. A raw, honest portrait of Jesus' humanity and suffering — he cried real tears and learned obedience the hard way.

gospel.synopsis: Some Greeks ask to see Jesus. This triggers his declaration: "The hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified." Then the grain of wheat metaphor: unless it falls to the ground and dies, it remains just a grain; but if it dies, it produces much fruit. Jesus is troubled: "What should I say? 'Father, save me from this hour'? But it was for this purpose that I came to this hour. Father, glorify your name." A voice from heaven answers. Jesus announces: "When I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw everyone to myself." The cross is now in plain sight.

lent-05-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to embrace the hard truth at the heart of the gospel: new life requires death. Jeremiah's new covenant promise is beautiful, but it cost something — it cost God's Son, who offered prayers with loud cries and tears. The grain of wheat must fall and die. We are one week from Palm Sunday, and the readings stop softening the blow. The hour has come. For music ministers: images of the grain of wheat, being lifted up, new covenant, the heart as the place where God's law is written.

lent-05-c.logline: God declares "I am doing something new!" through Isaiah, and Jesus tells the accusers of the woman caught in adultery: "Let the one without sin cast the first stone."
lent-05-c.trajectory: Lent C focuses on repentance and mercy: Temptation → Transfiguration → Repent or perish → Prodigal Son → Woman caught in adultery.

first.synopsis: God, who once opened a way through the sea and destroyed Pharaoh's army, now says something startling: "Remember not the events of the past." Something new is springing forth. In the desert, God makes a way; in the wasteland, rivers. Water in the desert for his chosen people to drink. The past deliverance was real, but the new thing God is doing will surpass even the Exodus. A text bursting with hope and forward momentum.

second.synopsis: Paul counts everything as loss compared to knowing Christ. He wants to know the power of Christ's resurrection and the sharing of his sufferings, being conformed to his death. He has not yet arrived at perfect maturity, but he presses on: "Forgetting what lies behind but straining forward to what lies ahead, I continue my pursuit toward the goal." A restless, athletic spirituality — not complacency but hunger.

gospel.synopsis: The scribes and Pharisees drag a woman caught in adultery before Jesus: the Law says stone her, what do you say? The accusers base their case on commandments chiseled in stone — hard, permanent, unyielding. Jesus bends down and writes on the ground with his finger — in dirt, impermanent and erasable. Then: "Let the one among you who is without sin be the first to throw a stone at her." He bends down again. One by one, beginning with the elders, they walk away. Left alone with the woman, he asks, "Has no one condemned you?" "No one, sir." "Neither do I condemn you. Go, and from now on do not sin any more." Year C's Lenten mercy arc reaches its climax: mercy does not ignore sin, but it refuses to destroy the sinner.

lent-05-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to put down our stones. Year C has been building toward this moment: repent or perish (Week 3), come home to the father (Week 4), and now — neither do I condemn you. The woman caught in adultery is not released from moral responsibility; she is told to sin no more. But she is released from the death sentence her accusers were eager to carry out. Isaiah says God is doing something new. This is it: mercy that does not pretend sin does not exist but refuses to let sin have the last word.

palm-sunday-a.logline: The crowds cry "Hosanna!" as Jesus enters Jerusalem on a donkey, but by Friday the cry has become "Crucify him!" — Matthew's Passion unfolds.
palm-sunday-a.trajectory: Palm Sunday marks the dramatic pivot from Lent into Holy Week. The procession gospel celebrates Jesus' triumphal entry; the Mass readings plunge into the Passion narrative. The emotional whiplash is the point — the same crowd, the same week, shouts both "Hosanna" and "Crucify."

first.synopsis: The third Servant Song: the Lord God has given me a well-trained tongue to speak to the weary. Morning after morning he opens my ear. I gave my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who plucked my beard. My face I did not shield from buffets and spitting. The Lord God is my help; I have set my face like flint. This is the voice of Jesus on the way to the cross — obedient, resolute, absorbing violence without retaliation.

second.synopsis: The great Christ hymn: though he was in the form of God, he did not regard equality with God something to be grasped. He emptied himself, took the form of a slave, and became obedient to the point of death — even death on a cross. Because of this, God greatly exalted him and gave him the name above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bend and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord. The downward arc of self-emptying, then the upward arc of exaltation.

gospel.synopsis: The procession gospel is the triumphal entry: Jesus rides into Jerusalem on a donkey as crowds spread cloaks and branches, crying "Hosanna to the Son of David!" The whole city is shaken. The Passion narrative then tells the full story: the Last Supper, Gethsemane ("My soul is sorrowful even to death"), Judas' betrayal, Peter's denial, the trial before the Sanhedrin and Pilate, the scourging, the crucifixion at Golgotha, darkness at noon, "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?", and Jesus giving up his spirit. Matthew uniquely includes the earthquake, the opening of tombs, and the centurion's confession: "Truly, this was the Son of God!"

palm-sunday-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to walk the entire road from triumph to death in a single liturgy. We process with palms and sing Hosanna; an hour later we hear the Passion read aloud and stand in the crowd that chose Barabbas. The Philippians hymn gives us the theological frame: this is what it looks like when God empties himself completely. Matthew's Passion is dramatic, detailed, and devastating — earthquake, torn veil, opened tombs. We do not skip to the resurrection. We stay at the cross.

palm-sunday-b.logline: Jesus enters Jerusalem to cries of "Hosanna!" but Mark's stark, unsparing Passion strips away every comfort until only the centurion's confession remains.
palm-sunday-b.trajectory: Palm Sunday marks the dramatic pivot from Lent into Holy Week. The procession gospel celebrates Jesus' triumphal entry; the Mass readings plunge into the Passion narrative. The emotional whiplash is the point — the same crowd, the same week, shouts both "Hosanna" and "Crucify."

first.synopsis: The third Servant Song: the Lord God has given me a well-trained tongue to speak to the weary. Morning after morning he opens my ear. I gave my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who plucked my beard. My face I did not shield from buffets and spitting. The Lord God is my help; I have set my face like flint. This is the voice of Jesus on the way to the cross — obedient, resolute, absorbing violence without retaliation.

second.synopsis: The great Christ hymn: though he was in the form of God, he did not regard equality with God something to be grasped. He emptied himself, took the form of a slave, and became obedient to the point of death — even death on a cross. Because of this, God greatly exalted him and gave him the name above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bend and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord. The downward arc of self-emptying, then the upward arc of exaltation.

gospel.synopsis: The procession gospel (Mark or John option) tells of the triumphal entry with cloaks, branches, and "Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord!" The Passion according to Mark is the most spare and relentless of the four. It includes the anointing at Bethany ("She has anticipated anointing my body for burial"), the Last Supper, Gethsemane ("Abba, Father, take this cup"), the mysterious young man who flees naked, Peter's denial, the trial, the scourging, Simon of Cyrene, and the crucifixion. Mark's Jesus is starkly alone: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" Jesus gives a loud cry and dies. The centurion says, "Truly this man was the Son of God!" — the first human in Mark's gospel to say it.

palm-sunday-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us into Mark's unflinching account of the Passion, where Jesus is progressively abandoned by everyone — the crowds, the disciples, Peter, and seemingly even God. Mark offers no softening, no last-minute comfort. The centurion's confession at the foot of the cross is the theological climax of the entire gospel: the Son of God is revealed not in power but in death. The Philippians hymn prepares us: this is what divine self-emptying looks like. We enter Holy Week stripped of illusion.

palm-sunday-c.logline: The disciples praise God with joy as Jesus approaches Jerusalem, but Luke's Passion reveals a savior who forgives even from the cross: "Today you will be with me in Paradise."
palm-sunday-c.trajectory: Palm Sunday marks the dramatic pivot from Lent into Holy Week. The procession gospel celebrates Jesus' triumphal entry; the Mass readings plunge into the Passion narrative. The emotional whiplash is the point — the same crowd, the same week, shouts both "Hosanna" and "Crucify."

first.synopsis: The third Servant Song: the Lord God has given me a well-trained tongue to speak to the weary. Morning after morning he opens my ear. I gave my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who plucked my beard. My face I did not shield from buffets and spitting. The Lord God is my help; I have set my face like flint. This is the voice of Jesus on the way to the cross — obedient, resolute, absorbing violence without retaliation.

second.synopsis: The great Christ hymn: though he was in the form of God, he did not regard equality with God something to be grasped. He emptied himself, took the form of a slave, and became obedient to the point of death — even death on a cross. Because of this, God greatly exalted him and gave him the name above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bend and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord. The downward arc of self-emptying, then the upward arc of exaltation.

gospel.synopsis: The procession gospel is uniquely Lukan: as Jesus approaches the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of disciples praises God with joy. Pharisees tell him to silence them; he replies, "If they keep silent, the stones will cry out!" The Passion according to Luke is the gentlest of the four, but no less devastating. Distinctive moments: Luke alone records Jesus kneeling to pray in Gethsemane — the only time Jesus kneels in the gospels, a posture of desperate supplication in the ancient world; Jesus heals the servant's ear during the arrest; the Lord turns and looks at Peter after his denial; Jesus tells the weeping women, "Do not weep for me"; he prays "Father, forgive them"; and to the repentant thief: "Today you will be with me in Paradise." His last words: "Father, into your hands I commend my spirit." The centurion says, "This man was innocent beyond doubt." Luke's Jesus dies as he lived — merciful, forgiving, trusting the Father.

palm-sunday-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to enter Luke's Passion, where Jesus extends mercy to the very end. Year C has been the cycle of repentance and mercy — from the call to repent, to the prodigal son's homecoming, to the woman spared from stoning — and now the mercy reaches its ultimate expression on the cross itself. "Father, forgive them." "Today you will be with me in Paradise." "Into your hands I commend my spirit." Even in death, Jesus is the merciful one. For music ministers: forgiveness, Paradise, commendation, the stones crying out, and the self-emptying hymn of Philippians 2.

easter-sunday-abc.logline: The tomb is empty, death is defeated, and everything has changed -- this is the day the Lord has made.
easter-sunday-abc.trajectory: Easter Sunday launches the fifty-day season of joy. The readings announce the core event: Jesus is risen. From here the season unfolds through resurrection appearances, the Good Shepherd, the Farewell Discourse, Ascension, and finally Pentecost.

first.synopsis: Peter preaches to the household of Cornelius, summarizing the entire life and mission of Jesus: how God anointed him with the Holy Spirit and power, how he went about doing good, how they killed him on a cross, and how God raised him on the third day. Peter says, 'We ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead.' He and the other apostles are eyewitnesses, commissioned to testify that everyone who believes in Jesus receives forgiveness of sins.

second.synopsis: Paul tells the Colossians: if you were raised with Christ, seek what is above. Your life is now hidden with Christ in God, and when he appears, you will appear with him in glory. The alternate reading from 1 Corinthians uses Passover imagery: Christ our paschal lamb has been sacrificed, so clear out the old yeast of malice and wickedness and celebrate with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.

gospel.synopsis: Mary Magdalene comes to the tomb early in the morning while it is still dark and finds the stone removed. She runs to Peter and the beloved disciple: 'They have taken the Lord.' The two disciples race to the tomb. Peter goes in and sees the burial cloths neatly arranged. Then the beloved disciple enters, sees, and believes -- though they did not yet understand the Scripture that Jesus had to rise from the dead.

easter-sunday-abc.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to stand in the stunned joy of Easter morning. The tomb is empty. The burial cloths are folded. Something unprecedented has happened, and the world will never be the same. Peter's sermon in Acts shows us what a life shaped by this event looks like: bold witness, rooted in personal encounter. The question for us is the same one the empty tomb posed to those first disciples -- what will you do now that death has been defeated?

easter-02-divine-mercy-a.logline: Thomas moves from doubt to the deepest confession of faith while the early Church models radical shared life (Resurrection Appearances).
easter-02-divine-mercy-a.trajectory: Easter Week 2 focuses on resurrection appearances. The gospel is always John 20 -- the 'doubting Thomas' story. The first reading from Acts describes the early community's shared life, showing the Church already living the resurrection. Year A's second reading from 1 Peter celebrates the 'living hope' born from the resurrection.

first.synopsis: The first believers devote themselves to the apostles' teaching, communal life, the breaking of bread, and prayer. Awe comes upon everyone as wonders and signs are done through the apostles. They share everything in common, selling property and distributing to anyone in need. They meet daily in the temple and break bread in their homes with glad and sincere hearts. And every day the Lord adds to their number.

second.synopsis: Peter blesses God who in great mercy gave us new birth to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ. This inheritance is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven. Even though you may suffer various trials now, the genuineness of your faith -- more precious than gold tested by fire -- will prove to be for praise and glory. Though you have not seen Jesus, you love him; though you do not see him now, you believe and rejoice with indescribable joy.

gospel.synopsis: On Easter evening, the disciples are locked behind doors in fear. Jesus appears: 'Peace be with you.' He shows them his hands and side, breathes on them, and says, 'Receive the Holy Spirit.' Thomas is absent and refuses to believe without seeing the wounds. A week later, Jesus appears again: 'Put your finger here. Do not be unbelieving, but believe.' Thomas cries out, 'My Lord and my God!' Jesus responds: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and have believed.'

easter-02-divine-mercy-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to bring our doubts to the risen Christ rather than away from him. Thomas's honest struggle leads to the deepest profession of faith in the entire gospel: 'My Lord and my God!' Meanwhile, Acts shows us that the resurrection wasn't just an idea the early Church believed -- it was a reality that reshaped how they lived, shared, and ate together. Faith that has wrestled with doubt and come through the other side is stronger, not weaker.

easter-02-divine-mercy-b.logline: Thomas touches the wounds of the risen Christ while the early community shares everything in common (Resurrection Appearances).
easter-02-divine-mercy-b.trajectory: Easter Week 2 focuses on resurrection appearances. The gospel is always John 20 -- the 'doubting Thomas' story. The first reading from Acts describes the early community's unity. Year B's second reading from 1 John declares that faith in Jesus conquers the world.

first.synopsis: The community of believers is of one heart and mind. No one claims any possession as their own; they have everything in common. With great power the apostles bear witness to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus. There is no needy person among them, because those who own property or houses sell them and bring the proceeds to the apostles for distribution according to need.

second.synopsis: Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ is begotten by God. The way we know we love God's children is when we love God and keep his commandments -- and his commandments are not burdensome. Whoever is begotten by God conquers the world, and the victory that conquers the world is our faith. Who is the victor over the world but the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God? Jesus came through water and blood, and the Spirit testifies to the truth.

gospel.synopsis: On Easter evening, the disciples are locked behind doors in fear. Jesus appears: 'Peace be with you.' He shows them his hands and side, breathes on them, and says, 'Receive the Holy Spirit.' Thomas is absent and refuses to believe without seeing the wounds. A week later, Jesus appears again: 'Put your finger here. Do not be unbelieving, but believe.' Thomas cries out, 'My Lord and my God!' Jesus responds: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and have believed.'

easter-02-divine-mercy-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see that resurrection faith is not passive or abstract -- it conquers the world. Thomas's doubt gives way to the most profound act of faith in the gospels. In Acts, the early community's radical generosity is the direct fruit of the apostles' witness to the resurrection. And 1 John reminds us that this faith is the victory that overcomes every obstacle. The risen Christ meets us in our fear and doubt, and the encounter changes everything.

easter-02-divine-mercy-c.logline: Thomas encounters the risen Lord as the apostles perform signs and wonders and John sees the Living One on Patmos (Resurrection Appearances).
easter-02-divine-mercy-c.trajectory: Easter Week 2 focuses on resurrection appearances. The gospel is always John 20 -- the 'doubting Thomas' story. Year C's first reading shows the apostles performing signs in Solomon's portico. The second reading from Revelation gives us John's vision of the risen Christ on Patmos -- 'I was dead, but now I am alive forever.'

first.synopsis: Many signs and wonders are done among the people at the hands of the apostles. They gather in Solomon's portico, and though outsiders are afraid to join them, the people hold them in high esteem. Believers in the Lord, great numbers of men and women, are added. People carry the sick into the streets so that Peter's shadow might fall on them. Crowds gather from surrounding towns bringing the sick and those disturbed by unclean spirits, and they are all cured.

second.synopsis: John, exiled on Patmos for proclaiming God's word, is caught up in the Spirit on the Lord's day. He hears a voice like a trumpet and turns to see seven gold lampstands with one like a son of man in their midst, wearing a long robe with a gold sash. John falls at his feet as though dead. But the figure touches him and says, 'Do not be afraid. I am the first and the last, the one who lives. Once I was dead, but now I am alive forever and ever. I hold the keys to death and the netherworld.'

gospel.synopsis: On Easter evening, the disciples are locked behind doors in fear. Jesus appears: 'Peace be with you.' He shows them his hands and side, breathes on them, and says, 'Receive the Holy Spirit.' Thomas is absent and refuses to believe without seeing the wounds. A week later, Jesus appears again: 'Put your finger here. Do not be unbelieving, but believe.' Thomas cries out, 'My Lord and my God!' Jesus responds: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and have believed.'

easter-02-divine-mercy-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to encounter the risen Christ as the one who conquers death -- not as a distant memory, but as living reality. Thomas touches the wounds; John on Patmos sees the glorified Christ holding the keys to death itself; the apostles in Acts channel resurrection power into healing. The words 'Do not be afraid' echo across all three readings. The risen Lord meets us in our exile, our doubt, and our suffering, and declares: I am alive forever.

easter-03-a.logline: Two disciples recognize Jesus in the breaking of bread on the road to Emmaus while Peter proclaims the resurrection in Jerusalem (Resurrection Appearances).
easter-03-a.trajectory: Easter Week 3 continues the resurrection appearances. Year A gives us the Emmaus road story -- one of the most beloved in the gospels. Acts shows Peter's Pentecost sermon declaring that death could not hold Jesus. The second reading from 1 Peter speaks of being ransomed by the precious blood of Christ.

first.synopsis: Peter stands up on Pentecost and preaches boldly. Jesus the Nazorean was a man commended by God with mighty deeds and signs. You killed him by crucifixion, but God raised him up, 'releasing him from the throes of death, because it was impossible for him to be held by it.' Peter quotes David's psalm about not being abandoned to the netherworld. David died and was buried, but as a prophet he foresaw the resurrection of the Christ. God raised Jesus -- the apostles are all witnesses.

second.synopsis: Peter urges reverence during our time of sojourning, reminding us that we were ransomed from futile conduct not with perishable things like silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ, as of a spotless unblemished lamb. He was known before the foundation of the world but revealed in the final time for us. Through him we believe in God who raised him from the dead and gave him glory, so that our faith and hope are in God.

gospel.synopsis: Two disciples walk to Emmaus, downcast and grieving. A stranger joins them and asks what they are discussing. They pour out their disappointment: 'We were hoping he would be the one to redeem Israel.' The stranger opens the Scriptures, showing how the Christ had to suffer and enter into glory. At the village, they urge him to stay. At table, he takes bread, blesses it, breaks it -- and their eyes are opened. They recognize Jesus, but he vanishes. 'Were not our hearts burning within us?' They rush back to Jerusalem.

easter-03-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to recognize the risen Christ in the breaking of bread and the opening of Scripture -- which is to say, in the Mass itself. The Emmaus story is the pattern for every liturgy: we walk with Jesus, we hear the Word, we break bread, and our eyes are opened. Meanwhile, Peter's sermon in Acts shows that recognition demands proclamation. The Christ who walks beside us in our disappointment is the same Christ who reveals himself at the table.

easter-03-b.logline: The risen Jesus appears to his startled disciples in Jerusalem, eats fish, and commissions them as witnesses (Resurrection Appearances).
easter-03-b.trajectory: Easter Week 3 continues the resurrection appearances. Year B gives us Jesus appearing to the disciples in Jerusalem -- proving he is no ghost by eating baked fish. Acts shows Peter calling for repentance. The second reading from 1 John calls Jesus our Advocate with the Father.

first.synopsis: Peter speaks to the people after healing a crippled man. The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob has glorified his servant Jesus -- the one they handed over and denied before Pilate, the Holy and Righteous One. 'The author of life you put to death, but God raised him from the dead; of this we are witnesses.' Peter acknowledges they acted out of ignorance, and that God brought to fulfillment what the prophets foretold: that the Christ would suffer. He calls them to repent and be converted.

second.synopsis: John writes so that his children may not sin, but if anyone does, 'we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous one.' Jesus is expiation for our sins -- not only ours, but for those of the whole world. The way to be sure we know him is to keep his commandments. Anyone who claims to know him but does not keep his commandments is a liar. But whoever keeps his word, the love of God is truly perfected in that person.

gospel.synopsis: The two Emmaus disciples are telling their story when Jesus himself stands in the midst of the group: 'Peace be with you.' They are terrified, thinking they see a ghost. Jesus says, 'Look at my hands and feet. Touch me -- a ghost does not have flesh and bones.' Still incredulous with joy, he asks for food and eats baked fish in front of them. Then he opens their minds to understand the Scriptures and commissions them: repentance and forgiveness will be preached in his name to all nations. 'You are witnesses.'

easter-03-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see that the resurrection is utterly physical and concrete -- not a spiritual metaphor. Jesus eats fish. He shows his hands and feet. He has flesh and bones. This matters because it means the risen life touches the real, material world. In Acts, Peter calls the people to repentance with the astonishing title 'author of life.' And 1 John reminds us that this risen Christ is our Advocate, standing before the Father on our behalf when we fall.

easter-03-c.logline: The risen Jesus restores Peter with a threefold 'Do you love me?' at a lakeside breakfast while the apostles rejoice in suffering for the Name (Resurrection Appearances).
easter-03-c.trajectory: Easter Week 3 continues the resurrection appearances. Year C gives us the breakfast on the beach -- one of the most tender scenes in Scripture. Peter's threefold denial is healed by a threefold confession of love. Acts shows the apostles rejoicing after being punished by the Sanhedrin. Revelation gives us the cosmic worship of the Lamb.

first.synopsis: The apostles are brought before the Sanhedrin. The high priest demands, 'We gave you strict orders to stop teaching in that name!' Peter responds, 'We must obey God rather than men.' He proclaims that the God of their ancestors raised Jesus, whom they had killed by hanging on a tree, and exalted him as leader and savior. 'We are witnesses of these things, as is the Holy Spirit.' The Sanhedrin has them flogged and orders them to stop. They leave rejoicing that they were found worthy to suffer for the Name.

second.synopsis: John sees and hears the voices of countless angels surrounding the throne, the living creatures, and the elders. They cry out in a loud voice: 'Worthy is the Lamb that was slain to receive power and riches, wisdom and strength, honor and glory and blessing.' Then every creature in heaven, on earth, under the earth, and in the sea joins the chorus: 'To the one who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor, glory and might, forever and ever.' The elders fall down and worship.

gospel.synopsis: After the resurrection, the disciples go fishing on the Sea of Tiberias and catch nothing all night. At dawn, Jesus stands on the shore: 'Cast the net to the right side.' The net fills with 153 large fish. The beloved disciple recognizes him: 'It is the Lord!' Peter jumps into the water. Jesus has bread and fish ready on a charcoal fire: 'Come, have breakfast.' Then three times Jesus asks Peter, 'Do you love me?' Three times Peter affirms his love. Three times Jesus commissions him: 'Feed my lambs. Tend my sheep. Feed my sheep. Follow me.'

easter-03-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let the risen Christ restore what is broken in us. Peter denied Jesus three times by a charcoal fire; now by another charcoal fire, Jesus gives him three chances to say 'I love you.' There is no lecture, no punishment -- just a question and a commission. In Acts, the apostles absorb punishment and respond with joy. And in Revelation, all creation joins in worshipping the Lamb. The resurrection is not just forgiveness; it is restoration and re-commissioning for the work ahead.

easter-04-a.logline: Jesus calls himself the gate of the sheepfold as Peter's preaching adds thousands to the flock and we are called to follow the Shepherd's voice (Good Shepherd Sunday).
easter-04-a.trajectory: Easter Week 4 is always Good Shepherd Sunday -- John 10. This is the midpoint of Easter, pivoting from resurrection appearances to Jesus's teaching about his ongoing relationship with his people. Year A focuses on Jesus as the gate: 'I came so that they might have life and have it more abundantly.' Traditional vocations Sunday.

first.synopsis: Peter's Pentecost sermon reaches its climax: 'Let the whole house of Israel know for certain that God has made both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified.' The crowd is cut to the heart: 'What are we to do?' Peter answers: 'Repent and be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.' About three thousand people are baptized that day.

second.synopsis: Peter holds up Christ as the model for patient suffering: when insulted, he returned no insult; when he suffered, he did not threaten. He bore our sins in his body upon the cross so that we might live for righteousness. 'By his wounds you have been healed.' The letter draws on shepherd imagery directly: 'You had gone astray like sheep, but you have now returned to the shepherd and guardian of your souls.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus teaches about the sheepfold. Anyone who climbs over the wall is a thief, but the one who enters through the gate is the shepherd. The sheep hear his voice; he calls them by name and leads them out. They follow him because they recognize his voice but will not follow a stranger. The Pharisees miss the point, so Jesus says plainly: 'I am the gate for the sheep. Whoever enters through me will be saved. A thief comes to steal and destroy; I came so that they might have life and have it more abundantly.'

easter-04-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to recognize the voice of the Good Shepherd in our lives -- and to ask whether we are leading others toward abundant life or away from it. Jesus does not just tend the sheep; he is the gate itself, the way in and out to safety and pasture. In Acts, three thousand people respond to Peter's preaching by entering through that gate in baptism. And 1 Peter reminds us that we were all sheep who had gone astray but have now returned. This is a day to pray for vocations to ministry and priesthood.

easter-04-b.logline: Jesus the Good Shepherd lays down his life freely for the sheep, calling us to recognize his voice and follow (Good Shepherd Sunday).
easter-04-b.trajectory: Easter Week 4 is always Good Shepherd Sunday -- John 10. Year B gives us the heart of the Good Shepherd discourse: 'I am the good shepherd. I lay down my life for the sheep.' Acts declares that salvation comes through no other name. The second reading from 1 John marvels that we are called children of God.

first.synopsis: Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, addresses the leaders and elders. He has healed a crippled man, and they want to know by what power. Peter declares: 'It was in the name of Jesus Christ the Nazorean whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead.' He quotes the psalm about the stone rejected by the builders becoming the cornerstone. Then the bold claim: 'There is no salvation through anyone else, nor is there any other name under heaven given to the human race by which we are to be saved.'

second.synopsis: John marvels at the Father's love: 'See what love the Father has bestowed on us that we may be called the children of God. Yet so we are.' The reason the world does not know us is that it did not know him. We are God's children now, but what we shall be has not yet been revealed. What we do know: when it is revealed, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus declares: 'I am the good shepherd. A good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.' A hired hand runs when the wolf comes because he has no real concern for the sheep. But Jesus knows his sheep and they know him, just as the Father knows him and he knows the Father. He will lay down his life for them. 'I have other sheep that do not belong to this fold. These also I must lead, and there will be one flock, one shepherd.' No one takes his life from him; he lays it down freely.

easter-04-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hear the difference between a shepherd who stays and a hired hand who runs. The good shepherd knows each sheep by name and will die before abandoning them. This is not abstract theology -- it describes how Jesus relates to each of us personally. In Acts, Peter's bold declaration that there is no other name by which we are saved echoes the exclusivity of the shepherd's voice. And 1 John reminds us of the staggering intimacy: we are not just sheep in his flock but children of God.

easter-04-c.logline: Jesus promises eternal life to his sheep whom no one can snatch from his hand, as Paul and Barnabas turn to the Gentiles (Good Shepherd Sunday).
easter-04-c.trajectory: Easter Week 4 is always Good Shepherd Sunday -- John 10. Year C gives us the shortest and most direct Good Shepherd passage: 'My sheep hear my voice. I give them eternal life.' Acts shows Paul and Barnabas pivoting to the Gentiles. Revelation shows the Lamb shepherding the multitude from every nation.

first.synopsis: Paul and Barnabas arrive in Antioch of Pisidia and preach in the synagogue. The following sabbath, almost the entire city gathers to hear the word. But the Jewish leaders, filled with jealousy, contradict Paul. So Paul and Barnabas declare: 'It was necessary that God's word be spoken to you first, but since you reject it, we now turn to the Gentiles.' The Gentiles are delighted; the word spreads through the whole region. Though Paul and Barnabas are expelled, the disciples are filled with joy and the Holy Spirit.

second.synopsis: John sees a great multitude that no one can count, from every nation, race, people, and tongue, standing before the throne in white robes with palm branches. These are the ones who survived the great distress and washed their robes in the blood of the Lamb. God will shelter them. They will never hunger or thirst again. 'The Lamb who is in the center of the throne will shepherd them and lead them to springs of life-giving water, and God will wipe away every tear from their eyes.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus says with striking simplicity: 'My sheep hear my voice; I know them, and they follow me. I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish. No one can take them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all, and no one can take them out of the Father's hand. The Father and I are one.'

easter-04-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us into the deep security of belonging to the Good Shepherd. No one can snatch us from his hand -- or from the Father's hand. This is one of the most reassuring promises in all of Scripture. In Acts, the flock expands dramatically as the gospel moves to the Gentiles, fulfilling the shepherd's words about 'other sheep.' And Revelation shows the end of the story: the Lamb himself shepherds a vast, diverse multitude to springs of living water. Every tear will be wiped away.

easter-05-a.logline: Jesus reveals himself as the way, the truth, and the life as the early Church appoints deacons and Peter calls us living stones (Farewell Discourse begins).
easter-05-a.trajectory: Easter Weeks 5-6 shift into Jesus's Farewell Discourse (John 14-17), where he prepares his disciples for his departure. Year A begins with 'I am the way and the truth and the life' -- one of the great 'I am' statements. Acts shows the Church growing and organizing. 1 Peter calls the community living stones being built into a spiritual house.

first.synopsis: As the number of disciples grows, a complaint arises: Greek-speaking widows are being neglected in the daily distribution of food. The Twelve gather the community and say, 'It is not right for us to neglect the word of God to serve at table.' They ask the community to select seven reputable men filled with the Spirit and wisdom for this service. Stephen and six others are chosen and presented to the apostles, who pray and lay hands on them. The word of God continues to spread, and even a large group of priests become obedient to the faith.

second.synopsis: Peter urges the community: Come to Christ, a living stone rejected by human beings but chosen and precious in God's sight. Like living stones, let yourselves be built into a spiritual house, a holy priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God. The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. Peter declares to the baptized: 'You are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people of his own, so that you may announce the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his wonderful light.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus begins his Farewell Discourse: 'Do not let your hearts be troubled. In my Father's house there are many dwelling places. I am going to prepare a place for you.' Thomas asks, 'How can we know the way?' Jesus answers: 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' Philip says, 'Show us the Father.' Jesus replies: 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father.' He promises that whoever believes in him will do even greater works, because he is going to the Father.

easter-05-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust Jesus as the way forward when the path is unclear. The Farewell Discourse begins with Jesus calming troubled hearts -- he is going somewhere, and he is preparing a place for us. In Acts, the early Church faces growing pains and responds with creative new ministry. And 1 Peter reframes our identity entirely: we are living stones, a royal priesthood, called out of darkness. The way, the truth, and the life is not a roadmap but a person.

easter-05-b.logline: Jesus declares 'I am the vine, you are the branches' as Saul joins the Church and John calls us to love in deed and truth (Farewell Discourse).
easter-05-b.trajectory: Easter Weeks 5-6 are the Farewell Discourse (John 14-17). Year B gives us the vine and branches -- one of Jesus's most vivid images for the spiritual life. Acts tells the dramatic story of Saul's integration into the community. 1 John insists that love must be active, not just words.

first.synopsis: Saul arrives in Jerusalem and tries to join the disciples, but they are all afraid of him. Barnabas takes charge, brings Saul to the apostles, and vouches for his conversion and bold preaching in Damascus. Saul moves about freely and speaks boldly in the name of the Lord, debating with the Greek-speaking Jews, who try to kill him. The brothers send him to Tarsus for safety. The Church throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria is now at peace, being built up and growing in numbers with the consolation of the Holy Spirit.

second.synopsis: John urges: 'Let us love not in word or speech but in deed and truth.' This is how we know we belong to the truth and reassure our hearts -- for God is greater than our hearts and knows everything. His commandment is clear: believe in the name of Jesus Christ and love one another. Those who keep his commandments remain in him, and he in them. The way we know he remains in us is from the Spirit he gave us.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus says: 'I am the true vine, and my Father is the vine grower.' Every branch that does not bear fruit is taken away; every fruitful branch is pruned to bear more. 'Remain in me, as I remain in you. Just as a branch cannot bear fruit on its own unless it remains on the vine, so neither can you unless you remain in me.' Jesus makes the relationship explicit: 'I am the vine, you are the branches. Whoever remains in me and I in him will bear much fruit, because without me you can do nothing.'

easter-05-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine whether we are remaining connected to the vine -- or trying to produce fruit on our own. The image is organic and honest: branches that stay connected bear fruit; branches that disconnect wither. In Acts, Barnabas models what remaining on the vine looks like in practice: he risks his reputation to welcome Saul into the community. And 1 John grounds it all: remain in love, love in action, and the Spirit confirms the connection.

easter-05-c.logline: Jesus gives a new commandment -- love one another as I have loved you -- as Paul and Barnabas build churches and John sees a new heaven and earth (Farewell Discourse).
easter-05-c.trajectory: Easter Weeks 5-6 are the Farewell Discourse (John 14-17). Year C places the new commandment at the center: love one another as Jesus has loved us. Acts shows Paul and Barnabas strengthening young churches. Revelation gives us the stunning vision of a new Jerusalem where God dwells with humanity.

first.synopsis: Paul and Barnabas retrace their missionary journey, returning to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch. They strengthen the disciples and exhort them to persevere in the faith, warning that 'it is necessary for us to undergo many hardships to enter the kingdom of God.' They appoint elders in each church and commend them to the Lord with prayer and fasting. When they return to their home base of Antioch, they call the church together and report how God has opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.

second.synopsis: John sees a new heaven and a new earth. The former world has passed away. A new Jerusalem comes down out of heaven from God, prepared like a bride. A loud voice from the throne declares: 'Behold, God's dwelling is with the human race. He will dwell with them and they will be his people.' God will wipe every tear from their eyes. There shall be no more death or mourning, wailing or pain. The One on the throne says: 'Behold, I make all things new.'

gospel.synopsis: After Judas leaves the table, Jesus speaks: 'Now is the Son of Man glorified, and God is glorified in him.' He tells his disciples he will only be with them a little while longer, and then gives them the new commandment: 'Love one another. As I have loved you, so you also should love one another. This is how all will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another.'

easter-05-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to take the new commandment seriously as the distinguishing mark of the Church: 'This is how all will know that you are my disciples.' Not by our theology or our buildings or our programs, but by our love. In Acts, Paul and Barnabas live this out by returning to young communities to strengthen them, even at personal risk. And Revelation paints the final destination: a world where God dwells among us and every tear is wiped away. The new commandment is the doorway to the new creation.

easter-06-a.logline: Jesus promises the Advocate -- the Spirit of truth who will never leave us -- as the gospel spreads to Samaria and Peter calls us to give a reason for our hope (Farewell Discourse).
easter-06-a.trajectory: Easter Week 6 continues the Farewell Discourse, building toward Ascension and Pentecost. Year A introduces the Advocate, the Spirit of truth: 'I will not leave you orphans.' Acts shows the gospel reaching Samaria and the Spirit being given through the laying on of hands. 1 Peter calls us to be ready to explain our hope.

first.synopsis: Philip goes down to Samaria and proclaims the Christ. The crowds pay attention when they hear his message and see the signs he does: unclean spirits come out of the possessed, and paralyzed and crippled people are cured. Great joy fills the city. When the apostles in Jerusalem hear that Samaria has accepted the word of God, they send Peter and John. They pray that the Samaritans might receive the Holy Spirit -- for it had not yet fallen on them; they had only been baptized. Peter and John lay hands on them, and they receive the Holy Spirit.

second.synopsis: Peter urges: 'Sanctify Christ as Lord in your hearts. Always be ready to give an explanation to anyone who asks you for a reason for your hope, but do it with gentleness and reverence.' Keep your conscience clear so that those who defame you may be put to shame. It is better to suffer for doing good than for doing evil. Christ suffered for sins once, the righteous for the sake of the unrighteous, that he might lead us to God. Put to death in the flesh, he was brought to life in the Spirit.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells his disciples: 'If you love me, you will keep my commandments. And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Advocate to be with you always, the Spirit of truth.' The world cannot accept this Spirit, but the disciples know him because he remains with them. Jesus promises: 'I will not leave you orphans; I will come to you.' On that day, they will realize that Jesus is in the Father, and they are in Jesus, and Jesus is in them. Love and obedience are intertwined.

easter-06-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust the promise that we are not orphans. Jesus is going away, but he is sending the Advocate -- the Spirit of truth -- to remain with us always. In Acts, we see this promise fulfilled as the Holy Spirit falls upon the Samaritans through the apostles' hands. And 1 Peter gives us practical direction: be ready to explain your hope, but do it gently. The Farewell Discourse is building toward departure, but Jesus keeps circling back to reassurance: I am not abandoning you.

easter-06-b.logline: Jesus commands his friends to love one another as the Holy Spirit falls on Gentiles and John proclaims that God is love (Farewell Discourse).
easter-06-b.trajectory: Easter Week 6 continues the Farewell Discourse, building toward Ascension and Pentecost. Year B gives us the love commandment at its most intimate: 'I no longer call you slaves; I have called you friends.' Acts shows the Holy Spirit falling on Cornelius's household -- Gentiles receiving the Spirit before baptism. 1 John distills the gospel to three words: God is love.

first.synopsis: Peter enters the house of Cornelius, a Gentile, and declares: 'In truth, I see that God shows no partiality. In every nation, whoever fears him and acts uprightly is acceptable to him.' While Peter is still speaking, the Holy Spirit falls upon all who are listening. The Jewish believers who came with Peter are astounded that the gift of the Holy Spirit has been poured out on Gentiles too -- they can hear them speaking in tongues and glorifying God. Peter orders them baptized in the name of Jesus Christ.

second.synopsis: John calls the beloved community: 'Let us love one another, because love is of God; everyone who loves is begotten by God and knows God. Whoever is without love does not know God, for God is love.' This is how God's love was revealed: he sent his only Son into the world so that we might have life through him. The heart of it: 'Not that we have loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son as expiation for our sins.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus says: 'As the Father loves me, so I also love you. Remain in my love.' He has told them this so that his joy may be in them and their joy might be complete. Then the commandment: 'Love one another as I love you. No one has greater love than this, to lay down one's life for one's friends.' He redefines the relationship: 'I no longer call you slaves. I have called you friends, because I have told you everything I have heard from my Father. It was not you who chose me, but I who chose you.'

easter-06-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let the word 'friend' sink in. Jesus does not call his followers servants or subjects but friends -- people who are trusted with everything the Father has revealed. In Acts, the boundaries of God's love shatter as the Spirit falls on Gentiles before anyone can gatekeep. And 1 John gives us the theological foundation: God is love, and love always moves first. We did not choose him; he chose us. We did not love first; he did.

easter-06-c.logline: Jesus promises the Holy Spirit will teach everything and leaves his peace as the early Church resolves its first major dispute (Farewell Discourse).
easter-06-c.trajectory: Easter Week 6 continues the Farewell Discourse, building toward Ascension and Pentecost. Year C gives us the promise of the Advocate and the gift of peace: 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you.' Acts shows the Council of Jerusalem working through the Gentile controversy. Revelation shows the new Jerusalem descending in glory.

first.synopsis: A major controversy: some Jewish Christians insist that Gentile converts must be circumcised. Paul and Barnabas are sent to Jerusalem to resolve it. The apostles and elders, in agreement with the whole church, send a letter: 'It is the decision of the Holy Spirit and of us not to place on you any burden beyond these necessities.' The Gentiles need only abstain from meat sacrificed to idols, blood, strangled animals, and unlawful marriage. The early Church navigates its first major doctrinal dispute through discernment and council.

second.synopsis: An angel takes John to a high mountain and shows him the holy city Jerusalem coming down from God, gleaming with divine splendor like jasper, clear as crystal. It has twelve gates with the names of the twelve tribes and twelve foundation stones with the names of the twelve apostles. John sees no temple in the city, because its temple is the Lord God almighty and the Lamb. The city needs no sun or moon -- the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells his disciples: 'Whoever loves me will keep my word, and my Father will love him, and we will come to him and make our dwelling with him.' He promises the Advocate, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in his name: 'He will teach you everything and remind you of all that I told you.' Then the gift: 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give it to you. Do not let your hearts be troubled or afraid.'

easter-06-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to receive the peace Christ gives -- a peace fundamentally different from anything the world offers. It is not the absence of conflict but the presence of the Spirit. In Acts, the early Church demonstrates this peace in action, navigating a crisis that could have split the community by listening to the Holy Spirit together. And Revelation shows the destination: a city lit not by the sun but by the Lamb himself. The Spirit will teach us everything we need to know.

ascension-a.logline: Jesus sends his disciples to the ends of the earth with the Great Commission: 'I am with you always' (Ascension).
ascension-a.trajectory: The Ascension marks the pivot from Easter joy to mission. Jesus has been appearing to his disciples for forty days; now he entrusts them with the work and departs. But this is not abandonment -- it is commissioning. The promise of the Spirit is imminent. Year A gives us Matthew's Great Commission: go, make disciples of all nations.

first.synopsis: Luke opens Acts by recounting how Jesus presented himself alive after his suffering, appearing over forty days and speaking about the kingdom of God. He tells the apostles to wait in Jerusalem for the promise of the Father: 'You will be baptized with the Holy Spirit.' They ask, 'Are you going to restore the kingdom to Israel now?' Jesus redirects: 'You will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes upon you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, throughout Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth.' Then he is lifted up, and a cloud takes him from their sight. Two men in white ask, 'Why are you standing there looking at the sky?'

second.synopsis: Paul prays that God will give the Ephesians a Spirit of wisdom and revelation. He asks that the eyes of their hearts be enlightened to know the hope of God's call, the riches of his inheritance, and the surpassing greatness of his power. This is the same power God worked in Christ, raising him from the dead and seating him at his right hand in the heavens, far above every authority and power. God put all things beneath Christ's feet and gave him as head over all things to the Church, which is his body.

gospel.synopsis: The eleven disciples go to the mountain in Galilee where Jesus told them to go. When they see him, they worship -- though some doubt. Jesus approaches and says: 'All power in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go, therefore, and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, until the end of the age.'

ascension-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to stop looking at the sky and start looking at the world. The Ascension is not a story of Jesus leaving; it is a story of the Church being sent. 'All power has been given to me -- now go.' The promise is breathtaking: 'I am with you always.' In Acts, the angels practically scold the disciples for standing around. And Ephesians reminds us that the same power that raised Jesus is at work in us. The mission is ours now.

ascension-b.logline: Jesus sends the disciples into the whole world to proclaim the gospel as the Lord works with them and confirms the word through signs (Ascension).
ascension-b.trajectory: The Ascension marks the pivot from Easter joy to mission. Year B gives us Mark's version, which is brief and action-oriented: go, proclaim, and the Lord will work with you. The second reading options from Ephesians emphasize the gifts Christ gives to build up the Church. The first reading (Acts 1) is the same across all three years.

first.synopsis: Luke opens Acts by recounting how Jesus presented himself alive after his suffering, appearing over forty days and speaking about the kingdom of God. He tells the apostles to wait in Jerusalem for the promise of the Father: 'You will be baptized with the Holy Spirit.' They ask, 'Are you going to restore the kingdom to Israel now?' Jesus redirects: 'You will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes upon you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, throughout Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth.' Then he is lifted up, and a cloud takes him from their sight.

second.synopsis: The primary reading is the same prayer from Ephesians 1 as Year A: Paul prays for wisdom, revelation, and enlightened hearts, celebrating God's power that seated Christ above all authority. The alternate longer reading from Ephesians 4 urges the community to live worthy of their calling with humility, gentleness, and patience. Christ who descended is the one who ascended, and he gave gifts to the Church: apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, and teachers, to build up the body of Christ toward mature faith.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells his disciples: 'Go into the whole world and proclaim the gospel to every creature. Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved.' He promises signs: driving out demons, speaking new languages, laying hands on the sick. Then the Lord Jesus is taken up into heaven and takes his seat at the right hand of God. The disciples go forth and preach everywhere, 'while the Lord worked with them and confirmed the word through accompanying signs.'

ascension-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust that when we go and proclaim, the Lord works with us. Mark's Ascension account is characteristically no-nonsense: Jesus gives the command, is taken up, and the disciples get to work. The Lord does not leave them to figure it out alone but confirms the word through signs. Ephesians adds that Christ gives specific gifts -- apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, teachers -- so the Church can grow toward maturity. The Ascension is not the end of the story but the beginning of ours.

ascension-c.logline: Jesus blesses his disciples, is taken up to heaven, and they return to Jerusalem with great joy to await the promise of the Father (Ascension).
ascension-c.trajectory: The Ascension marks the pivot from Easter joy to mission. Year C gives us Luke's Ascension account -- the only one that includes the detail of Jesus raising his hands and blessing them as he departs. The response is not grief but great joy. Hebrews (alternate second reading) speaks of Christ entering the heavenly sanctuary on our behalf.

first.synopsis: Luke opens Acts by recounting how Jesus presented himself alive after his suffering, appearing over forty days and speaking about the kingdom of God. He tells the apostles to wait in Jerusalem for the promise of the Father: 'You will be baptized with the Holy Spirit.' They ask, 'Are you going to restore the kingdom to Israel now?' Jesus redirects: 'You will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes upon you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, throughout Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth.' Then he is lifted up, and a cloud takes him from their sight.

second.synopsis: The primary reading is Paul's prayer from Ephesians 1 for wisdom, revelation, and enlightened hearts, celebrating God's power that raised Christ and seated him above all authority. The alternate from Hebrews declares that Christ entered not a sanctuary made by hands but heaven itself, appearing before God on our behalf. He offered himself once for all to take away sin. Through his blood we have confidence to enter the sanctuary by the new and living way he opened through his flesh. Let us hold unwaveringly to our hope, for he who promised is trustworthy.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells his disciples that repentance and forgiveness of sins will be preached in his name to all nations, beginning from Jerusalem: 'You are witnesses of these things.' He promises to send what the Father has promised, but they must stay in the city until they are 'clothed with power from on high.' Then he leads them out to Bethany, raises his hands, and blesses them. As he blesses them, he parts from them and is taken up to heaven. They do him homage and return to Jerusalem with great joy, continually praising God in the temple.

ascension-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to notice that the disciples' response to Jesus's departure is not sorrow but great joy. They return to the temple praising God. Why? Because the Ascension is not loss but fulfillment -- Jesus has entered the heavenly sanctuary on our behalf, and the promise of the Spirit is about to be kept. Luke's beautiful detail of Jesus raising his hands to bless them as he departs means the last thing they see is his blessing. We live under that same blessing, waiting for the power from on high.

easter-07-a.logline: Jesus prays for his disciples' unity and glory as the apostles gather in the upper room with Mary, waiting in prayer for the Spirit (Between Ascension and Pentecost).
easter-07-a.trajectory: Easter Week 7 falls between Ascension and Pentecost -- a week of waiting, prayer, and anticipation. The gospels this week are drawn from Jesus's High Priestly Prayer (John 17). Year A gives us Jesus praying for those the Father gave him, asking that they may share his glory. Acts shows the community gathered in prayer in the upper room. 1 Peter speaks of sharing in Christ's sufferings.

first.synopsis: After Jesus is taken up to heaven, the apostles return to Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives. They go to the upper room -- Peter, John, James, Andrew, Philip, Thomas, Bartholomew, Matthew, James son of Alphaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas son of James. All of them devote themselves with one accord to prayer, together with some women, and Mary the mother of Jesus, and his brothers.

second.synopsis: Peter encourages: 'Rejoice to the extent that you share in the sufferings of Christ, so that when his glory is revealed you may also rejoice exultantly.' If you are insulted for the name of Christ, blessed are you, for the Spirit of glory and of God rests upon you. No one should suffer as a criminal, but whoever suffers as a Christian should not be ashamed but should glorify God because of the name.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus raises his eyes to heaven and prays: 'Father, the hour has come. Give glory to your son, so that your son may glorify you.' Eternal life is this: to know the only true God and the one he sent, Jesus Christ. Jesus says he has accomplished the work the Father gave him and asks to be glorified with the glory he had before the world began. He has revealed the Father's name to those given to him out of the world. They have kept God's word and believed. 'I pray for them. I will no longer be in the world, but they are in the world, while I am coming to you.'

easter-07-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us into the posture of the upper room: gathered together, praying, waiting. Between Ascension and Pentecost, the Church is in a liminal space -- Jesus has departed but the Spirit has not yet come. It is a week to wait actively, as the apostles do with Mary. Jesus's prayer in John 17 reveals what he wants for us: to know the Father, to share his glory, to be protected in the world. And 1 Peter reminds us that suffering for the name of Christ is not shame but glory.

easter-07-b.logline: Jesus prays for his disciples' unity and consecration in truth as the community chooses Matthias to complete the Twelve (Between Ascension and Pentecost).
easter-07-b.trajectory: Easter Week 7 falls between Ascension and Pentecost. Year B continues the High Priestly Prayer with Jesus asking the Father to keep his disciples in unity and consecrate them in truth. Acts shows the community choosing Matthias to replace Judas -- the Twelve must be complete before Pentecost. 1 John distills everything to 'God is love.'

first.synopsis: Peter stands among about one hundred and twenty believers and says that Scripture must be fulfilled concerning Judas, who was guide for those who arrested Jesus. Someone must take his place as witness to the resurrection. They propose two candidates: Justus and Matthias. They pray: 'You, Lord, who know the hearts of all, show which one you have chosen.' They cast lots, and the lot falls on Matthias, who is counted with the eleven apostles.

second.synopsis: John writes: 'If God so loved us, we also must love one another.' No one has ever seen God, but if we love one another, God remains in us and his love is brought to perfection in us. We know we remain in him because he has given us of his Spirit. The Father sent his Son as savior of the world. Whoever acknowledges that Jesus is the Son of God, God remains in him and he in God. 'God is love, and whoever remains in love remains in God and God in him.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus prays: 'Holy Father, keep them in your name that you have given me, so that they may be one just as we are one.' He protected them while he was with them; none was lost except Judas. Now Jesus is coming to the Father and speaks so that they may share his joy completely. He gave them God's word, and the world hated them because they do not belong to it. Jesus does not ask that they be taken out of the world, but kept from the evil one. 'Consecrate them in the truth. Your word is truth. As you sent me into the world, so I sent them into the world.'

easter-07-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hear Jesus pray for us -- not to be taken out of the world but to be consecrated in truth while we remain in it. The Church is sent into the world, not extracted from it. In Acts, the community prepares for mission by completing the Twelve, trusting God to reveal the right person. And 1 John gives us the compass: 'Whoever remains in love remains in God and God in him.' Between Ascension and Pentecost, we are a community rooted in love, waiting to be sent.

easter-07-c.logline: Jesus prays that all believers may be one as Stephen becomes the first martyr and Revelation cries 'Come, Lord Jesus!' (Between Ascension and Pentecost).
easter-07-c.trajectory: Easter Week 7 falls between Ascension and Pentecost. Year C gives us the climax of the High Priestly Prayer: Jesus prays not just for the disciples but for all who will believe through their word -- that they may be brought to perfection as one. Acts shows Stephen's martyrdom. Revelation closes with the cry 'Come, Lord Jesus!'

first.synopsis: Stephen, filled with the Holy Spirit, looks up and sees the glory of God and Jesus standing at the right hand of God: 'Behold, I see the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God.' The crowd covers their ears, rushes upon him, drags him out of the city, and stones him. The witnesses lay their cloaks at the feet of a young man named Saul. As they stone him, Stephen prays, 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit,' and then, 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.' And he falls asleep.

second.synopsis: Jesus declares: 'Behold, I am coming soon. I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end.' Blessed are those who wash their robes to have the right to the tree of life. 'I, Jesus, sent my angel to give you this testimony. I am the root and offspring of David, the bright morning star.' The Spirit and the bride say, 'Come.' The one who gives this testimony says, 'Yes, I am coming soon.' John responds: 'Amen! Come, Lord Jesus!'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus prays not only for the disciples but for all who will believe through their word: 'That they may all be one, as you, Father, are in me and I in you, that they also may be in us, that the world may believe that you sent me.' He has given them the glory the Father gave him, so that they may be one as Father and Son are one -- 'brought to perfection as one, that the world may know that you sent me and loved them even as you loved me.' Jesus prays that where he is, they may also be, to see his glory.

easter-07-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hear the prayer Jesus is still praying for us: that we may be one. This is not polite ecumenism but the deepest desire of Christ's heart -- that our unity would be so complete the world would believe. Stephen's martyrdom in Acts shows what this unity costs: he dies forgiving his killers, echoing Jesus on the cross. And Revelation ends the whole Bible with a cry of longing: 'Come, Lord Jesus!' Between Ascension and Pentecost, we hold the tension between what is and what is coming.

pentecost-a.logline: The Holy Spirit descends in wind and fire, the Church is born speaking every language, and Jesus breathes the Spirit onto his disciples (Pentecost).
pentecost-a.trajectory: Pentecost is the climax of the Easter season -- the fiftieth day. The promise is fulfilled: the Holy Spirit arrives. What began with an empty tomb ends with a room full of fire. The season of Easter joy becomes the ongoing life of the Spirit-filled Church. Year A's second reading from 1 Corinthians emphasizes the diversity of gifts within the one Spirit. The primary gospel is John 20 -- Jesus breathing the Spirit on the disciples.

first.synopsis: When the time for Pentecost is fulfilled, the disciples are all together in one place. Suddenly a noise like a strong driving wind fills the house. Tongues as of fire appear and rest on each of them. They are all filled with the Holy Spirit and begin to speak in different tongues. Devout Jews from every nation are in Jerusalem and are amazed: 'How does each of us hear them in our own language?' Parthians, Medes, Elamites, people from Mesopotamia to Rome, Cretans and Arabs -- all hear the mighty acts of God in their native tongues.

second.synopsis: Paul declares: 'No one can say "Jesus is Lord" except by the Holy Spirit.' There are different kinds of spiritual gifts but the same Spirit, different forms of service but the same Lord, different workings but the same God who produces all of them in everyone. To each person the Spirit is given for some benefit. Just as a body has many parts but is one body, so it is with Christ. In one Spirit we were all baptized into one body -- Jews or Greeks, slaves or free -- and all given to drink of one Spirit.

gospel.synopsis: On Easter evening, the disciples are locked in a room for fear. Jesus comes and stands in their midst: 'Peace be with you.' He shows them his hands and side, and the disciples rejoice. Again he says, 'Peace be with you. As the Father has sent me, so I send you.' Then he breathes on them and says, 'Receive the Holy Spirit. Whose sins you forgive are forgiven them, and whose sins you retain are retained.'

pentecost-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to receive the Holy Spirit -- not as a historical event but as a present reality. The Church is born in wind and fire and immediately speaks every language on earth. This is the reversal of Babel: where human pride scattered and confused, the Spirit gathers and makes understood. Paul reminds us that the Spirit gives different gifts to each person for the common good. And in the gospel, the risen Jesus breathes on his disciples just as God breathed life into Adam. This is new creation. The Easter season ends where the Church's mission begins.

pentecost-b.logline: Wind and fire descend on the gathered disciples as the Spirit gives diverse gifts for one body and guides the Church into all truth (Pentecost).
pentecost-b.trajectory: Pentecost is the climax of the Easter season -- the fiftieth day. The Spirit arrives and the Church is born. Year B offers two second reading options: 1 Corinthians 12 on gifts of the Spirit, or Galatians 5 on the fruit of the Spirit. The alternate gospel from John 15-16 promises the Spirit of truth who will guide into all truth.

first.synopsis: When the time for Pentecost is fulfilled, the disciples are all together in one place. Suddenly a noise like a strong driving wind fills the house. Tongues as of fire appear and rest on each of them. They are all filled with the Holy Spirit and begin to speak in different tongues. Devout Jews from every nation are in Jerusalem and are amazed: 'How does each of us hear them in our own language?' People from across the known world hear the mighty acts of God proclaimed in their native tongues.

second.synopsis: The primary reading from 1 Corinthians declares that no one can say 'Jesus is Lord' except by the Holy Spirit. There are different gifts, services, and workings, but the same Spirit, Lord, and God. In one Spirit we were all baptized into one body. The alternate from Galatians contrasts the works of the flesh -- rivalry, jealousy, selfishness -- with the fruit of the Spirit: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, generosity, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control. If we live in the Spirit, let us follow the Spirit.
