gospel.synopsis: The primary gospel is Easter evening: Jesus appears to the frightened disciples, says 'Peace be with you,' and breathes on them: 'Receive the Holy Spirit.' The alternate from John 15-16 contains Jesus's promise about the Advocate: 'When the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide you to all truth. He will not speak on his own but will declare the things that are coming. He will glorify me, because he will take from what is mine and declare it to you.'

pentecost-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to be guided by the Spirit of truth into the fullness of what Christ has revealed. Pentecost is not just a birthday party for the Church -- it is the moment the Spirit takes up permanent residence among us. In Acts, the Spirit breaks through every language barrier. Paul (or the Galatians reading) shows us what life in the Spirit produces: love, joy, peace, patience. And the alternate gospel promises that the Spirit will continue to teach the Church things we are not yet ready to bear. The Spirit is still at work.

pentecost-c.logline: The Spirit descends in tongues of fire, making adopted children of God who cry 'Abba, Father!' as the Church is born (Pentecost).
pentecost-c.trajectory: Pentecost is the climax of the Easter season -- the fiftieth day. The Spirit arrives and the Church is born. Year C offers a second reading option from Romans 8 that emphasizes the Spirit of adoption -- we are children of God and heirs with Christ. The alternate gospel from John 14 promises the Advocate will teach everything and remind us of all Jesus said.

first.synopsis: When the time for Pentecost is fulfilled, the disciples are all together in one place. Suddenly a noise like a strong driving wind fills the house. Tongues as of fire appear and rest on each of them. They are all filled with the Holy Spirit and begin to speak in different tongues. Devout Jews from every nation are in Jerusalem and are amazed: 'How does each of us hear them in our own language?' People from across the known world hear the mighty acts of God proclaimed in their native tongues.

second.synopsis: The primary reading from 1 Corinthians celebrates the diversity of gifts within the one Spirit and the one body. The alternate from Romans 8 is powerful: if the Spirit of the one who raised Jesus dwells in you, he will give life to your mortal bodies through that Spirit. We are not debtors to the flesh. Those led by the Spirit are children of God. 'You did not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but a Spirit of adoption, through whom we cry, "Abba, Father!"' We are heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ.

gospel.synopsis: The primary gospel is Easter evening: Jesus appears, says 'Peace be with you,' and breathes on the disciples: 'Receive the Holy Spirit.' The alternate from John 14 promises: 'If you love me, keep my commandments, and I will ask the Father and he will give you another Advocate to be with you always.' Whoever loves Jesus will keep his word, and the Father and Son will come and make their dwelling with him. 'The Advocate, the Holy Spirit, will teach you everything and remind you of all that I told you.'

pentecost-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to claim our identity as children of God. The Spirit who descends in wind and fire is the same Spirit who dwells in us, the Spirit of adoption through whom we cry 'Abba, Father!' Pentecost is not distant history -- it is the ongoing reality of the Church. Every time we gather, the Spirit is present, teaching us, reminding us, giving life to our mortal bodies. The Easter season ends not with a conclusion but with a commissioning: filled with the Spirit, we are sent into the world as Christ was sent.

ordinary-time-02-a.logline: John the Baptist identifies Jesus as the Lamb of God, handing off his mission to the one who baptizes with the Holy Spirit.
ordinary-time-02-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): OT 2 stands alone as a transitional week. The gospel is from John (not Matthew), bridging the Christmas/Epiphany season into Ordinary Time. The semi-continuous reading of 1 Corinthians begins this week.

first.synopsis: The second of Isaiah's Servant Songs. God tells his servant that restoring Israel alone is 'too little' -- he will also be a light to the nations, so that God's salvation reaches the ends of the earth. This expands the mission far beyond one people. The servant's vocation is global, universal, and unstoppable.

second.synopsis: Paul opens his letter to the Corinthians with a greeting. He identifies himself as an apostle by God's will, addresses the community as 'sanctified in Christ Jesus' and 'called to be holy,' and extends grace and peace. It is a brief but loaded opening -- Paul is establishing his authority before addressing serious problems in the community.

gospel.synopsis: John the Baptist sees Jesus approaching and declares, 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world.' He testifies that he saw the Spirit descend like a dove and remain on Jesus, confirming this is the one who will baptize with the Holy Spirit. John's whole ministry was preparation for this moment of recognition and witness.

ordinary-time-02-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to recognize that God's saving work is never small or private. Isaiah's servant is sent to the whole world, and John the Baptist points everyone toward the Lamb who takes away the sin of the world. We are called to be witnesses who point others toward Christ, not gatekeepers who limit who gets access to grace.

ordinary-time-02-b.logline: The boy Samuel learns to hear God's voice, while Jesus' first disciples follow him after hearing 'Come, and you will see.'
ordinary-time-02-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): OT 2 uses a gospel from John (not Mark), as all three cycles do for this week. The semi-continuous reading of 1 Corinthians begins. Mark's continuous narrative will pick up next week.

first.synopsis: Young Samuel is sleeping in the temple when God calls his name three times. Each time, Samuel runs to Eli thinking the old priest called him. Finally Eli realizes it is the Lord, and tells Samuel to respond, 'Speak, Lord, for your servant is listening.' Samuel does, and from that night forward the Lord is with him. It is a beautiful story about learning to recognize God's voice.

second.synopsis: Paul makes a startling claim: your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit. He argues against treating the body as disposable or irrelevant to the spiritual life. 'You have been purchased at a price,' he writes, so 'glorify God in your body.' The second reading runs its own semi-continuous course through 1 Corinthians and does not directly connect to the other readings this week.

gospel.synopsis: Two of John the Baptist's disciples hear him call Jesus 'the Lamb of God' and follow Jesus. When Jesus asks, 'What are you looking for?' they ask where he is staying. He says, 'Come, and you will see.' They stay with him that day. Andrew then finds his brother Simon and declares, 'We have found the Messiah.' Jesus renames Simon as Peter. A chain reaction of invitation and discovery.

ordinary-time-02-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to listen for God's voice and to follow where it leads. Samuel had to learn to recognize the Lord's call; the first disciples had to act on curiosity and follow Jesus home. Both stories remind us that vocation begins with attentiveness -- hearing the invitation, saying yes, and then telling others what we have found.

ordinary-time-02-c.logline: God delights in his people like a bridegroom rejoices in his bride, and Jesus reveals his glory at the wedding feast of Cana.
ordinary-time-02-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): OT 2 uses a gospel from John (not Luke). The wedding at Cana is paired with Isaiah's bridal imagery. The semi-continuous reading of 1 Corinthians begins with spiritual gifts.

first.synopsis: God promises to transform Jerusalem's identity. No longer called 'Forsaken' or 'Desolate,' she will be renamed 'My Delight' and 'Espoused.' The imagery is intimate and joyful: as a bridegroom rejoices in his bride, so God rejoices in his people. It is a love poem from God to his city, dripping with tenderness and restoration.

second.synopsis: Paul teaches that the Spirit distributes different gifts to different people -- wisdom, knowledge, faith, healing, prophecy, discernment, tongues -- but all come from the same Spirit. No gift is self-generated or self-serving; each is given 'for some benefit.' The diversity of gifts serves the unity of the community.

gospel.synopsis: At a wedding in Cana, the wine runs out. Mary tells Jesus, and despite his cryptic response ('My hour has not yet come'), she tells the servers, 'Do whatever he tells you.' Jesus turns six stone jars of water into superb wine -- roughly 150 gallons. The headwaiter is astonished that the best wine was saved for last. John calls this 'the beginning of his signs,' through which Jesus revealed his glory.

ordinary-time-02-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see God as one who delights in abundance, not scarcity. Isaiah portrays God rejoicing over his people like a bridegroom. At Cana, Jesus' first miracle is not healing or exorcism but providing lavish wine at a party. God's glory shows up in the places where joy is running dry, transforming ordinary water into something extraordinary.

ordinary-time-03-a-word-of-god-sunday.logline: Jesus begins his public ministry in Galilee, fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy that people in darkness have seen a great light, and calls his first disciples.
ordinary-time-03-a-word-of-god-sunday.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): OT 3 marks the launch of Jesus' Galilean ministry. Matthew explicitly quotes Isaiah to show Jesus fulfilling prophecy. This is also Word of God Sunday (since 2020), celebrating Scripture's role in the life of the Church.

first.synopsis: The land of Zebulun and Naphtali -- degraded and darkened by foreign occupation -- will see a great light. God will shatter the yoke of oppression as decisively as he did on the day of Midian. The joy will be like harvest time, like dividing spoils after victory. What was once a region of gloom becomes the epicenter of hope.

second.synopsis: Paul confronts division in the Corinthian church. People are picking sides: 'I belong to Paul,' 'I belong to Apollos,' 'I belong to Cephas.' Paul cuts through the factionalism with sharp questions: 'Is Christ divided? Was Paul crucified for you?' He was sent not to baptize but to preach the gospel, and not with fancy rhetoric that would empty the cross of its meaning.

gospel.synopsis: After John's arrest, Jesus moves to Capernaum in Galilee, fulfilling Isaiah's words about light dawning in the land of darkness. His preaching begins: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.' Walking by the sea, he calls Simon, Andrew, James, and John, who leave their nets and father to follow immediately. Jesus then travels through Galilee teaching, proclaiming the kingdom, and healing every disease.

ordinary-time-03-a-word-of-god-sunday.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hear the call that launched everything. Jesus enters the darkest, most marginalized territory and declares that the kingdom of heaven is breaking in right there. He calls ordinary working people to drop what they are doing and follow. On this Word of God Sunday, we are reminded that Scripture is not decoration -- it is the light that Isaiah promised, still shining into our darkness.

ordinary-time-03-b-word-of-god-sunday.logline: Jesus proclaims 'The kingdom of God is at hand -- repent and believe,' and calls his first disciples from their fishing nets.
ordinary-time-03-b-word-of-god-sunday.trajectory: Year B (Mark): OT 3 begins Mark's continuous gospel narrative. Mark's account is characteristically urgent -- everything happens 'at once' and 'immediately.' Also Word of God Sunday.

first.synopsis: God sends Jonah to Nineveh with a stark message: 'Forty days more and Nineveh shall be destroyed.' To everyone's surprise -- including Jonah's -- the entire city repents. From the greatest to the least, they fast and put on sackcloth. God sees their change of heart and relents. It is the most successful prophetic preaching in the Bible, and the prophet hates every minute of it.

second.synopsis: Paul writes with urgency: 'The time is running out.' He tells the Corinthians to hold everything loosely -- marriage, grief, joy, possessions -- because 'the world in its present form is passing away.' This is not nihilism; it is radical freedom. When you know the old order is ending, you stop clinging to it.

gospel.synopsis: After John's arrest, Jesus comes to Galilee proclaiming: 'This is the time of fulfillment. The kingdom of God is at hand. Repent, and believe in the gospel.' He calls Simon, Andrew, James, and John from their boats and nets. They follow immediately. Mark wastes no words -- the kingdom is here, and it demands a response right now.

ordinary-time-03-b-word-of-god-sunday.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to respond to urgency with action. Nineveh repented immediately upon hearing God's word. The first disciples dropped their nets without hesitation. Paul says the time is short. On this Word of God Sunday, the readings ask: when God's word breaks in, do we deliberate endlessly, or do we move?

ordinary-time-03-c-word-of-god-sunday.logline: Jesus reads from Isaiah in his hometown synagogue and declares, 'Today this Scripture passage is fulfilled in your hearing.'
ordinary-time-03-c-word-of-god-sunday.trajectory: Year C (Luke): OT 3 is Luke's 'Nazareth manifesto' -- Jesus' programmatic statement of his entire mission, read from Isaiah's scroll. Also Word of God Sunday, which makes this pairing especially potent.

first.synopsis: Ezra the priest reads the Law to the returned exiles from daybreak to midday. The people stand, raise their hands, and weep as they hear God's word. But Nehemiah tells them not to weep: 'Today is holy to the Lord. Go, eat rich foods and drink sweet drinks, and share with those who have nothing. Rejoicing in the Lord must be your strength!' The word brings tears, then feasting.

second.synopsis: Paul's famous Body of Christ teaching. Just as a body has many parts but is one, so it is with Christ's community. The foot cannot say 'I am not a hand, so I don't belong.' The eye cannot say to the hand 'I don't need you.' The parts that seem weakest are the most necessary. If one part suffers, all suffer. You are Christ's body, and individually parts of it.

gospel.synopsis: Luke opens with his purpose statement — writing an orderly account for Theophilus. Then we jump to Jesus returning to Galilee in the power of the Spirit. In the Nazareth synagogue, he reads from Isaiah: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to bring glad tidings to the poor, liberty to captives, sight to the blind, freedom to the oppressed.' The Isaiah passage references the jubilee year — the radical social restructuring from Leviticus 25, when debts were forgiven, slaves freed, and land returned. Jesus rolls up the scroll and says, 'Today this Scripture passage is fulfilled in your hearing.' He is inaugurating a permanent jubilee.

ordinary-time-03-c-word-of-god-sunday.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to take Scripture personally. Ezra reads the Law and the people are transformed. Jesus reads Isaiah and claims it is happening right now, in this room. On Word of God Sunday, the question is not whether we believe the Bible is true in the abstract, but whether we believe it is being fulfilled today -- in our hearing, in our communities, through our hands.

ordinary-time-04-a.logline: Jesus opens the Sermon on the Mount with the Beatitudes, turning the world's values upside down: blessed are the poor, the mourning, the meek.
ordinary-time-04-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Sermon on the Mount 1/6. This is the opening salvo of Jesus' most sustained teaching block in Matthew. The Beatitudes set the tone for everything that follows over the next six weeks. The first reading from Zephaniah echoes the 'humble and lowly' theme.

first.synopsis: Zephaniah calls the humble to seek justice and humility as shelter from God's anger. God promises to leave among his people a 'humble and lowly' remnant who take refuge in the Lord's name. They will do no wrong, speak no lies, and live in peace. It is a portrait of the kind of people the Beatitudes will bless.

second.synopsis: Paul reminds the Corinthians of their own profile: not many wise, powerful, or noble by human standards. God deliberately chose the foolish to shame the wise, the weak to shame the strong, the lowly and despised to reduce the somethings to nothing. The point: no one can boast before God. 'Whoever boasts, should boast in the Lord.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus goes up the mountain, sits down, and begins to teach. The Beatitudes pour out: blessed are the poor in spirit, those who mourn, the meek, those who hunger for righteousness, the merciful, the clean of heart, the peacemakers, those persecuted for righteousness. Each 'blessed' reverses worldly expectations. This is not advice for success -- it is a description of the kingdom's citizens.

ordinary-time-04-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see blessedness where the world sees failure. Zephaniah's humble remnant, Paul's foolish and weak Corinthians, and Jesus' poor and mourning disciples all share a common profile: they have nothing to boast about except God. The Sermon on the Mount begins here because you cannot hear the rest until you accept that the world's scoreboard is wrong.

ordinary-time-04-b.logline: Moses promises a prophet like himself, and Jesus teaches in the Capernaum synagogue with an authority that astonishes everyone -- even the demons obey him.
ordinary-time-04-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. Jesus' early Galilean ministry shows his authority in word and deed. The emphasis is on the astonishing newness of his teaching.

first.synopsis: Moses tells the people that God will raise up a prophet like himself from among their own kin. They are to listen to this prophet. God will put his words into the prophet's mouth. But anyone who presumes to speak falsely in God's name will die. This sets up the expectation: when the real prophet comes, you will know by his authority.

second.synopsis: Paul discusses the practical differences between married and unmarried life. The unmarried can be anxious about the things of the Lord; the married are divided between God and spouse. Paul is not condemning marriage but encouraging undivided devotion to the Lord. This runs its own course through 1 Corinthians and does not directly connect to the gospel.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus enters the synagogue in Capernaum and teaches. Mark deliberately omits what Jesus taught — focusing only on how he taught: the people are astonished because he teaches with authority, not like the scribes. A man with an unclean spirit confronts him: 'Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are -- the Holy One of God!' Jesus commands, 'Quiet! Come out of him!' The spirit convulses the man and leaves. Everyone is amazed: 'A new teaching with authority. He commands even the unclean spirits and they obey him.'

ordinary-time-04-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to encounter the authority of Jesus -- not authority that comes from credentials or tradition, but from his very identity. Moses promised a prophet who would speak God's own words. In the Capernaum synagogue, that promise is fulfilled in a way no one expected: a teacher whose words have power over evil itself. The question is whether we will listen.

ordinary-time-04-c.logline: The hometown crowd turns on Jesus when he reminds them that God's prophets were sent to outsiders, not insiders who felt entitled to miracles.
ordinary-time-04-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): This is the dramatic conclusion of the Nazareth synagogue scene that began last week. Luke pairs it with Jeremiah's call, linking prophetic rejection to prophetic mission. 1 Corinthians 13 (the love hymn) provides the second reading.

first.synopsis: God tells Jeremiah he was known and dedicated before birth, appointed a prophet to the nations. But the job comes with a warning: stand up, speak everything God commands, and do not be crushed by opposition. God will make Jeremiah 'a fortified city, a pillar of iron, a wall of brass.' They will fight against him but not prevail. Prophets need armor because truth makes people furious.

second.synopsis: Paul's hymn to love, arguably the most famous passage in his letters. Without love, even speaking in angelic tongues is just noise. Love is patient, kind, not jealous or pompous. It bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things. Love never fails. Faith, hope, and love remain, 'but the greatest of these is love.' This reading runs independently of the gospel.

gospel.synopsis: Continuing from last week, the Nazareth crowd initially speaks highly of Jesus. But when he reminds them that Elijah was sent to a foreign widow and Elisha healed a foreign leper -- implying God's favor is not limited to insiders -- they are filled with fury. They drive him to the brow of a hill to hurl him off, but he passes through them and walks away. The hometown that knew him as Joseph's son cannot accept that God's grace is bigger than their claim on it.

ordinary-time-04-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine whether we are comfortable with a God who shows mercy beyond our circle. Jeremiah was fortified against his own people's rage. Jesus' neighbors tried to kill him when he suggested God works among outsiders. The prophetic word is hardest to hear when it challenges our sense of ownership over God's favor.

ordinary-time-05-a.logline: Sermon on the Mount 2/6: Jesus tells his disciples they are salt and light -- their good deeds should shine so that others glorify God.
ordinary-time-05-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Sermon on the Mount 2/6. After the Beatitudes, Jesus now tells his followers what their identity means in practice. Isaiah provides the 'light' imagery that Jesus picks up.

first.synopsis: God tells the people what real worship looks like: share your bread with the hungry, shelter the homeless, clothe the naked, do not turn your back on your own. Then -- and only then -- 'your light shall break forth like the dawn.' If you remove oppression and feed the afflicted, 'light shall rise for you in the darkness.' The light is not automatic; it flows from justice.

second.synopsis: Paul reminds the Corinthians that he did not come with impressive rhetoric or worldly wisdom. He resolved to know nothing except Jesus Christ crucified. He came in weakness, fear, and trembling, relying on a demonstration of Spirit and power rather than persuasive words. The goal: their faith should rest on God's power, not human cleverness.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells his disciples, 'You are the salt of the earth' -- but salt that loses its taste is useless. 'You are the light of the world' -- a city on a hill cannot be hidden, and no one lights a lamp to put it under a bushel basket. 'Your light must shine before others, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your heavenly Father.' Identity leads to visibility.

ordinary-time-05-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to understand that holiness is not hidden. Isaiah says light breaks forth when we share bread and shelter the homeless. Jesus says we are already salt and light -- the question is whether we will be flavorful and visible. Our good deeds are not for self-promotion; they point others toward God.

ordinary-time-05-b.logline: Job laments his suffering while Jesus heals Simon's mother-in-law and drives out demons, then slips away before dawn to pray.
ordinary-time-05-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. A day in the life of Jesus' early ministry: teaching, healing, exorcism, prayer. Job provides the contrast -- someone deep in suffering who has not yet encountered the healer.

first.synopsis: Job is at his lowest. He compares human life to drudgery and slavery. Nights drag on with restlessness; days fly past without hope. 'Remember that my life is like the wind; I shall not see happiness again.' This is raw, unfiltered lament -- the cry of someone who has lost everything and sees no way forward.

second.synopsis: Paul says preaching the gospel is not something he can boast about -- it is an obligation. 'Woe to me if I do not preach it!' Though free, he has made himself a slave to all. 'To the weak I became weak, to win over the weak. I have become all things to all, to save at least some.' Everything is for the sake of the gospel.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus leaves the synagogue and heals Simon's mother-in-law of a fever — Mark's word for 'raised her up' (egeiro) is the same word the angel will use at the empty tomb, deliberately using resurrection language for an ordinary healing. By evening, the whole town is gathered at the door, bringing the sick and possessed. Jesus cures many and drives out demons. Then, rising very early before dawn, he goes to a deserted place to pray. When the disciples find him and say, 'Everyone is looking for you,' he replies, 'Let us go on to the nearby villages that I may preach there also. For this purpose have I come.'

ordinary-time-05-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to bring our brokenness into the presence of the healer. Job cries out from the depths of despair; Jesus responds to every kind of suffering that comes to the door. But Jesus also models something crucial: before the crowds and demands consume him, he prays. Ministry that does not begin in prayer eventually burns out.

ordinary-time-05-c.logline: Isaiah sees God's glory and cries 'I am undone,' Peter sees Jesus' power and falls to his knees saying 'Depart from me, I am a sinful man.'
ordinary-time-05-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. The call of Peter echoes Isaiah's temple vision -- both involve overwhelming encounters with holiness, confession of unworthiness, and being sent on mission anyway.

first.synopsis: In the year King Uzziah died, Isaiah sees the Lord on a high throne, attended by seraphim crying 'Holy, holy, holy!' Isaiah is undone: 'Woe is me! I am a man of unclean lips.' A seraph touches his mouth with a burning coal and purges his sin. Then God asks, 'Whom shall I send?' Isaiah answers, 'Here I am -- send me!' The pattern: encounter holiness, confess unworthiness, be cleansed, be sent.

second.synopsis: Paul hands on the core of the gospel: Christ died for our sins, was buried, was raised on the third day, and appeared to Cephas, the Twelve, five hundred at once, James, all the apostles, and finally to Paul himself. Paul calls himself 'the least of the apostles' because he persecuted the church. 'By the grace of God I am what I am, and his grace to me has not been ineffective.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus teaches crowds from Simon's boat, then tells Simon to put out into deep water and lower his nets. Simon protests -- they fished all night and caught nothing -- but obeys. The catch is so enormous that both boats nearly sink. Simon Peter falls at Jesus' knees: 'Depart from me, Lord, for I am a sinful man.' Jesus replies, 'Do not be afraid; from now on you will be catching men.' They leave everything and follow.

ordinary-time-05-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to recognize that the encounter with God's holiness does not disqualify us -- it commissions us. Isaiah confessed his unclean lips and was sent. Peter confessed his sinfulness and was called. Paul persecuted the church and became its greatest apostle. God does not wait for us to be worthy; he makes us worthy by calling us.

ordinary-time-06-a.logline: Sermon on the Mount 3/6: Jesus deepens the commandments -- not just 'don't kill' but 'don't even hold anger'; not just 'don't lie' but 'let your yes be yes.'
ordinary-time-06-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Sermon on the Mount 3/6. Jesus begins his 'You have heard it said... but I say to you' teaching, the antitheses. He is not abolishing the Law but radicalizing it, demanding transformation at the level of the heart.

first.synopsis: Sirach presents the fundamental truth of moral freedom: God sets before you fire and water, life and death, good and evil -- stretch forth your hand to whichever you choose. God commands no one to act unjustly and gives no one license to sin. The wisdom of the Lord is immense; he sees everything. Human choice is real, and God holds us accountable for it.

second.synopsis: Paul distinguishes between the wisdom of this age (which is passing away) and God's mysterious, hidden wisdom. This divine wisdom was predetermined before the ages for our glory, and 'none of the rulers of this age knew it; for if they had known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.' The Spirit reveals what eye has not seen and ear has not heard.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus declares he has come not to abolish the Law but to fulfill it. Then come the antitheses: 'You have heard it said, Do not kill -- but I say, whoever is angry with his brother will be liable to judgment.' Reconcile before you bring your gift to the altar. 'You have heard it said, Do not commit adultery -- but I say, anyone who looks with lust has already committed adultery in his heart.' Do not swear oaths at all -- 'Let your Yes mean Yes, and your No mean No.'

ordinary-time-06-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to move from surface compliance to interior transformation. Sirach says God gives us real moral choice. Jesus says the choice goes deeper than we thought: it is not enough to avoid murder if your heart harbors contempt; it is not enough to keep oaths if your word cannot stand on its own. God wants not just obedient behavior but a transformed heart.

ordinary-time-06-b.logline: A leper says 'If you wish, you can make me clean,' and Jesus -- moved with pity -- reaches out and touches the untouchable.
ordinary-time-06-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. Jesus' healing ministry extends to those excluded by purity laws. Mark emphasizes Jesus' compassion and the man's inability to keep the secret quiet.

first.synopsis: The Law prescribes how leprosy is to be handled. The priest declares the person unclean. The leper must tear his garments, go bareheaded, cover his mouth, and cry out 'Unclean, unclean!' He must live alone, outside the camp. This is not just a medical quarantine -- it is total social and religious exclusion.

second.synopsis: Paul summarizes his ethical teaching: 'Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do everything for the glory of God.' Avoid giving offense to anyone. He tries to please everyone, not for his own benefit but so they may be saved. 'Be imitators of me, as I am of Christ.' A short, punchy reading about living for others.

gospel.synopsis: A leper approaches Jesus and kneels: 'If you wish, you can make me clean.' Moved with pity, Jesus stretches out his hand and touches him -- something the Law forbade. 'I do will it. Be made clean.' The leprosy leaves immediately. Jesus tells him to tell no one, but the man spreads the news everywhere, making it impossible for Jesus to enter towns openly.

ordinary-time-06-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see who we have declared 'unclean' and placed outside our camp. Leviticus built walls around the sick; Jesus crosses those walls with a touch. The leper's faith was not in doubt -- 'if you wish, you can.' The question was whether Jesus would be willing to reach across the boundary of exclusion. He was.

ordinary-time-06-c.logline: Luke's Beatitudes come with blessings and woes: blessed are you who are poor, but woe to you who are rich -- you have already received your consolation.
ordinary-time-06-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. Luke's version of the Beatitudes (the 'Sermon on the Plain') is more concrete and more confrontational than Matthew's, adding four woes to the four blessings.

first.synopsis: Jeremiah draws a sharp contrast. Cursed is the one who trusts in human beings -- like a barren bush in the desert. Blessed is the one who trusts in the Lord -- like a tree planted beside water, with deep roots, green leaves, and fruit even in drought. Where you place your trust determines whether you wither or flourish.

second.synopsis: Paul confronts those who deny the resurrection. If Christ has not been raised, faith is in vain, and we are still in our sins. 'If for this life only we have hoped in Christ, we are the most pitiable people of all.' But the punchline: 'Now Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.' Everything hangs on the resurrection.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus stands on a stretch of level ground and teaches. Luke's Beatitudes are direct -- 'Blessed are you who are poor' (not 'poor in spirit') -- and they come with matching woes: 'Woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation. Woe to you who are filled now, for you will be hungry. Woe to you who laugh now. Woe to you when all speak well of you.' These are not gentle encouragements; they are reversals.

ordinary-time-06-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to reckon with the full force of Jesus' economic vision. Jeremiah says trusting in human security is a dead end. Luke's Jesus does not soften the Beatitudes with spiritual qualifiers -- he blesses actual poverty and pronounces woe on actual wealth. This is uncomfortable, and it is meant to be. The question is where we are rooted.

ordinary-time-07-a.logline: Sermon on the Mount 4/6: 'Love your enemies' -- Jesus demands a holiness that goes beyond reciprocity, just as the Father makes the sun rise on the bad and the good.
ordinary-time-07-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Sermon on the Mount 4/6. The antitheses continue with the most radical of all: love of enemies. This teaching builds on the Beatitudes' vision of mercy and peacemaking and pushes it to the extreme.

first.synopsis: God tells Moses to speak to the whole community: 'Be holy, for I, the Lord, your God, am holy.' Do not bear hatred in your heart. Do not take revenge or hold a grudge. 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself. I am the Lord.' This is the Torah's great love commandment -- which Jesus will now radicalize beyond its original scope.

second.synopsis: Paul declares: you are the temple of God, and the Spirit dwells in you. Anyone who destroys God's temple, God will destroy. The wisdom of this world is foolishness to God. Stop boasting about human leaders -- Paul, Apollos, Cephas -- because everything belongs to you, you belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus overturns 'an eye for an eye' with 'offer no resistance to one who is evil.' Turn the other cheek. Give your cloak too. Go the extra mile. Then the summit: 'Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you, that you may be children of your heavenly Father, for he makes his sun rise on the bad and the good.' If you only love those who love you, what is unusual about that? 'Be perfect, just as your heavenly Father is perfect.'

ordinary-time-07-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us beyond the natural human instinct toward reciprocity. Leviticus said 'love your neighbor as yourself'; Jesus extends the neighbor category to include the enemy. The standard is not fairness but the Father's own indiscriminate generosity -- sun and rain for all. This is the Sermon on the Mount at its most demanding.

ordinary-time-07-b.logline: God declares 'I am doing something new' and 'your sins I remember no more,' while Jesus forgives a paralytic's sins before healing his body.
ordinary-time-07-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. The paralytic lowered through the roof is one of Mark's most vivid miracle stories, but the real controversy is about forgiveness, not healing.

first.synopsis: God says: Stop remembering the past. 'See, I am doing something new! Now it springs forth, do you not perceive it?' In the desert I make a way, in the wasteland, rivers. Yet Israel grew weary of God and burdened him with sins. But God's response is astonishing: 'It is I, I, who wipe out, for my own sake, your offenses; your sins I remember no more.'

second.synopsis: Paul insists that Jesus Christ was not 'yes and no' -- he was and is the definitive 'Yes.' All of God's promises find their Yes in him. God has anointed us, put his seal upon us, and given the Spirit in our hearts as a first installment. This begins the semi-continuous reading of 2 Corinthians.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus is teaching in a packed house in Capernaum when four men lower a paralytic through the roof. Seeing their faith, Jesus says, 'Child, your sins are forgiven.' The scribes are scandalized: 'Who but God alone can forgive sins?' Jesus responds: 'Which is easier, to say Your sins are forgiven, or Rise and walk?' He tells the paralytic to rise, and the man walks out carrying his mat. Everyone is astounded.

ordinary-time-07-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust that God is doing something genuinely new in our lives. Isaiah says forget the old things -- something fresh is springing up. Jesus does not just heal a body; he forgives sins, claiming an authority that only God possesses. The friends who tore open a roof show us what persistent, creative faith looks like: finding a way in when every door is blocked.

ordinary-time-07-c.logline: David spares Saul's life when he could have killed him, and Jesus teaches 'Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.'
ordinary-time-07-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. Luke's 'Sermon on the Plain' continues with love of enemies and radical mercy. David's refusal to take revenge on Saul provides the Old Testament echo.

first.synopsis: David sneaks into Saul's camp at night and finds his enemy sleeping. Abishai urges him to kill Saul on the spot, but David refuses: 'Who can lay hands on the Lord's anointed and remain unpunished?' Instead, he takes Saul's spear and water jug and retreats. From a distance, he announces what he did: 'The Lord delivered you into my grasp, but I would not harm the Lord's anointed.' Restraint in the face of a perfect opportunity for revenge.

second.synopsis: Paul contrasts the first Adam (earthly, natural) with the last Adam, Christ (heavenly, life-giving spirit). Just as we have borne the image of the earthly one, we shall also bear the image of the heavenly one. This reading is part of Paul's sustained argument about resurrection in 1 Corinthians 15.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus lays out the most demanding teaching in the gospels: love your enemies, do good to those who hate you, bless those who curse you. Turn the other cheek. Give your tunic and your cloak. Lend expecting nothing back. 'Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.' Stop judging. Stop condemning. Forgive. Give generously, 'and gifts will be given to you; a good measure, packed together, shaken down, and overflowing, will be poured into your lap.'

ordinary-time-07-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to practice mercy when we have every right to seek justice. David could have killed Saul and been legally justified. Jesus asks for something harder than restraint -- active love for enemies, generosity toward those who take from us. The measure we use will be measured back to us. Mercy is not weakness; it is the family resemblance of God's children.

ordinary-time-08-a.logline: Sermon on the Mount 5/6: 'Do not worry about tomorrow' -- Jesus points to birds and wildflowers as evidence that the Father provides.
ordinary-time-08-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Sermon on the Mount 5/6. Jesus moves from moral teaching to trust teaching: if God cares for birds and flowers, how much more for you? Isaiah's tenderness pairs perfectly.

first.synopsis: Zion cries, 'The Lord has forsaken me; my Lord has forgotten me.' God's response is one of the most tender images in Scripture: 'Can a mother forget her infant, be without tenderness for the child of her womb? Even should she forget, I will never forget you.' Just five verses, but they carry enormous weight.

second.synopsis: Paul calls himself a servant of Christ and steward of God's mysteries. Stewards must be trustworthy, but Paul does not even judge himself -- the Lord is the one who judges. 'Do not make any judgment before the appointed time,' when the Lord will bring to light what is hidden in darkness and manifest the motives of our hearts.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus says you cannot serve both God and mammon. Then he unfolds his teaching on worry: 'Do not worry about your life, what you will eat or drink or wear.' Look at the birds -- they do not sow or reap, but the Father feeds them. Look at the wildflowers -- not even Solomon was dressed like them. 'Seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be given you besides. Do not worry about tomorrow; tomorrow will take care of itself.'

ordinary-time-08-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to release the anxious grip we have on our own survival. Isaiah's God says, 'Even if a mother could forget, I will never forget you.' Jesus says, 'Look at the birds, look at the flowers -- your Father knows what you need.' This is not a call to irresponsibility but to reordered priorities: seek the kingdom first, and let the Father handle the rest.

ordinary-time-08-b.logline: God courts Israel like a lover in the desert, and Jesus says his presence is like a wedding feast -- you do not fast while the bridegroom is with you.
ordinary-time-08-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. Questions arise about why Jesus' disciples do not fast. His answer introduces the bridegroom image and the new wine metaphor. 2 Corinthians begins its semi-continuous run.

first.synopsis: God speaks as a spurned lover who refuses to give up. 'I will lead her into the desert and speak to her heart.' God promises a new betrothal: 'I will espouse you to me forever: in right and in justice, in love and in mercy; I will espouse you in fidelity, and you shall know the Lord.' A stunning image of God's relentless, covenant love.

second.synopsis: Paul says the Corinthians themselves are his letter of recommendation -- 'written not in ink but by the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets that are hearts of flesh.' He ministers a new covenant, one not of letter but of Spirit, because 'the letter brings death, but the Spirit gives life.'

gospel.synopsis: People ask why Jesus' disciples do not fast like John's or the Pharisees'. Jesus answers with a question: 'Can the wedding guests fast while the bridegroom is with them?' The time for fasting will come when the bridegroom is taken away. Then he adds: no one patches an old cloak with new cloth, and no one pours new wine into old wineskins. What Jesus brings cannot be contained in the old forms.

ordinary-time-08-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to recognize that Jesus brings something so new it cannot be patched onto old frameworks. Hosea shows God courting his people back with tenderness. Jesus calls himself the bridegroom whose very presence transforms the occasion. We are invited to ask: are we trying to pour new wine into old wineskins, or are we letting God do the new thing?

ordinary-time-08-c.logline: You know people by their fruit: a good tree does not bear rotten fruit, and from the fullness of the heart the mouth speaks.
ordinary-time-08-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. The Sermon on the Plain concludes with parables about blindness, hypocrisy, and bearing fruit. The test of character is what comes out of us.

first.synopsis: Sirach uses vivid images of testing: shake a sieve and the husks appear. Fire tests pottery; tribulation tests the just. Speech reveals character like fruit reveals the care given to a tree. 'Praise no one before he speaks, for it is then that people are tested.' Talk is not cheap -- it is the definitive revelation of who you are.

second.synopsis: Paul reaches the climax of his resurrection argument. When the mortal puts on immortality, death is swallowed up in victory. 'Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?' The sting of death is sin, but God gives victory through Jesus Christ. Therefore, 'be firm, steadfast, always fully devoted to the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus asks, 'Can a blind person guide a blind person? Will not both fall into a pit?' He warns against hypocrisy: why notice the splinter in your brother's eye but miss the beam in your own? Remove the beam first. Then the fruit test: a good tree bears good fruit, a rotten tree bears rotten fruit. 'A good person out of the store of goodness in his heart produces good; for from the fullness of the heart the mouth speaks.'

ordinary-time-08-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine what comes out of us when we are tested. Sirach says speech reveals character the way fruit reveals a tree's health. Jesus warns against the self-deception of correcting others while ignoring our own blindness. The fullness of the heart overflows through the mouth. What fills us is eventually what flows from us.

ordinary-time-09-a.logline: Sermon on the Mount 6/6: The grand conclusion -- build your house on rock by actually doing what Jesus teaches, not just hearing it.
ordinary-time-09-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Sermon on the Mount 6/6. The six-week arc concludes with Jesus' stark warning: hearing is not enough; only doing the Father's will counts. The house-on-rock parable brings the series to a dramatic close. Romans begins its semi-continuous run.

first.synopsis: Moses sets before the people a blessing and a curse: a blessing for obeying God's commandments, a curse for disobedience and idolatry. Take these words into your heart and soul; bind them at your wrist, wear them on your forehead. The choice is stark and binary -- obey or stray, blessing or curse.

second.synopsis: Paul declares that God's righteousness has been manifested apart from the Law, through faith in Jesus Christ. All have sinned and fall short, but all are justified freely by grace through the redemption in Christ Jesus. 'We consider that a person is justified by faith apart from works of the law.' This opens Paul's sustained argument in Romans about grace, faith, and justification.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus warns: 'Not everyone who says to me, Lord, Lord, will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father.' Some will claim they prophesied and drove out demons in his name, and he will say, 'I never knew you.' Then the famous parable: the wise man builds his house on rock (hearing and doing), the fool builds on sand (hearing without doing). When the storm comes, only one house stands.

ordinary-time-09-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to close the gap between hearing and doing. The Sermon on the Mount ends not with a test of doctrine but a test of practice. Moses said choose blessing or curse. Jesus says the difference between wisdom and foolishness is whether you act on what you hear. Six weeks of extraordinary teaching come down to one question: will you build on rock or sand?

ordinary-time-09-b.logline: Jesus declares 'The sabbath was made for man, not man for the sabbath' and heals a man's withered hand on the sabbath, provoking deadly opposition.
ordinary-time-09-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. Two sabbath controversies show Jesus asserting his authority over religious law. The conflict with the Pharisees escalates to the point where they begin plotting his death.

first.synopsis: God commands Israel to keep the sabbath holy. No work for anyone -- not you, your children, your slaves, your animals, or the foreigners among you. The reason is deeply humane: 'Remember that you too were once a slave in Egypt.' The sabbath exists because God liberated slaves, and no one should be worked without rest.

second.synopsis: Paul says God who made light shine out of darkness has shone in our hearts through Christ. But 'we hold this treasure in earthen vessels' so the power is clearly God's, not ours. Afflicted but not constrained, persecuted but not abandoned, struck down but not destroyed -- 'always carrying about in the body the dying of Jesus, so that the life of Jesus may be manifested in our body.'

gospel.synopsis: Two sabbath stories back to back. First, the disciples pick grain on the sabbath; Jesus defends them by citing David eating the bread of offering and declaring, 'The sabbath was made for man, not man for the sabbath.' Then in the synagogue, with a man's withered hand, Jesus asks, 'Is it lawful to do good on the sabbath or to do evil?' Silence. Angry and grieved, he heals the man. The Pharisees immediately begin plotting with the Herodians to destroy him.

ordinary-time-09-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to ask whether our religious observance serves people or constrains them. Deuteronomy rooted sabbath rest in the liberation of slaves -- it was always about human dignity. Jesus recovers that original purpose: the sabbath exists for human flourishing, not as a trap. When religion becomes an excuse not to do good, something has gone deeply wrong.

ordinary-time-09-c.logline: A Roman centurion's faith astonishes Jesus: 'I am not worthy to have you enter under my roof -- just say the word and my servant will be healed.'
ordinary-time-09-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. The centurion story highlights faith from outside Israel. Solomon's prayer for foreigners in the first reading provides the Old Testament setup. Galatians begins its semi-continuous run.

first.synopsis: At the temple dedication, Solomon prays for the foreigner who comes from a distant land to honor God. He asks God to listen from heaven and do whatever the foreigner asks, 'that all the peoples of the earth may know your name.' From the very beginning, Israel's temple was meant to be a house of prayer for all nations.

second.synopsis: Paul opens Galatians with heat. He is amazed the Galatians are so quickly abandoning the gospel for a different version. He pronounces a curse on anyone -- even an angel from heaven -- who preaches a different gospel. 'Am I trying to please people? If I were still trying to please people, I would not be a slave of Christ.' The tone is confrontational from the first sentence.

gospel.synopsis: A Roman centurion's slave is dying. The centurion sends Jewish elders to ask Jesus for help, and they vouch for him: 'He loves our nation and built our synagogue.' But as Jesus approaches, the centurion sends friends with an extraordinary message: 'Lord, I am not worthy to have you enter under my roof. Just say the word and let my servant be healed.' Jesus is amazed: 'Not even in Israel have I found such faith.' The slave is healed.

ordinary-time-09-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to recognize that faith shows up in unexpected people and places. Solomon prayed that God would hear the foreigner. The centurion -- a Roman military officer, a pagan by Jewish standards -- displays the kind of trust that amazes Jesus himself. His words echo in every Mass: 'Lord, I am not worthy.' Faith is not about pedigree; it is about humble trust.

ordinary-time-10-a.logline: God desires mercy, not sacrifice -- and Jesus calls Matthew the tax collector to follow him, then sits down to eat with sinners.
ordinary-time-10-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Mission and Discipleship 1/4. After the Sermon on the Mount, Matthew shifts to Jesus' actions. The call of Matthew (the tax collector for whom this gospel is named) signals that Jesus' community includes those considered unworthy.

first.synopsis: God laments that Israel's piety is like a morning cloud -- it evaporates quickly. He has struck them through the prophets and slain them with his words. The core indictment: 'It is love that I desire, not sacrifice, and knowledge of God rather than holocausts.' God cares more about the heart's disposition than religious performance.

second.synopsis: Paul holds up Abraham as the model of faith. Against all hope, Abraham believed he would become 'the father of many nations.' He did not weaken in faith despite his old age and Sarah's barren womb. His faith was credited as righteousness -- and Paul says the same will be credited to us who believe in the one who raised Jesus from the dead.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus sees Matthew sitting at the tax collector's booth and says, 'Follow me.' Matthew gets up and follows. At dinner in Matthew's house, many tax collectors and sinners are present. The Pharisees ask why Jesus eats with such people. Jesus replies: 'Those who are well do not need a physician, but the sick do. Go and learn the meaning of the words, I desire mercy, not sacrifice. I did not come to call the righteous but sinners.'

ordinary-time-10-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to ask whether our religion is about mercy or performance. Hosea's God wants love, not burnt offerings. Jesus deliberately seeks out the people everyone else avoids. When we find ourselves uncomfortable with who is welcome at the table, we may be on the wrong side of the gospel. The physician goes to the sick, not the healthy.

ordinary-time-10-b.logline: Genesis tells the story of humanity's fall, and Jesus redefines family: 'Whoever does the will of God is my brother and sister and mother.'
ordinary-time-10-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. Jesus faces accusations of being possessed, responds with the 'house divided' argument, and redefines who belongs to his family. Genesis provides the backdrop of how sin entered the world.

first.synopsis: After eating from the forbidden tree, Adam hides from God. 'Where are you?' God asks. Adam blames Eve; Eve blames the serpent. God curses the serpent: 'I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and hers; he will strike at your head, while you strike at his heel.' The pattern of sin: shame, hiding, blame-shifting -- and God's promise that it will not have the last word.

second.synopsis: Paul says faith drives him to speak. Though the outer self is wasting away, the inner self is being renewed day by day. 'This momentary light affliction is producing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison.' We look not to what is seen but to what is unseen, because the visible is transitory; the invisible is eternal.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus' relatives think he is out of his mind. Scribes from Jerusalem accuse him of being possessed by Beelzebul. Jesus demolishes their logic: a kingdom divided against itself cannot stand. Then his mother and brothers arrive and call for him. Jesus looks at those seated around him and says, 'Here are my mother and my brothers. Whoever does the will of God is my brother and sister and mother.' Blood ties are secondary to the kingdom.

ordinary-time-10-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to reconsider what 'family' means in the kingdom of God. Genesis shows the original family fracturing through sin and blame. Jesus' own blood relatives think he is crazy. But Jesus creates a new family based not on biology or status but on doing God's will. The most intimate language -- mother, brother, sister -- belongs to those who follow.

ordinary-time-10-c.logline: Elijah raises a widow's dead son, and Jesus raises a widow's dead son at Nain -- 'A great prophet has arisen in our midst; God has visited his people.'
ordinary-time-10-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. The raising of the widow's son at Nain deliberately echoes Elijah's miracle, signaling that Jesus is in the prophetic tradition but surpassing it.

first.synopsis: The son of the widow of Zarephath stops breathing. She accuses Elijah of bringing her guilt to light. Elijah takes the boy, stretches himself over him three times, and prays desperately. The Lord hears; the boy revives. Elijah returns the child to his mother: 'See! Your son is alive.' The widow responds: 'Now indeed I know that you are a man of God.'

second.synopsis: Paul insists his gospel came not from human teaching but through a direct revelation of Jesus Christ. He recounts his former life persecuting the church, his call by grace, and his early years in Arabia and Damascus before finally visiting Cephas in Jerusalem. The gospel he preaches has divine origin, not human credentials.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus approaches the town of Nain and meets a funeral procession: a widow's only son is being carried out. Moved with pity, Jesus says, 'Do not weep,' touches the coffin, and commands, 'Young man, I tell you, arise!' The dead man sits up and begins to speak. Jesus gives him to his mother. The crowd is seized with fear: 'A great prophet has arisen in our midst. God has visited his people.'

ordinary-time-10-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust that God meets us at the point of our deepest loss. Both the widow of Zarephath and the widow of Nain were facing the worst possible scenario -- a mother burying her only son. In both cases, God intervenes through a prophet who restores life. Jesus does not wait to be asked; he is moved by compassion. God visits his people precisely where grief is heaviest.

ordinary-time-11-a.logline: Mission and Discipleship: Jesus sees crowds like sheep without a shepherd, names his twelve apostles, and sends them out to proclaim the kingdom and heal.
ordinary-time-11-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Mission and Discipleship 2/4. Jesus commissions the Twelve. The focus shifts from what Jesus teaches to what his followers are sent to do. The first reading echoes the 'kingdom of priests' identity.

first.synopsis: At Sinai, God tells Moses to remind Israel what he has done: 'I bore you up on eagle wings and brought you here to myself.' If they keep the covenant, they will be God's 'special possession, dearer to me than all other people,' a 'kingdom of priests, a holy nation.' The calling is both intimate and public -- beloved by God and set apart for a mission.

second.synopsis: Paul marvels at the timing of God's love: 'While we were still sinners Christ died for us.' We did not earn reconciliation; it happened while we were enemies. How much more, now that we are reconciled, will we be saved by his life? 'We boast of God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received reconciliation.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus sees the crowds, troubled and abandoned 'like sheep without a shepherd,' and his heart is moved with pity. He tells the disciples, 'The harvest is abundant but the laborers are few.' Then he summons the Twelve by name, gives them authority over unclean spirits and diseases, and sends them out: 'Go to the lost sheep of Israel. Proclaim the kingdom of heaven is at hand. Cure the sick, raise the dead, cleanse lepers, drive out demons. Without cost you have received; without cost you are to give.'

ordinary-time-11-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see ourselves as sent, not just saved. Exodus calls Israel a kingdom of priests -- every member has a priestly vocation. Jesus looks at harassed, helpless people and his response is not to do it all himself but to commission laborers. The mission is clear, the authority is given, and the terms are non-negotiable: everything received freely must be given freely.

ordinary-time-11-b.logline: The kingdom of God is like a mustard seed -- the smallest of all seeds that becomes the largest of plants, with branches big enough to shelter birds.
ordinary-time-11-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. Jesus teaches in parables about the mysterious growth of the kingdom -- it grows on its own, invisibly, from the smallest beginning to the largest result.

first.synopsis: God promises to take a tender shoot from the top of a cedar and plant it on a high mountain. It will bear fruit and become majestic; birds of every kind will nest in its shade. God brings low the high tree and lifts the lowly tree, withers the green tree and makes the dry tree bloom. The Lord reverses what seems established and grows what seems insignificant.

second.synopsis: Paul says we are always courageous, knowing that while at home in the body we are away from the Lord. 'We walk by faith, not by sight.' We would rather leave the body and go home to the Lord, but either way, we aspire to please him. All must appear before Christ's judgment seat to receive recompense for what we did in the body.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells two seed parables. First: a man scatters seed, sleeps and rises, and the seed grows on its own -- 'he knows not how.' The earth yields fruit by itself: blade, ear, full grain. Second: the kingdom is like a mustard seed, 'the smallest of all seeds,' that becomes the largest of plants with branches big enough for birds to nest. Jesus spoke only in parables to the crowds, but explained everything privately to his disciples.

ordinary-time-11-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust the slow, hidden work of God's kingdom. Ezekiel's tender shoot becomes a great tree. Jesus' mustard seed becomes a shelter. The kingdom does not grow by our anxious effort but 'of its own accord.' Our job is to scatter the seed faithfully, sleep and rise, and trust that God handles the growth we cannot see or control.

ordinary-time-11-c.logline: A sinful woman anoints Jesus' feet with tears and ointment, and Jesus tells Simon: the one who has been forgiven much, loves much.
ordinary-time-11-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. One of Luke's most distinctive scenes. The contrast between Simon the Pharisee and the unnamed woman dramatizes the relationship between forgiveness and love. David's confrontation with Nathan echoes the theme of sin, confession, and pardon.

first.synopsis: Nathan confronts David over his affair with Bathsheba and murder of Uriah. He catalogs God's generosity: 'I anointed you king, rescued you from Saul, gave you everything.' Then the accusation: 'Why have you spurned the Lord?' David confesses simply: 'I have sinned against the Lord.' Nathan responds: 'The Lord has forgiven your sin; you shall not die.' Confession meets immediate pardon.

second.synopsis: Paul declares that no one is justified by works of the law but only through faith in Christ. 'I have been crucified with Christ; yet I live, no longer I, but Christ lives in me.' He refuses to nullify God's grace: 'If justification comes through the law, then Christ died for nothing.' This is the heart of Paul's theology in compressed form.

gospel.synopsis: A Pharisee named Simon invites Jesus to dinner. A sinful woman enters, weeps at Jesus' feet, bathes them with tears, dries them with her hair, and anoints them with ointment. Simon is scandalized. Jesus tells a parable: two debtors, one owed 500 days' wages, the other 50, both forgiven. Who loves more? The one forgiven more. Jesus turns to the woman and contrasts her lavish love with Simon's minimal hospitality: 'Her many sins have been forgiven because she has shown great love.' He tells her, 'Your faith has saved you; go in peace.'

ordinary-time-11-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see the connection between being forgiven and being able to love. David confessed his sin and was pardoned. The unnamed woman's extravagant devotion flowed from her experience of extravagant forgiveness. Simon offered the bare minimum because he felt no need for mercy. The question is not whether we have sinned, but whether we know we have been forgiven -- and what that knowledge sets free in us.

ordinary-time-12-a.logline: Mission and Discipleship: Jesus tells the Twelve not to fear those who kill the body -- 'you are worth more than many sparrows.'
ordinary-time-12-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Mission and Discipleship 3/4. The commissioning continues with a sober warning: the mission will bring persecution. But God's knowledge of each disciple is intimate enough to count the hairs on their heads.

first.synopsis: Jeremiah voices the prophet's lament: friends are watching for him to stumble, whispering 'Denounce him!' But the Lord is with him 'like a mighty champion'; his persecutors will stumble instead. He entrusts his cause to God and breaks into praise: 'Sing to the Lord! He has rescued the life of the poor from the power of the wicked.'

second.synopsis: Paul traces the architecture of sin and grace. Through one man (Adam) sin entered the world, and through sin, death. But the gift is not like the transgression: 'If by the transgression of the one the many died, how much more did the grace of God and the gracious gift of the one man Jesus Christ overflow for the many.' Grace is not proportional to sin; it overwhelms it.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells the Twelve: 'Fear no one.' What is whispered will be proclaimed from housetops. Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Two sparrows are sold for a small coin, yet not one falls without the Father's knowledge. 'Even all the hairs of your head are counted. You are worth more than many sparrows.' Acknowledge Jesus before others, and he will acknowledge you before the Father. Deny him, and he will deny you.

ordinary-time-12-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to choose courage over self-preservation. Jeremiah's enemies conspired against him, but God was his champion. Jesus does not promise his followers safety -- he promises that their suffering is known to the last detail by a Father who counts the hairs on their heads. The sparrow theology is not sentimental; it is a call to fearless witness in a dangerous world.

ordinary-time-12-b.logline: A violent storm threatens to sink the boat, and Jesus rebukes the wind and sea: 'Quiet! Be still!' -- leaving the disciples to wonder, 'Who then is this?'
ordinary-time-12-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. The stilling of the storm is a revelation of Jesus' identity -- the one who has power over chaos itself. Job's God who set limits to the sea provides the Old Testament echo.

first.synopsis: God finally answers Job -- out of a storm. 'Who shut within doors the sea when it burst forth from the womb? When I made the clouds its garment and thick darkness its swaddling bands? When I set limits for it and said: Thus far shall you come but no farther, and here shall your proud waves be stilled!' Only the Creator has authority over the chaotic sea.

second.synopsis: Paul says the love of Christ impels us: since one died for all, all have died. Those who live should no longer live for themselves but for Christ. 'Whoever is in Christ is a new creation: the old things have passed away; behold, new things have come.' A compressed theology of transformation.

gospel.synopsis: Evening falls and Jesus tells the disciples, 'Let us cross to the other side.' A violent squall comes up; waves break over the boat. Jesus is asleep on a cushion. The terrified disciples wake him: 'Teacher, do you not care that we are perishing?' He rebukes the wind and commands the sea: 'Quiet! Be still!' Total calm. Then he asks them, 'Why are you terrified? Do you not yet have faith?' They are filled with awe: 'Who then is this whom even wind and sea obey?'

ordinary-time-12-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let Jesus into the storms we are navigating. In Job, God is the one who set limits to the sea. In Mark, Jesus exercises that same divine authority, sleeping peacefully while chaos rages. The disciples' question -- 'Who is this?' -- is the question Mark wants every reader to answer. And the harder question: 'Do you not yet have faith?'

ordinary-time-12-c.logline: Peter declares 'You are the Christ of God,' and Jesus immediately predicts his suffering, death, and resurrection, then says 'Take up your cross daily.'
ordinary-time-12-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. The hinge point of the gospel: Peter's confession leads directly to the first passion prediction and the call to carry the cross. Zechariah and Galatians both touch on piercing, identity, and baptismal transformation.

first.synopsis: God promises to pour out 'a spirit of grace and petition' on Jerusalem. They shall look on the one they have pierced and mourn as for an only son. On that day a fountain will open to purify from sin and uncleanness. The early church read this as pointing directly to the crucified Christ.

second.synopsis: Paul declares: through faith, all are children of God in Christ. In baptism, you have clothed yourselves with Christ. 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, neither slave nor free, not male and female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus.' Old categories of division are dissolved in baptismal identity. If you belong to Christ, you are Abraham's heirs.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus prays alone, then asks his disciples, 'Who do the crowds say that I am?' Various answers: John the Baptist, Elijah, one of the prophets. 'But who do you say that I am?' Peter answers, 'The Christ of God.' Jesus immediately predicts his suffering, rejection, death, and resurrection. Then to all: 'If anyone wishes to come after me, he must deny himself and take up his cross daily and follow me. Whoever wishes to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will save it.'

ordinary-time-12-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hold together the glory and the cost of following Christ. Peter got the identity right: Jesus is the Messiah. But Jesus immediately redefines what Messiah means: not triumphant conquest but suffering, death, and resurrection. If we want to follow this particular Messiah, the path goes through self-denial and daily cross-bearing. There is no Easter without Friday.

ordinary-time-13-a.logline: Mission and Discipleship concludes: whoever loses his life for Jesus' sake will find it, and whoever gives even a cup of cold water will not lose his reward.
ordinary-time-13-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Mission and Discipleship 4/4. The final installment of Jesus' mission discourse. The cost is high -- putting Jesus above family -- but so is the promise. Even the smallest act of hospitality counts.

first.synopsis: A woman of influence in Shunem regularly feeds Elisha and prepares a room for him. He asks what can be done for her. Learning that she has no son and her husband is old, Elisha promises, 'This time next year you will be fondling a baby son.' Generous hospitality to a prophet brings an unexpected, life-changing reward.

second.synopsis: Paul teaches that baptism is death and resurrection with Christ. 'We were buried with him through baptism into death, so that, just as Christ was raised from the dead, we too might live in newness of life.' If we died with Christ, we believe we will also live with him. Think of yourselves as dead to sin and living for God in Christ Jesus.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus raises the stakes: 'Whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me.' Whoever does not take up his cross and follow is not worthy. But there is paradox: 'Whoever loses his life for my sake will find it.' Then a stunning promise about hospitality: whoever receives a prophet gets a prophet's reward; whoever gives a cup of cold water to a disciple 'will surely not lose his reward.' Even the smallest kindness counts in the kingdom.

ordinary-time-13-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to reckon with both the radical cost and the surprising accessibility of discipleship. Jesus demands a loyalty that surpasses even family bonds. But he also says a cup of cold water counts. The Shunammite woman's simple hospitality to Elisha brought her the gift of a son. In the kingdom, no sacrifice is too great and no kindness is too small.

ordinary-time-13-b.logline: God did not make death, and Jesus raises a dead girl and heals a woman who has been bleeding for twelve years -- faith overcomes both.
ordinary-time-13-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. Two healing stories are intertwined (a Markan 'sandwich'): the woman with hemorrhages and Jairus' daughter. Both involve twelve years, desperate faith, and Jesus' power over illness and death.

first.synopsis: God did not make death, nor does he rejoice in the destruction of the living. He fashioned all things for life; the creatures of the world are wholesome, and justice is undying. God made human beings to be imperishable, in the image of his own nature. But by the devil's envy, death entered the world. Death is an intruder, not God's design.

second.synopsis: Paul encourages generous giving by pointing to Christ: 'Though he was rich, for your sake he became poor, so that by his poverty you might become rich.' He calls for equality: your abundance should supply others' needs now, so that their abundance may supply yours later. 'Whoever had much did not have more, and whoever had little did not have less.'

gospel.synopsis: Two desperate stories interlocked. Jairus, a synagogue official, begs Jesus to heal his dying twelve-year-old daughter. On the way, a woman who has been hemorrhaging for twelve years touches Jesus' cloak and is healed: 'Daughter, your faith has saved you.' Then word comes that Jairus' daughter has died. Jesus says, 'Do not be afraid; just have faith.' He enters, takes the child's hand, and says, 'Talitha koum -- Little girl, arise!' She gets up and walks. Two women, two kinds of suffering, one response: faith.

ordinary-time-13-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to bring our most desperate situations to Jesus and persist in faith. The woman with hemorrhages had tried everything and spent everything. Jairus was told his daughter was already dead. Both kept going -- touching the cloak, walking to the house. Wisdom says God did not make death. Jesus proves it with his bare hands, speaking life into a dead child's body.

ordinary-time-13-c.logline: Elisha burns his plow and follows Elijah; Jesus tells would-be followers that no one who looks back after starting is fit for the kingdom.
ordinary-time-13-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. Jesus 'resolutely sets his face toward Jerusalem,' beginning the long journey that defines the second half of Luke's gospel. The cost of following is starkly presented.

first.synopsis: Elijah throws his cloak over Elisha, who is plowing with twelve yoke of oxen. Elisha asks to say goodbye to his parents first. Elijah's response is ambiguous, but Elisha takes decisive action: he slaughters the oxen, burns the plowing equipment to cook their flesh, feeds the people, and follows Elijah. He does not just leave his old life -- he destroys the equipment so he cannot go back.

second.synopsis: Paul declares: 'For freedom Christ set us free!' But this freedom is not license for the flesh; rather, 'serve one another through love.' The whole law is fulfilled in 'Love your neighbor as yourself.' If you live by the Spirit, you will not gratify the desires of the flesh. The Spirit and the flesh are opposed; freedom means choosing the Spirit.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus sets his face toward Jerusalem -- Luke's turning point. A Samaritan village rejects him; James and John want to call down fire, but Jesus rebukes them. Three would-be followers appear. 'I will follow you wherever you go' -- but the Son of Man has nowhere to rest his head. 'Let me bury my father first' -- 'Let the dead bury their dead; go and proclaim the kingdom.' 'Let me say farewell to my family' -- 'No one who sets a hand to the plow and looks to what was left behind is fit for the kingdom of God.'

ordinary-time-13-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to count the cost of following Jesus without romanticizing it. Elisha burned his plow -- there was no going back. Jesus tells three people the truth: this road has no comfortable resting place, no detours for family obligations, no backward glances. The journey to Jerusalem has begun, and the only direction is forward.

ordinary-time-14-a.logline: Parables begin: Jesus thanks the Father for revealing mysteries to the little ones and invites the burdened, 'Come to me and I will give you rest.'
ordinary-time-14-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Beginning of the Parables section. After the Sermon on the Mount (OT 4-9) and Mission Discourse (OT 10-13), Matthew enters Jesus' parable teaching. This week transitions with Jesus' prayer of praise and his invitation to the weary.

first.synopsis: Rejoice, daughter Zion! Your king comes to you -- just, a savior, meek, riding on a donkey. He will banish chariots and warhorses; he will proclaim peace to the nations. His dominion will stretch from sea to sea, to the ends of the earth. This is kingship defined by humility and peace, not military power.

second.synopsis: Paul says believers are not in the flesh but in the Spirit, if the Spirit of God dwells in them. The same Spirit that raised Jesus from the dead will give life to our mortal bodies. We are not debtors to the flesh. 'If you live according to the flesh, you will die, but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.'

gospel.synopsis: Jesus prays aloud: 'I give praise to you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, for although you have hidden these things from the wise and learned you have revealed them to little ones.' Then the great invitation: 'Come to me, all you who labor and are burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am meek and humble of heart; and you will find rest for yourselves. For my yoke is easy, and my burden light.'

ordinary-time-14-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to come to Jesus with our burdens rather than pretending we can carry them alone. Zechariah's king comes in meekness, not power. Jesus himself is meek and humble of heart. The mysteries of the kingdom are hidden from the self-sufficient 'wise' and revealed to the little ones who know they need help. Rest is not earned; it is received by those humble enough to accept it.

ordinary-time-14-b.logline: A prophet is not without honor except in his native place -- Jesus returns to Nazareth and is rejected by the people who watched him grow up.
ordinary-time-14-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. The rejection at Nazareth is a pivotal scene: those who know Jesus best refuse to believe. Paul's 'thorn in the flesh' and 'power made perfect in weakness' provide a powerful counterpoint.

first.synopsis: God sends Ezekiel to the Israelites -- 'rebels who have rebelled against me.' They are 'hard of face and obstinate of heart.' Whether they listen or not, 'they shall know that a prophet has been among them.' The prophet's job is to speak; the people's response is their own responsibility.

second.synopsis: Paul reveals a 'thorn in the flesh' -- some unspecified suffering he begged God three times to remove. God's answer: 'My grace is sufficient for you, for power is made perfect in weakness.' Paul's response: 'I will boast most gladly of my weaknesses, in order that the power of Christ may dwell with me. When I am weak, then I am strong.' One of the most paradoxical and consoling passages in Paul.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus returns to his hometown and teaches in the synagogue. The people are astonished but also offended: 'Is he not the carpenter, the son of Mary?' They know his family; they cannot reconcile familiarity with authority. Jesus observes, 'A prophet is not without honor except in his native place.' He is amazed at their lack of faith and can perform no mighty deed there, apart from curing a few sick.

ordinary-time-14-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine whether familiarity has bred contempt in our relationship with God. Ezekiel was sent to rebels who might not listen.
