Jesus' own neighbors could not see past the carpenter they remembered. Paul discovered that God's power shows up precisely in weakness, not in the impressive displays Nazareth demanded. Sometimes the biggest obstacle to faith is thinking we already know the whole story.

ordinary-time-14-c.logline: Jesus sends seventy-two disciples ahead of him like lambs among wolves, and they return rejoicing that even the demons are subject to them.
ordinary-time-14-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. The sending of the seventy-two expands the mission beyond the Twelve. Luke's version emphasizes peace, vulnerability, and the reversal of power. Galatians' 'marks of Jesus' reading provides a closing frame for Paul's letter.

first.synopsis: Rejoice with Jerusalem! God promises to spread prosperity over her like a river. The imagery is maternal: you will nurse at her abundant breasts, be carried in her arms, fondled in her lap. 'As a mother comforts her child, so will I comfort you.' When you see this, your hearts will rejoice and your bodies flourish like the grass.

second.synopsis: Paul boasts only in the cross. 'Through the cross the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world.' Circumcision and uncircumcision do not matter -- only a new creation. 'From now on, let no one make troubles for me; for I bear the marks of Jesus on my body.' This is the last reading from Galatians in the semi-continuous series.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus appoints seventy-two others and sends them in pairs to every town he intends to visit. 'The harvest is abundant but the laborers are few.' He sends them like lambs among wolves, carrying nothing. They are to bring peace to every house and cure the sick, announcing the kingdom of God. The seventy-two return with joy: 'Even the demons are subject to us because of your name!' Jesus responds, 'I have observed Satan fall like lightning from the sky. But do not rejoice that the spirits are subject to you; rejoice because your names are written in heaven.'

ordinary-time-14-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to embrace the paradox of missionary power: we are sent out vulnerable, carrying nothing, like lambs among wolves. Yet the kingdom breaks through. Isaiah promises a mother's comfort; Jesus promises authority over evil. But the real cause for joy is not that we have power -- it is that our names are written in heaven. Identity matters more than results.

ordinary-time-15-a.logline: Parables: Jesus tells the parable of the sower -- seed falls on the path, on rocks, among thorns, and on rich soil, and only the last bears fruit.
ordinary-time-15-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Parables section continues. The Sower is the first and foundational parable of Matthew 13, with a detailed allegorical explanation. Isaiah's word-as-rain image sets the stage.

first.synopsis: God compares his word to rain and snow that come down from heaven, water the earth, make it fertile, and give seed to the sower and bread to the eater. 'So shall my word be that goes forth from my mouth; my word shall not return to me void, but shall do my will, achieving the end for which I sent it.' God's word is effective; it accomplishes what it is sent to do.

second.synopsis: Paul says the sufferings of this present time are nothing compared with the glory to be revealed. All creation groans, waiting to be 'set free from slavery to corruption and share in the glorious freedom of the children of God.' Even we who have the firstfruits of the Spirit groan within ourselves, waiting for the redemption of our bodies.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells the crowd the parable of the sower. Seed falls on the path (snatched by the evil one), on rocky ground (received with joy but rootless), among thorns (choked by worldly anxiety and the lure of riches), and on rich soil (bearing fruit a hundred, sixty, or thirtyfold). When the disciples ask why he speaks in parables, Jesus explains that understanding the kingdom is a gift. He then gives the detailed allegorical explanation: the seed is the word, the soils are different human responses.

ordinary-time-15-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine what kind of soil we are. Isaiah promises that God's word never returns empty. But the parable shows that the word's effectiveness depends on the soil -- on our receptivity. The enemy, shallowness, anxiety, and greed can all prevent the word from bearing fruit. The question is not whether God's word is powerful; it is whether we create the conditions for it to take root.

ordinary-time-15-b.logline: Amos the shepherd-prophet is told to stop prophesying, and Jesus sends the Twelve out two by two with nothing but authority and a walking stick.
ordinary-time-15-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. The sending of the Twelve mirrors the Year A mission discourse but in Mark's spare, urgent style. Amos provides the model of an unlikely prophet. Ephesians begins its semi-continuous run.

first.synopsis: Amaziah, the priest of Bethel, tells Amos to leave and prophesy somewhere else -- this is the king's sanctuary. Amos replies: 'I was no prophet, nor have I belonged to a company of prophets; I was a shepherd and a dresser of sycamores.' But the Lord took him from following the flock and said, 'Go, prophesy to my people Israel.' Prophetic authority comes from God's call, not human credentials.

second.synopsis: A magnificent hymn of praise. God chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world. In love he destined us for adoption. In Christ we have redemption by his blood, the forgiveness of sins. God has made known the mystery of his will: to sum up all things in Christ. We were sealed with the Holy Spirit, the 'first installment of our inheritance.' This reading opens the semi-continuous series in Ephesians.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus summons the Twelve and sends them out two by two with authority over unclean spirits. His instructions are stark: take nothing but a walking stick -- no food, no money, no second tunic. Stay wherever you are welcomed; shake the dust off your feet where you are not. They go out, preach repentance, drive out demons, and anoint and cure the sick. The mission is lean, urgent, and Spirit-powered.

ordinary-time-15-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust that God equips the called, not the qualified. Amos was a shepherd with no prophetic credentials; God sent him anyway. Jesus sends the Twelve with nothing but authority and each other. The mission does not depend on our resources or preparation; it depends on the one who sends us. Traveling light is not deprivation -- it is dependence on God.

ordinary-time-15-c.logline: A lawyer asks 'Who is my neighbor?' and Jesus answers with the parable of the Good Samaritan: the one who showed mercy is the neighbor.
ordinary-time-15-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. The Good Samaritan is one of Luke's most distinctive and best-known parables, found only in his gospel. It redefines 'neighbor' from an object of obligation to an agent of mercy.

first.synopsis: Moses tells the people that God's command is not too mysterious or remote. It is not up in the sky or across the sea, requiring some hero to retrieve it. 'No, it is something very near to you, already in your mouths and in your hearts; you have only to carry it out.' The word of God is accessible; the only challenge is doing it.

second.synopsis: A hymn to Christ's supremacy. He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation. All things were created through him and for him. He is before all things, and in him all things hold together. He is the head of the body, the church. In him all the fullness was pleased to dwell, and through him to reconcile all things by the blood of his cross. This begins the semi-continuous reading of Colossians.

gospel.synopsis: A lawyer tests Jesus: 'What must I do to inherit eternal life?' Jesus turns the question back: 'What does the Law say?' The lawyer answers correctly: love God completely and your neighbor as yourself. But then he asks, 'And who is my neighbor?' Jesus tells the story of a man beaten and left for dead. A priest and a Levite pass by. A Samaritan -- a despised outsider -- stops, bandages the wounds, takes him to an inn, and pays for his care. 'Which of these was neighbor to the victim?' 'The one who showed mercy.' 'Go and do likewise.'

ordinary-time-15-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to stop asking 'Who qualifies as my neighbor?' and start asking 'Am I being a neighbor?' Deuteronomy says the command is not far away -- it is right here, in our hearts. Jesus reframes the neighbor question: the hero of the story is the outsider everyone despises, not the religious professionals who walked by. Mercy is not a theological concept; it is stopping on the road.

ordinary-time-16-a.logline: Parables continue: wheat and weeds grow together until the harvest -- God's patience means the righteous and the wicked coexist for now.
ordinary-time-16-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Parables section continues with three kingdom parables: weeds among wheat, mustard seed, and yeast. God's patience and the kingdom's hidden growth are the dominant themes.

first.synopsis: There is no god besides you who cares for all. Your might is the source of justice; your mastery makes you lenient. You judge with clemency and govern with lenience. You taught your people that those who are just must be kind, and you gave your children good ground for hope that you would permit repentance for their sins. God's power expresses itself as patience.

second.synopsis: The Spirit comes to the aid of our weakness, because we do not know how to pray as we ought. The Spirit intercedes with inexpressible groanings. The one who searches hearts knows the intention of the Spirit, who intercedes for the holy ones according to God's will. Even our prayer is assisted -- we are never left to figure it out alone.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells three parables. First, the weeds: a man sows good seed, but an enemy sows weeds among the wheat. The servants want to pull the weeds, but the master says no -- you might uproot the wheat too. Let both grow until harvest, when they will be separated. Second, the mustard seed: the smallest seed becomes the largest plant. Third, the yeast: a woman mixes it into flour and the whole batch rises. The kingdom grows hidden, mixed, and patient.

ordinary-time-16-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to practice God's patience in a world we want to sort into clean categories. Wisdom says God is lenient because he is powerful, not despite it. Jesus says wheat and weeds grow together until God sorts them out at the harvest -- and that is not our job. The kingdom works like yeast: invisibly, slowly, permeating everything. We are not called to be reapers; we are called to be patient.

ordinary-time-16-b.logline: Jesus sees a vast crowd 'like sheep without a shepherd' and is moved with pity, fulfilling Jeremiah's promise that God himself would gather the scattered flock.
ordinary-time-16-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Semi-continuous Mark. This sets up the feeding of the five thousand (next week). Jesus' compassion for the shepherdless crowd echoes Jeremiah's prophecy about bad shepherds replaced by a righteous shoot from David.

first.synopsis: God pronounces woe on the shepherds who mislead and scatter his flock. 'You have not cared for them, but I will take care to punish your evil deeds.' God himself will gather the remnant, bring them back, and appoint good shepherds. He will raise a righteous shoot from David who will reign wisely and bring justice. His name: 'The Lord our justice.'

second.synopsis: Christ is our peace. He made both groups (Jew and Gentile) one, breaking down the dividing wall of enmity. Through the cross he reconciled both with God in one body, putting enmity to death. He came and preached peace to those far off and those near, so that through him both have access in one Spirit to the Father.

gospel.synopsis: The apostles return from their mission and report everything to Jesus. He invites them to rest in a deserted place, but the crowds follow on foot and arrive first. When Jesus disembarks and sees the vast crowd, 'his heart was moved with pity for them, for they were like sheep without a shepherd; and he began to teach them many things.' Rest is postponed because need is present.

ordinary-time-16-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see the crowd through Jesus' eyes. Jeremiah condemned the shepherds who scattered the flock and promised God would personally gather them. Jesus looks at the disoriented crowd and responds not with a program but with compassion -- and he begins to teach. Good shepherding starts with seeing the people in front of you and being moved by their need.

ordinary-time-16-c.logline: Abraham welcomes three strangers and receives a promise, while Martha welcomes Jesus but is so busy serving she misses his presence -- Mary chose the better part.
ordinary-time-16-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. Martha and Mary is one of Luke's most distinctive stories, found only in his gospel. Abraham's hospitality to the three visitors at Mamre provides a contrasting model of welcome that leads to encounter.

first.synopsis: The Lord appears to Abraham at the oaks of Mamre. Three men are standing nearby. Abraham rushes to welcome them with extraordinary hospitality: water for their feet, bread, curds, milk, and the best steer, prepared quickly. While they eat, one of the visitors promises, 'This time next year Sarah will have a son.' Abraham's generous welcome of strangers becomes the occasion for God's most intimate promise.

second.synopsis: Paul rejoices in his sufferings for the sake of the church, filling up 'what is lacking in the afflictions of Christ.' He has been given a stewardship: to bring God's word to completion, the mystery hidden for ages but now revealed -- 'Christ in you, the hope for glory.' He proclaims Christ, admonishing and teaching everyone with wisdom.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus enters a village where Martha welcomes him. Her sister Mary sits at his feet listening. Martha, burdened with serving, complains: 'Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me to do all the work? Tell her to help me.' Jesus responds gently: 'Martha, Martha, you are anxious and worried about many things. There is need of only one thing. Mary has chosen the better part and it will not be taken from her.' Service matters, but presence comes first.

ordinary-time-16-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to balance hospitality with presence. Abraham welcomed strangers with lavish service and heard God's promise. Martha welcomed Jesus but got so caught up in the serving that she missed the guest. Mary sat and listened. The 'better part' is not laziness versus work -- it is the priority of receiving Christ's presence before rushing to serve. Sometimes the most important thing is to sit down.

ordinary-time-17-a.logline: Parables conclude: the kingdom is like a treasure in a field, a pearl of great price, a net full of fish -- worth everything you have.
ordinary-time-17-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Parables section continues. Three more parables of the kingdom's value: buried treasure, pearl merchant, and dragnet. Solomon's prayer for wisdom in the first reading captures the same spirit of recognizing what truly matters.

first.synopsis: God appears to Solomon in a dream: 'Ask something of me and I will give it to you.' Solomon does not ask for long life, riches, or his enemies' destruction. He asks for 'an understanding heart to judge your people and to distinguish right from wrong.' God is so pleased that Solomon asked for wisdom instead of wealth or power that he grants it in abundance, along with everything Solomon did not ask for.

second.synopsis: Paul declares that all things work for good for those who love God. Those God foreknew he predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son. Those he predestined he called; those he called he justified; those he justified he glorified. It is a chain of divine action from eternity past to eternity future, with no broken links.

gospel.synopsis: Three rapid-fire parables. The kingdom is like a treasure buried in a field -- a man finds it, hides it, and joyfully sells everything to buy that field. It is like a merchant who finds a pearl of great price and sells all he has to buy it. It is like a net thrown into the sea that collects fish of every kind -- sorted at the end of the age. Jesus asks, 'Do you understand all these things?' The disciples say yes. He replies that a scribe trained for the kingdom brings from his storeroom both the new and the old.

ordinary-time-17-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to ask what we are willing to give up for the thing that matters most. Solomon chose wisdom over wealth and received both. The man who found the treasure and the pearl merchant both sold everything joyfully -- not grimly, but with delight, because what they found was worth infinitely more than what they gave up. The kingdom is not a sacrifice; it is the discovery that makes every other sacrifice make sense.

ordinary-time-17-b.logline: Jesus feeds five thousand people with five barley loaves and two fish, beginning the great Bread of Life discourse that will unfold over the next four weeks.
ordinary-time-17-b.trajectory: Year B: Bread of Life Discourse 1/5. Mark's gospel is interrupted here by five weeks of John 6, beginning with the multiplication of the loaves. This is a MAJOR trajectory that continues through OT 21. Elisha's feeding miracle foreshadows Jesus' sign.

first.synopsis: A man brings Elisha twenty barley loaves from the firstfruits. Elisha says, 'Give it to the people to eat.' His servant protests: 'How can I set this before a hundred people?' But the Lord says they will eat and have some left over -- and they do. A small prophetic feeding that foreshadows the much larger one Jesus will perform.

second.synopsis: Paul urges the Ephesians to live worthily of their calling: with humility, gentleness, patience, bearing with one another in love. He stresses unity: 'One body and one Spirit, one Lord, one faith, one baptism; one God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all.' Seven 'ones' that ground the church's identity.

gospel.synopsis: A large crowd follows Jesus. He asks Philip, 'Where can we buy enough food for them?' Andrew finds a boy with five barley loaves and two fish: 'But what good are these for so many?' Jesus takes the loaves, gives thanks, and distributes them along with the fish. Everyone eats their fill. Twelve baskets of fragments remain. The crowd declares, 'This is truly the Prophet!' But when they try to make him king, Jesus withdraws to the mountain alone. The sign is eucharistic in every detail.

ordinary-time-17-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to bring what little we have and trust Jesus to multiply it. Elisha fed a hundred with twenty loaves; Jesus feeds five thousand with five. The boy's lunch was laughably inadequate, but offered to Jesus, it became more than enough. This is the first week of five in John's Bread of Life discourse. The feeding is not just a miracle -- it is a sign pointing to who Jesus is and what he gives.

ordinary-time-17-c.logline: Abraham boldly bargains with God over Sodom, and Jesus teaches the disciples to pray: 'Ask and you will receive; seek and you will find.'
ordinary-time-17-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Semi-continuous Luke. Jesus teaches the Lord's Prayer (Luke's version) and the parable of the persistent friend. Abraham's audacious negotiation with God over Sodom models the boldness Jesus encourages.

first.synopsis: Abraham learns God plans to destroy Sodom and begins a remarkable negotiation. 'Will you sweep away the innocent with the guilty? What if there are fifty? Forty-five? Forty? Thirty? Twenty? Ten?' Each time God agrees to spare the city. Abraham keeps pushing, apologizing for his boldness -- 'I am but dust and ashes' -- but never backing down. It is a model of audacious, persistent prayer that refuses to let God's mercy stop short.

second.synopsis: Paul tells the Colossians they were buried with Christ in baptism and raised with him through faith. Even when they were dead in their transgressions, God brought them to life, forgiving every transgression and obliterating the legal bond against them by nailing it to the cross. The slate is wiped clean.

gospel.synopsis: A disciple asks Jesus, 'Lord, teach us to pray.' Jesus gives them the Lord's Prayer (Luke's shorter version): Father, hallowed be your name, your kingdom come, give us daily bread, forgive us as we forgive, do not subject us to the final test. Then the parable of the persistent friend who knocks at midnight until he gets what he needs. The teaching: 'Ask and you will receive; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened.' If earthly fathers give good gifts to their children, 'how much more will the Father in heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him?'

ordinary-time-17-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to pray with boldness and persistence, not polite timidity. Abraham bargained with God and was not rebuked for it. Jesus teaches us to ask, seek, and knock -- and to keep at it. The Lord's Prayer is not a formula to recite but a framework for a relationship in which we approach God with the familiarity of children who know their Father wants to give good things. The ultimate gift is the Holy Spirit itself.

ordinary-time-18-a.logline: God feeds abundantly and freely -- Jesus multiplies five loaves for five thousand, and nothing can separate us from his love.
ordinary-time-18-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Feeding of the 5,000 -- the first of a sequence of signs and encounters that will lead to Peter's confession of faith.

first.synopsis: God extends a lavish, no-cost invitation: come to the water, eat without paying, drink wine and milk for free. Why spend money on what doesn't satisfy? The passage pivots from physical nourishment to spiritual covenant -- God will renew the everlasting promises made to David. It's an image of radical abundance offered to anyone willing to listen.

second.synopsis: Paul unleashes one of the Bible's most powerful declarations: nothing -- not hardship, not danger, not death itself, not angels, not any power in the cosmos -- can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus. We don't just survive our trials; we 'conquer overwhelmingly' through the one who loved us. This is Paul at his most defiant and hopeful.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus withdraws after hearing of John the Baptist's death, but the crowds follow him on foot. Moved with pity, he heals their sick. When evening comes and the disciples want to send the crowds away, Jesus says, 'Give them some food yourselves.' From five loaves and two fish, he feeds about five thousand men plus women and children, with twelve baskets left over. The Eucharistic overtones are unmistakable: he took, blessed, broke, and gave.

ordinary-time-18-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to trust that God's generosity doesn't run out. Isaiah's free feast, Paul's unbreakable love, and Jesus's abundant multiplication all point the same direction: God provides more than enough, especially when we think we have nothing to offer. What little we bring -- five loaves, two fish, our small selves -- becomes plenty in his hands.

ordinary-time-18-b.logline: Bread of Life Discourse 2/5: The crowd chases Jesus for bread, but he redirects them toward the bread that endures for eternal life.
ordinary-time-18-b.trajectory: Year B (John 6): Bread of Life Discourse 2/5. After last week's multiplication, the crowd tracks Jesus down. He challenges them to look beyond physical hunger.

first.synopsis: The Israelites in the desert grumble against Moses and Aaron, longing for the food of Egypt. God responds by raining down manna -- bread from heaven -- each morning, and sending quail in the evening. When the people see the flaky substance on the ground, they ask 'What is this?' (manna). Moses tells them: this is the bread the Lord has given you to eat. God feeds his people, but also tests their trust.

second.synopsis: Paul urges the Ephesians to stop living aimlessly as they did before knowing Christ. Put away the old self, corrupted by deceitful desires, and be renewed in the spirit of your minds. Put on the new self, created in God's way -- in righteousness and holiness of truth. It's a call to interior renovation, a complete change of identity.

gospel.synopsis: The crowd finds Jesus across the sea and he confronts their motives: 'You're looking for me because you ate the loaves, not because you saw signs.' He tells them to work for food that endures for eternal life. They invoke Moses and the manna. Jesus corrects them: it wasn't Moses who gave the bread from heaven -- it's the Father who gives the true bread. 'Sir, give us this bread always.' Then the great declaration: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me will never hunger.'

ordinary-time-18-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine what we're really hungry for. The crowd chased Jesus for another free meal, but he pointed them toward something deeper -- bread that satisfies eternally. Like the Israelites with manna, we're asked: will you trust God's provision day by day, even when it looks different from what you expected?

ordinary-time-18-c.logline: Ecclesiastes calls all possessions vanity; Jesus tells of a rich fool who stores up treasure but is not rich in what matters to God.
ordinary-time-18-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The journey narrative continues with Jesus's teaching on material attachment and the foolishness of greed.

first.synopsis: Qoheleth's bleak refrain: 'Vanity of vanities! All things are vanity!' What's the point of laboring with wisdom and skill if you just leave it all to someone who didn't work for it? Your days are sorrow, your nights are restless. This is Ecclesiastes at its most existential -- a bracing counterpoint to our culture of productivity and acquisition.

second.synopsis: Paul says: if you were raised with Christ, seek what is above. Think about heavenly things, not earthly ones. Put to death greed, impurity, and the old self. Put on the new self, continually renewed in the image of its creator. In Christ, all human divisions -- Greek, Jew, slave, free -- dissolve. Christ is all and in all.

gospel.synopsis: Someone asks Jesus to settle an inheritance dispute. Jesus refuses the role of arbitrator and warns against greed: 'One's life does not consist of possessions.' He tells the parable of the rich fool who tears down his barns to build bigger ones, hoarding grain and goods, saying 'Eat, drink, be merry.' But God says, 'You fool, this night your life will be demanded of you.' The moral: store up treasure in what matters to God, not just for yourself.

ordinary-time-18-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to ask what we're building our lives around. Ecclesiastes strips away the illusion that accumulation equals meaning. Jesus's rich fool learned too late that you can't take it with you. The question isn't whether you have enough -- it's whether you're 'rich in what matters to God.'

ordinary-time-19-a.logline: God speaks in the tiny whispering sound, and Peter walks on water until fear overtakes his faith.
ordinary-time-19-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Jesus walks on water and saves Peter -- a pivotal scene of faith, doubt, and the disciples' growing recognition of Jesus.

first.synopsis: At Mount Horeb, Elijah waits for God to pass by. A heavy wind comes -- God is not in the wind. An earthquake -- not in the earthquake. Fire -- not in the fire. Then: a tiny whispering sound. Elijah covers his face with his cloak and goes to the entrance of the cave. God's presence arrives not in spectacular power but in quiet intimacy. One of the most memorable passages in the Hebrew Bible.

second.synopsis: Paul pours out his anguish over his fellow Israelites who have not accepted Christ. He would even wish himself cut off from Christ if it would save them. He catalogs their gifts: the covenants, the law, the promises, the patriarchs, and from them -- according to the flesh -- the Christ. It's a deeply personal passage about Paul's grief and love for his own people.

gospel.synopsis: After feeding the five thousand, Jesus sends the disciples across the sea and goes up the mountain alone to pray. In the early morning, he comes to them walking on the water. They're terrified. Jesus says, 'Take courage, it is I; do not be afraid.' Peter asks to come to him on the water -- and does, until he notices the wind and begins to sink. 'Lord, save me!' Jesus catches him: 'O you of little faith, why did you doubt?' The disciples worship: 'Truly, you are the Son of God.'

ordinary-time-19-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to listen for God in the silence and to keep our eyes on Jesus when the storm rages. Elijah found God not in the earthquake but in the whisper. Peter walked on water until he looked at the waves instead of Jesus. Both stories ask: where is your attention fixed -- on the chaos, or on the One who stands in the midst of it?

ordinary-time-19-b.logline: Bread of Life Discourse 3/5: Elijah is fed for the journey; Jesus declares 'I am the living bread that came down from heaven.'
ordinary-time-19-b.trajectory: Year B (John 6): Bread of Life Discourse 3/5. Jesus intensifies his claim -- the bread from heaven is not manna; it is himself.

first.synopsis: Elijah, exhausted and wanting to die, collapses under a broom tree: 'This is enough, O Lord! Take my life.' An angel wakes him with a hearth cake and water. Elijah eats, sleeps, and is fed again. Strengthened by that food, he walks forty days and forty nights to the mountain of God. God feeds the prophet not with a spectacular miracle but with simple bread -- just enough for the journey.

second.synopsis: Paul gives practical instruction: don't grieve the Holy Spirit. Remove all bitterness, fury, and malice. Be kind, compassionate, forgiving -- as God forgave you in Christ. Be imitators of God. Live in love, as Christ loved us and gave himself as a sacrificial offering, 'a fragrant aroma.' The ethical life flows from the experience of being forgiven.

gospel.synopsis: The crowd murmurs: 'Isn't this Jesus, the son of Joseph? How can he say he came down from heaven?' Jesus responds: stop murmuring. No one comes to me unless drawn by the Father. Everyone taught by God comes to me. 'I am the bread of life. Your ancestors ate manna and died. This is the bread that comes down from heaven so that one may eat it and not die. I am the living bread -- and the bread that I will give is my flesh for the life of the world.' The stakes just got much higher.

ordinary-time-19-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let God feed us for the journey ahead, even when we feel like giving up. Elijah was ready to die, but God didn't argue with his despair -- he fed him. Jesus offers something far greater than manna: his own flesh for the life of the world. Sometimes faith begins not with understanding but with simply eating what God provides.

ordinary-time-19-c.logline: Faith means stepping out without knowing the destination -- Abraham went out 'not knowing where he was to go,' and Jesus tells us to be ready, for the Son of Man comes at an unexpected hour.
ordinary-time-19-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Jesus teaches vigilance and readiness through parables of watchful servants, continuing the journey narrative's theme of radical trust.

first.synopsis: The night of the Passover was known beforehand to Israel's ancestors, giving them courage. They awaited salvation with sure knowledge of God's promises. The holy children offered sacrifice in secret, putting the divine plan into effect with one accord. This brief passage connects trust in God's promises with the courage to act on them.

second.synopsis: The great faith chapter. Faith is 'the realization of what is hoped for and evidence of things not seen.' Abraham obeyed when called to go out to a place he didn't know. He lived as a stranger in the promised land, looking forward to 'the city whose architect and maker is God.' He was even willing to sacrifice Isaac, trusting that God could raise the dead. Faith here isn't a feeling -- it's a life lived in response to promises not yet fulfilled.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells the disciples: 'Do not be afraid, little flock, your Father is pleased to give you the kingdom.' Sell your belongings and give alms. Be like servants awaiting their master's return, ready to open the door the moment he knocks. If the master finds them vigilant, he will seat them at table and serve them. Peter asks, 'Is this meant for us or for everyone?' Jesus answers with the parable of the faithful steward -- much is required of those entrusted with much.

ordinary-time-19-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to live with our lamps lit. Abraham went out not knowing where he was going but trusting the One who called. Jesus asks: will you be found ready when the master returns? This isn't about anxiety -- it's about the freedom of living alert and generous, rather than hoarding and sleeping through what matters.

ordinary-time-20-a.logline: God's house is for all peoples, and a Canaanite woman's persistent faith convinces Jesus to heal her daughter.
ordinary-time-20-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The Canaanite woman -- one of the most provocative encounters in the gospels, expanding the boundaries of who belongs.

first.synopsis: God promises to bring foreigners to his holy mountain and make them joyful in his house of prayer. Their offerings will be acceptable on his altar. The key declaration: 'My house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples.' This is Isaiah's radical vision of inclusion -- salvation is not restricted to ethnic Israel but extends to anyone who keeps covenant with God.

second.synopsis: Paul reflects on the mystery of Israel's relationship to the gospel. As apostle to the Gentiles, he sees even Israel's rejection as serving God's plan -- 'the gifts and the call of God are irrevocable.' God delivered all to disobedience so that he might have mercy upon all. It's a passage about the strange, patient logic of divine mercy.

gospel.synopsis: A Canaanite woman begs Jesus to heal her demon-tormented daughter. Jesus initially ignores her. The disciples want her sent away. Jesus says he was sent only to Israel. She persists: 'Lord, help me.' He says it's not right to give the children's food to the dogs. She fires back: 'Even the dogs eat the scraps from their masters' table.' Jesus is moved: 'O woman, great is your faith!' Her daughter is healed instantly. Her outsider faith surpasses that of the insiders.

ordinary-time-20-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine who we've left outside the circle. Isaiah says God's house is for all peoples. The Canaanite woman -- a foreigner, a woman, an outsider -- displays the kind of bold, persistent faith that Jesus himself praises. God's mercy refuses to stay within the boundaries we draw.

ordinary-time-20-b.logline: Bread of Life Discourse 4/5: Wisdom sets her table; Jesus declares 'my flesh is true food and my blood is true drink' -- the most Eucharistic passage in the gospels.
ordinary-time-20-b.trajectory: Year B (John 6): Bread of Life Discourse 4/5. Jesus pushes the discourse to its most explicit Eucharistic language.

first.synopsis: Lady Wisdom has built her house and set her seven columns. She has prepared her feast -- meat dressed, wine mixed, table spread. She sends out her maidens to call from the heights: 'Let whoever is simple turn in here. Come, eat of my food, and drink of the wine I have mixed! Forsake foolishness that you may live.' Wisdom's banquet is open to all who are willing to learn.

second.synopsis: Paul says: watch how you live -- not as foolish but as wise, making the most of the opportunity. Don't get drunk on wine; be filled with the Spirit instead. Address one another in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, singing and playing to the Lord in your hearts. Give thanks always and for everything. A passage that's especially resonant for music ministers.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus escalates dramatically: 'The bread that I will give is my flesh for the life of the world.' The crowd quarrels: 'How can this man give us his flesh to eat?' Jesus doubles down: 'Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you do not have life within you.' John deliberately shifts from the Greek 'phagein' (eat) to 'trogein' (gnaw, munch) — making the language increasingly physical and uncomfortable. My flesh is true food, my blood is true drink. Whoever feeds on me will have life because of me. This is the most explicitly Eucharistic passage in John's gospel — and the most deliberately provocative.

ordinary-time-20-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to the table -- not as spectators but as participants. Wisdom prepares a feast; Jesus offers his own flesh and blood. The Eucharistic language couldn't be more direct or more challenging. This is the week the Bread of Life discourse gets uncomfortable, and that discomfort is the point.

ordinary-time-20-c.logline: Following Jesus creates division -- Jeremiah is thrown into a cistern for speaking truth, and Jesus says 'I have come to set the earth on fire.'
ordinary-time-20-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): A jarring passage -- Jesus warns that discipleship will divide even families. The cost of faithfulness is starkly named.

first.synopsis: The princes tell King Zedekiah that Jeremiah must die -- his prophecies are demoralizing the soldiers and the people. The weak king hands him over. They throw Jeremiah into a muddy cistern where he sinks. But Ebed-melech, a court official, intervenes: 'These men have done wrong.' The king orders Jeremiah pulled out before he dies. Speaking God's truth can land you in the pit, but God raises up unlikely rescuers.

second.synopsis: Since we're surrounded by a great cloud of witnesses, let us rid ourselves of every burden and sin. Persevere in the race, keeping our eyes fixed on Jesus, who endured the cross for the joy that lay before him. Consider how he endured opposition from sinners, so you won't grow weary. You haven't yet resisted to the point of shedding blood. The Christian life as endurance race.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus shocks the disciples: 'I have come to set the earth on fire, and how I wish it were already blazing!' He speaks of a baptism he must undergo and the anguish of waiting. Then the hard word: 'Do you think I have come to establish peace? No, but rather division.' Households of five will be split three against two. Father against son, mother against daughter. Following Jesus creates ruptures that no one can pretend away.

ordinary-time-20-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to reckon with the cost of discipleship. Jeremiah spoke God's word and ended up in a pit. Jesus warns that his message will divide families. This isn't comfortable, but it's honest: the gospel isn't meant to keep the peace at any price. Sometimes faithfulness creates conflict -- and that's a sign the fire is doing its work.

ordinary-time-21-a.logline: Peter confesses Jesus as the Christ and receives the keys to the kingdom -- the rock on which the Church is built.
ordinary-time-21-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Peter's confession at Caesarea Philippi -- the hinge of Matthew's gospel. Everything before this leads to it; everything after flows from it.

first.synopsis: God removes Shebna from office and installs Eliakim as the new master of the palace. Eliakim will receive the robe, the sash, and the authority. Most importantly, God places the key of the House of David on his shoulder -- 'when he opens, no one shall shut; when he shuts, no one shall open.' He'll be fixed like a peg in a sure spot. This passage directly prefigures Jesus giving Peter the keys.

second.synopsis: Paul breaks into a hymn of wonder: 'Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How inscrutable are his judgments and how unsearchable his ways!' Who has known the mind of the Lord? Who has been his counselor? Everything is from him, through him, and for him. Glory forever. This is theology as doxology -- thinking about God that can only end in praise.

gospel.synopsis: At Caesarea Philippi, Jesus asks: 'Who do people say the Son of Man is?' The disciples report various opinions. Then the real question: 'But who do you say that I am?' Peter answers: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.' Jesus responds: 'Blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah -- flesh and blood has not revealed this to you, but my heavenly Father.' He names Peter the rock, gives him the keys to the kingdom, and the power to bind and loose. Then strictly orders them to tell no one.

ordinary-time-21-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to answer the question Jesus puts directly to each of us: 'Who do you say that I am?' Not what have you heard, not what others believe -- what do you confess? Peter's answer changed everything. The keys are given not to the perfect but to the one willing to speak faith aloud.

ordinary-time-21-b.logline: Bread of Life Discourse 5/5: Many disciples walk away; Peter stays: 'Master, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life.'
ordinary-time-21-b.trajectory: Year B (John 6): Bread of Life Discourse 5/5. The final crisis -- Jesus's teaching is too hard and many leave. Peter's confession anchors those who remain.

first.synopsis: Joshua gathers the tribes at Shechem and presents a choice: serve the gods your ancestors served, or serve the Lord. His own commitment is clear: 'As for me and my household, we will serve the Lord.' The people respond: 'Far be it from us to forsake the Lord -- he brought us out of Egypt, performed great miracles, and protected us.' It's a public act of covenant renewal, a deliberate choosing.

second.synopsis: Paul compares the relationship between husband and wife to that between Christ and the Church. Husbands are to love their wives as Christ loved the Church -- giving himself up for her. The two become one flesh. 'This is a great mystery,' Paul says, 'but I speak in reference to Christ and the Church.' Whatever one makes of the household codes, the core analogy is breathtaking: marital love as icon of Christ's self-giving.

gospel.synopsis: Many disciples find Jesus's bread of life teaching too hard and leave: 'This saying is hard; who can accept it?' Jesus asks, 'Does this shock you?' He says the Spirit gives life; the flesh is of no avail. 'But there are some of you who do not believe.' Many return to their former way of life. Then Jesus turns to the Twelve: 'Do you also want to leave?' Peter answers: 'Master, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life. We have come to believe that you are the Holy One of God.' One of the great confessions of faith in Scripture.

ordinary-time-21-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to decide -- not just once, but again today. Joshua said choose. Many of Jesus's disciples chose to leave. Peter chose to stay, not because he fully understood, but because there was nowhere else to go. Sometimes faith isn't certainty; it's the honest realization that no alternative satisfies the way Jesus does.

ordinary-time-21-c.logline: The gate is narrow and the last will be first -- God gathers people from east and west while the presumptuous are shut out.
ordinary-time-21-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Jesus continues toward Jerusalem, warning that familiarity with him is no guarantee of entry into the kingdom.

first.synopsis: God declares he will gather nations of every language to see his glory. From distant coastlands that have never heard of him, fugitives will proclaim his fame. They will bring all the scattered brothers and sisters from every nation to Jerusalem, God's holy mountain. Some of these foreigners God will even take as priests and Levites. The vision is universal -- God's reach extends to the ends of the earth.

second.synopsis: The author reminds the community that discipline is a sign of love: 'Whom the Lord loves, he disciplines.' All discipline seems painful in the moment but later yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness. So strengthen your drooping hands and weak knees. Make straight paths for your feet. This is spiritual toughness rooted in parental love.

gospel.synopsis: Someone asks Jesus, 'Will only a few people be saved?' He doesn't give a number. Instead: 'Strive to enter through the narrow gate.' After the master locks the door, those outside will knock and say, 'We ate and drank in your company.' He'll answer, 'I do not know where you are from.' Meanwhile, people will come from east, west, north, and south to recline at table in the kingdom. 'Some are last who will be first, and some are first who will be last.'

ordinary-time-21-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us not to take our place at the table for granted. Casual familiarity with Jesus -- 'We ate and drank with you' -- doesn't guarantee anything. The gate is narrow, and outsiders may get in before the insiders. The real question isn't who's saved but whether we're striving to enter.

ordinary-time-22-a.logline: Peter calls Jesus the Christ one week and tries to block the cross the next -- 'Get behind me, Satan. Whoever wishes to save his life will lose it.'
ordinary-time-22-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The first prediction of the Passion. Peter, just named the rock, becomes a stumbling block. The cost of discipleship is laid bare.

first.synopsis: Jeremiah accuses God: 'You duped me, and I let myself be duped; you were too strong for me.' God's word has brought him nothing but mockery. He resolves to stop speaking in God's name -- but then it becomes like fire burning in his heart, imprisoned in his bones. He can't hold it in. One of the rawest, most honest prayers in the Bible: the prophet trapped between God's call and the cost of answering it.

second.synopsis: Paul urges: offer your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God -- this is your spiritual worship. Do not conform to this age but be transformed by the renewal of your mind. Then you'll be able to discern what is good, pleasing, and perfect. Two verses that have shaped Christian ethics ever since: the call to total, embodied dedication.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells the disciples he must go to Jerusalem, suffer, be killed, and rise on the third day. Peter rebukes him: 'God forbid, Lord!' Jesus fires back: 'Get behind me, Satan! You are thinking not as God does, but as human beings do.' Then to all: whoever wishes to come after me must deny himself, take up his cross, and follow me. Whoever wishes to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will find it. What profit is the whole world if you forfeit your life?

ordinary-time-22-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to stop editing the gospel to avoid the cross. Peter wanted a Messiah without suffering. Jesus says that's satanic thinking. Jeremiah tried to stop prophesying and couldn't. The cross isn't optional -- it's the path. And paradoxically, losing your life for Christ's sake is the only way to find it.

ordinary-time-22-b.logline: Jesus confronts the Pharisees: ritual purity without interior conversion is empty -- 'This people honors me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me.'
ordinary-time-22-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): After the Bread of Life discourse, Mark returns. Jesus challenges religious externalism and redefines what truly defiles a person.

first.synopsis: Moses tells Israel: hear the statutes and decrees, observe them, and you will live. Don't add to them or subtract from them. Other nations will see your wisdom and say, 'This great nation is truly a wise and intelligent people.' For what great nation has gods so close as the Lord is to us? The law is a gift, not a burden -- it's meant to make Israel a light to the nations.

second.synopsis: Every good gift comes from the Father of lights, in whom there is no shadow or change. Humbly welcome the word planted in you. But be doers of the word, not hearers only. Pure religion before God is this: care for orphans and widows, and keep yourself unstained by the world. James's definition of religion is strikingly practical.

gospel.synopsis: The Pharisees criticize Jesus's disciples for eating with unwashed hands. Jesus quotes Isaiah: 'This people honors me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me; in vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines human precepts.' Then to the crowd: nothing that enters from outside can defile; it's what comes out from within -- evil thoughts, greed, malice, deceit, arrogance -- that defiles. The heart is where the real action is.

ordinary-time-22-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine whether our religious practice matches our interior life. Jesus doesn't dismiss tradition -- he challenges tradition disconnected from the heart. James says the same: real religion is caring for the vulnerable and keeping your soul honest. Lip service without heart service is empty.

ordinary-time-22-c.logline: Take the lowest place at the table, invite those who cannot repay you, and discover that true honor comes from God, not from social positioning.
ordinary-time-22-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Jesus dines at a Pharisee's house and teaches about humility -- both in seating and in guest lists.

first.synopsis: Conduct your affairs with humility and you'll be loved more than a giver of gifts. The greater you are, the more you should humble yourself. Don't seek things too sublime for you. The wise appreciate proverbs; an attentive ear is the joy of the wise. Water quenches fire; alms atone for sins. Sirach's practical wisdom about the quiet power of humility.

second.synopsis: You haven't approached a mountain of blazing fire and storm like Sinai. You've approached Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem, countless angels in celebration, the assembly of the firstborn enrolled in heaven, and Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant. His sprinkled blood speaks more eloquently than Abel's. The new covenant surpasses the old in intimacy and glory.

gospel.synopsis: At a Pharisee's dinner, Jesus notices guests choosing places of honor and tells a parable: when invited to a banquet, take the lowest place, so the host may say 'move up higher.' Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled; the one who humbles himself will be exalted. Then to the host: don't invite friends and rich neighbors who can repay you. Invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind -- you'll be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.

ordinary-time-22-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to rethink how we seek honor -- and who we invite to our table. Jesus's advice isn't just about manners at dinner parties. It's a vision of the kingdom where social calculation gives way to radical generosity, and the people who can't repay you are exactly the ones God wants you to welcome.

ordinary-time-23-a.logline: Love is the fulfillment of the law, and where two or three are gathered in Jesus's name, he is there -- but reconciliation comes first.
ordinary-time-23-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Jesus teaches about fraternal correction and communal prayer in Matthew's 'church discourse' -- practical instructions for life together.

first.synopsis: God appoints Ezekiel as watchman for Israel. If God tells the wicked they will die and Ezekiel doesn't warn them, their blood is on his hands. But if he warns them and they refuse to turn, they die for their own guilt, and the watchman saves himself. It's a heavy responsibility: speaking truth to those going astray is not optional but obligatory.

second.synopsis: Owe nothing to anyone except to love one another. All the commandments -- don't steal, don't kill, don't covet -- are summed up in one: love your neighbor as yourself. Love does no evil to the neighbor. Therefore love is the fulfillment of the law. Paul distills the entire moral life into a single principle.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus lays out a process for dealing with sin in the community: first go privately; if that fails, bring witnesses; if that fails, tell the church. Whatever you bind on earth is bound in heaven. Then the promise: if two of you agree on earth about anything you pray for, it shall be granted. 'Where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them.' Communal prayer requires communal reconciliation.

ordinary-time-23-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to take responsibility for each other. The watchman must speak. The brother must be approached. Love is the fulfillment of the law, but love sometimes means the hard conversation. And the promise underneath it all: where even two or three gather in his name, Christ is present.

ordinary-time-23-b.logline: Jesus opens the ears of the deaf and loosens the tongue of the mute -- 'Ephphatha!' Be opened!
ordinary-time-23-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Back in Mark's gospel after the Bread of Life weeks. Jesus heals a deaf man with a speech impediment in the Decapolis -- gentile territory.

first.synopsis: Say to those whose hearts are frightened: Be strong, fear not! God comes to save you. Then the blind will see, the deaf will hear, the lame will leap like a stag, the mute will sing. Streams will burst forth in the desert. The burning sands will become pools. Isaiah's vision of the messianic age is a world healed in every dimension -- body, heart, and landscape.

second.synopsis: Show no partiality. If a rich man in fine clothes comes in and you seat him well while telling the poor man to stand in the corner, you've become judges with evil designs. Did not God choose the poor in the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom? James is direct: favoritism based on wealth is incompatible with faith in Christ.

gospel.synopsis: In the Decapolis, people bring Jesus a deaf man with a speech impediment. Jesus takes him aside privately, puts his fingers in the man's ears, touches his tongue, looks up to heaven, groans, and says 'Ephphatha!' -- Be opened! The man's ears open and he speaks plainly. Jesus orders silence, but the more he orders it, the more they proclaim: 'He has done all things well. He makes the deaf hear and the mute speak.'

ordinary-time-23-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to be opened. Isaiah promises a world where the deaf hear and the mute sing. Jesus makes it happen with a groan and a word. The 'Ephphatha' at baptism echoes this moment -- we're all asked to have our ears and lips opened to receive and proclaim the gospel.

ordinary-time-23-c.logline: Count the cost before you follow -- Jesus tells the crowds that discipleship requires renouncing everything, even family ties.
ordinary-time-23-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Jesus draws enormous crowds but refuses to let popularity soften his message. Discipleship demands total commitment.

first.synopsis: Who can know God's counsel or conceive what the Lord intends? Human plans are timid and unsure. The body burdens the soul; we can barely guess what's on earth, let alone what's in heaven. Only through God's wisdom and holy spirit are the paths of those on earth made straight. A meditation on the limits of human understanding and the need for divine guidance.

second.synopsis: Paul writes to Philemon about Onesimus, a runaway slave Paul has converted in prison. He sends Onesimus back -- 'my own heart' -- asking Philemon to receive him no longer as a slave but as a beloved brother, both as a person and in the Lord. 'Welcome him as you would me.' The shortest and most personal of Paul's letters, with enormous implications for human dignity.

gospel.synopsis: Great crowds travel with Jesus and he turns to address them with startling demands: unless you hate father, mother, wife, children, even your own life, you cannot be my disciple. Carry your own cross. He illustrates with two parables: a builder who counts the cost before constructing a tower, and a king who calculates before going to war. The conclusion: anyone who does not renounce all possessions cannot be my disciple. The language of 'hate' means radical prioritization, not emotional hostility.

ordinary-time-23-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to count the cost honestly. Jesus isn't recruiting fans -- he's calling disciples, and he wants them to know the price upfront. Like a builder calculating materials or a king assessing his army, we need to ask: am I willing to put following Jesus above everything else? The honesty is its own kind of respect.

ordinary-time-24-a.logline: Forgive not seven times but seventy-seven times -- the parable of the unforgiving servant shows that those who receive mercy must give it.
ordinary-time-24-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The parable of the unforgiving servant -- Jesus's most extended teaching on forgiveness, concluding the community discourse.

first.synopsis: Wrath and anger are hateful things, yet sinners cling to them. Forgive your neighbor's injustice, and then when you pray, your own sins will be forgiven. How can anyone who holds anger expect healing from the Lord? Remember your last days and set enmity aside. Think of the commandments and overlook faults. Sirach makes the connection explicit: receiving forgiveness and giving forgiveness are inseparable.

second.synopsis: None of us lives for oneself, and no one dies for oneself. If we live, we live for the Lord; if we die, we die for the Lord. Whether we live or die, we are the Lord's. Christ died and came to life so that he might be Lord of both the dead and the living. In just three verses, Paul reframes the entire scope of human existence around belonging to Christ.

gospel.synopsis: Peter asks, 'How often must I forgive? Seven times?' Jesus says seventy-seven times. Then a parable: a king forgives a servant an enormous debt (millions). That servant immediately goes out and chokes a fellow servant who owes him a tiny amount, refusing mercy. When the king hears, he's furious: 'Should you not have had pity, as I had pity on you?' The servant is handed over to the torturers. 'So will my heavenly Father do to you, unless each of you forgives your brother from your heart.'

ordinary-time-24-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to practice the forgiveness we've received. The math is staggering: the king forgave millions; the servant couldn't forgive pennies. Sirach says it plainly -- if you refuse mercy to others, how can you expect it from God? Forgiveness isn't a feeling; it's a decision to extend the same grace that was extended to you.

ordinary-time-24-b.logline: Peter confesses Jesus as the Christ, but Jesus immediately teaches that the Son of Man must suffer -- and so must anyone who follows him.
ordinary-time-24-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Peter's confession at Caesarea Philippi and the first passion prediction. Mark's turning point -- the journey to Jerusalem begins.

first.synopsis: The third Suffering Servant Song: 'I gave my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who plucked my beard.' The servant doesn't rebel or turn back. He sets his face like flint, knowing God is his help and he will not be disgraced. 'Who will prove me wrong?' This servant endures abuse with fierce, grounded trust in God's vindication.

second.synopsis: James's most famous passage: What good is faith without works? If a brother is hungry and you say 'Go in peace, keep warm,' but give nothing -- what good is that? Faith without works is dead. 'Demonstrate your faith to me without works, and I will demonstrate my faith to you from my works.' Faith is shown in action, not just profession.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus asks his disciples, 'Who do people say that I am?' Various answers. 'But who do you say that I am?' Peter: 'You are the Christ.' Then Jesus begins teaching that the Son of Man must suffer greatly, be rejected, killed, and rise after three days. Peter rebukes him. Jesus rebukes Peter: 'Get behind me, Satan. You are thinking as human beings do.' Then to the crowd: whoever wishes to come after me must deny himself, take up his cross, and follow me. Whoever loses his life for the gospel will save it.

ordinary-time-24-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hold together two truths that Peter couldn't reconcile: Jesus is the Christ, and the Christ must suffer. We want the glory without the cross. Jesus says they're inseparable. James agrees -- faith that doesn't cost you anything isn't faith at all. Real discipleship has a cross-shaped footprint.

ordinary-time-24-c.logline: The lost sheep, the lost coin, the prodigal son -- Luke's great triptych of God's relentless, joyful mercy toward sinners.
ordinary-time-24-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The three parables of mercy in Luke 15 -- arguably the most beloved chapter in the gospels. God's joy at finding what was lost.

first.synopsis: While Moses is on the mountain, the people make a golden calf. God tells Moses to step aside so his wrath can consume them. Moses intervenes: 'Why should your wrath blaze against your own people? Remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel and the promises you swore.' And God relented. Moses functions as intercessor, standing between a sinful people and their God, appealing not to their goodness but to God's faithfulness.

second.synopsis: Paul confesses: I was a blasphemer, a persecutor, and arrogant, but I was treated mercifully because I acted out of ignorance. 'Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. Of these I am the foremost.' But God showed patience with Paul as an example for all who would come to believe. To the king of ages, invisible, the only God -- honor and glory forever. Paul as exhibit A of divine mercy.

gospel.synopsis: The Pharisees grumble that Jesus welcomes sinners and eats with them. Jesus responds with three parables. A shepherd leaves ninety-nine sheep to find one lost one and throws a party when he finds it. A woman turns her house upside down looking for one lost coin. Then the prodigal son: the younger son takes his inheritance, squanders it, hits bottom feeding pigs, comes to his senses, and returns. The father sees him from far off, runs to him, embraces him, and throws a feast. The older brother is furious. The father says, 'We had to celebrate -- your brother was dead and has come to life.' Heaven's joy over one sinner's return is the heartbeat of the chapter.

ordinary-time-24-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let ourselves be found. God is not waiting for us to get our act together -- he's actively searching, like a shepherd in the wilderness, like a woman sweeping every corner. And when we return, there's no lecture, no probation period -- just a party. The only ones left out are those, like the older brother, who resent the mercy given to others.

ordinary-time-25-a.logline: The workers hired at the last hour receive the same wage -- God's generosity offends our sense of fairness because his ways are not our ways.
ordinary-time-25-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The parable of the workers in the vineyard -- Jesus's most provocative teaching on grace versus merit.

first.synopsis: Seek the Lord while he may be found. Let the wicked forsake their way and turn to the Lord for mercy, for God is generous in forgiving. 'My thoughts are not your thoughts, nor are your ways my ways.' As high as the heavens are above the earth, so are God's ways above ours. The gap between human logic and divine logic is the whole point.

second.synopsis: Paul is caught between two desires: to depart this life and be with Christ (which is far better) or to remain in the flesh for the benefit of the community. 'For to me, life is Christ, and death is gain.' He doesn't know which to choose. Either way, he asks only that they conduct themselves in a way worthy of the gospel. A remarkably candid passage about mortality and purpose.

gospel.synopsis: A landowner hires workers at dawn, mid-morning, noon, mid-afternoon, and late afternoon. At day's end, he pays everyone the same daily wage, starting with the last hired. Those who worked all day grumble. The owner responds: 'I'm not cheating you. Are you envious because I am generous?' The last will be first and the first will be last. This parable isn't about labor economics -- it's about a God whose generosity shatters our scorekeeping.

ordinary-time-25-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let go of the scorecard. Isaiah warns us: God's ways are not our ways. The vineyard workers who labored all day got exactly what was promised. Their anger comes from comparing, not from injustice. God's generosity to latecomers doesn't diminish what the early workers received. Grace isn't fair -- it's better than fair.

ordinary-time-25-b.logline: The disciples argue about who is greatest; Jesus places a child in their midst and says true greatness is service to the smallest.
ordinary-time-25-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Second passion prediction. The disciples don't understand and are afraid to ask -- then argue about who's the greatest. Jesus redefines greatness.

first.synopsis: The wicked plot against the just one because he's obnoxious to them -- he calls out their sins and claims to be God's son. 'Let us test him with revilement and torture. Let us condemn him to a shameful death; if he's really God's son, God will defend him.' This passage reads like a script for Good Friday, written centuries before -- the righteous one persecuted precisely because of his goodness.

second.synopsis: Where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there's disorder and every foul practice. But wisdom from above is pure, peaceable, gentle, full of mercy. The fruit of righteousness is sown in peace. Where do wars and conflicts among you come from? From your own disordered passions. You covet and cannot obtain; you fight and wage war. You don't have because you don't ask -- or you ask wrongly, just to spend it on your passions.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus privately teaches the disciples that the Son of Man will be handed over, killed, and rise after three days. They don't understand and are afraid to ask. On the road, they argue about who is the greatest. At home, Jesus sits down, calls the Twelve, and says: 'If anyone wishes to be first, he shall be the last of all and the servant of all.' He takes a child, places it in their midst, and says: 'Whoever receives one child such as this in my name, receives me.'

ordinary-time-25-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to notice the gap between what Jesus is saying and what we're thinking about. While he talks about suffering and death, the disciples argue about rank. Jesus responds by placing a child -- someone with zero social status -- at the center. Greatness in his kingdom looks like service to the smallest and least powerful.

ordinary-time-25-c.logline: You cannot serve both God and mammon -- the parable of the shrewd steward challenges us to be as clever about eternal things as we are about money.
ordinary-time-25-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The parable of the dishonest steward -- one of Jesus's strangest and most debated teachings about the use of wealth.

first.synopsis: Amos denounces those who trample the needy and destroy the poor. They can't wait for the sabbath to end so they can cheat customers with rigged scales and sell even the refuse of the wheat. They buy the poor for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals. God swears: 'Never will I forget a thing they have done!' Economic exploitation of the vulnerable provokes God's harshest anger.

second.synopsis: Paul urges prayers for everyone, including kings and those in authority, so that we may lead quiet, tranquil lives. God wills everyone to be saved and come to knowledge of the truth. There is one God and one mediator -- the man Christ Jesus, who gave himself as ransom for all. In every place, people should pray with holy hands raised, without anger or argument.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells of a steward about to be fired for squandering his master's property. Thinking fast, he calls in his master's debtors and reduces their bills to win their favor. The master actually commends the dishonest steward for acting shrewdly. Jesus's point isn't to praise dishonesty but to note that worldly people are more resourceful about their interests than 'children of light' are about eternal things. His conclusion: 'You cannot serve both God and mammon.' Use wealth wisely for eternal purposes; don't let it use you.

ordinary-time-25-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to get as serious about eternal things as we are about money. Amos condemns those who exploit the poor for profit. Jesus's parable of the shrewd steward asks a pointed question: if a dishonest manager can plan so cleverly for his future, why can't we be that strategic about the kingdom? You have to choose: God or mammon.

ordinary-time-26-a.logline: Actions speak louder than words -- the son who said no but then went is closer to the kingdom than the one who said yes and stayed home.
ordinary-time-26-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The parable of the two sons -- Jesus confronts the chief priests with the gap between religious talk and actual obedience.

first.synopsis: God addresses the accusation that his ways are unfair. When a virtuous person turns to sin, they die because of it. When a wicked person turns from sin, they live. It's not about your resume -- it's about which direction you're facing right now. God's justice is dynamic and responsive, always leaving room for conversion.

second.synopsis: Paul includes the great Christ hymn: though he was in the form of God, Jesus did not grasp at equality with God. He emptied himself, taking the form of a slave, becoming obedient to death -- even death on a cross. Therefore God greatly exalted him and gave him the name above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bend and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord. This is the template for Christian life: humility, self-emptying, exaltation by God.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus asks: a man has two sons. He tells both to go work in the vineyard. The first says 'I will not' but changes his mind and goes. The second says 'Yes, sir' but doesn't go. Which did the father's will? Obviously the first. Jesus's punch line: tax collectors and prostitutes are entering the kingdom of God before you, because they believed John the Baptist and changed their lives, while you saw it and still didn't change your minds.

ordinary-time-26-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to match our actions to our words. Religious language without corresponding behavior means nothing. The son who initially refused but then obeyed is closer to the kingdom than the one who said all the right things and did nothing. It's never too late to change your mind and actually go to the vineyard.

ordinary-time-26-b.logline: Don't hoard the Spirit -- Moses wishes all God's people were prophets, and Jesus warns against causing the little ones to stumble.
ordinary-time-26-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Jesus teaches against exclusivism and warns about scandal -- whoever is not against us is for us, but woe to those who cause the little ones to sin.

first.synopsis: God takes some of the spirit on Moses and gives it to seventy elders, who begin to prophesy. Two men, Eldad and Medad, weren't at the tent but prophesied in the camp anyway. Joshua wants them stopped. Moses's response is generous and liberating: 'Are you jealous for my sake? Would that all the people of the Lord were prophets!' The Spirit blows where it wills, and gatekeeping is a temptation, not a virtue.

second.synopsis: James issues a blistering warning to the rich: weep and wail over your impending miseries. Your wealth has rotted, your gold has corroded. The wages you withheld from workers cry out to heaven. You've lived in luxury and fattened your hearts for the day of slaughter. You've condemned and murdered the righteous one. This is prophetic anger in the tradition of Amos -- injustice to workers is an offense against God.

gospel.synopsis: John tells Jesus they tried to stop someone driving out demons in Jesus's name because 'he doesn't follow us.' Jesus says don't stop him: whoever is not against us is for us. Anyone who gives you a cup of water because you belong to Christ won't lose their reward. Then a severe warning: whoever causes a little one to sin would be better off thrown into the sea with a millstone around their neck. If your hand, foot, or eye causes you to sin, cut it off -- better to enter life maimed than whole in Gehenna.

ordinary-time-26-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to be generous with the Spirit and ruthless with our own sin. Moses wished everyone could prophesy. Jesus tells the disciples to stop gatekeeping. But the same Jesus who's generous toward outsiders is fierce about scandal -- causing the vulnerable to stumble is the gravest offense. Guard against that with everything you have.

ordinary-time-26-c.logline: The rich man and Lazarus -- a chasm fixed by indifference in this life becomes permanent in the next.
ordinary-time-26-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The parable of the rich man and Lazarus -- Luke's most vivid teaching on the eternal consequences of ignoring the poor.

first.synopsis: Amos pronounces woe on the complacent who lie on beds of ivory, eat the finest food, drink wine from bowls, and anoint themselves with the best oils -- yet are not troubled by the collapse around them. They will be the first to go into exile. Their wanton revelry will end. Amos's target: luxury that exists in willful blindness to suffering.

second.synopsis: Paul charges Timothy: pursue righteousness, devotion, faith, love, patience, gentleness. Compete well for the faith. Keep the commandment without stain until the appearance of the Lord Jesus Christ -- the blessed and only ruler, King of kings and Lord of lords, who dwells in unapproachable light. A soaring passage that moves from practical virtue to cosmic praise.

gospel.synopsis: A rich man dresses in purple and dines sumptuously every day. At his door lies Lazarus, covered with sores, longing for scraps. Both die. Lazarus goes to Abraham's bosom; the rich man goes to torment. The rich man begs Abraham to send Lazarus with water, then to warn his brothers. Abraham says they have Moses and the prophets. 'If they won't listen to them, they won't be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead.' The sin isn't wealth itself -- it's stepping over the man at the gate every single day.

ordinary-time-26-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to see who is lying at our gate. The rich man's sin wasn't anything dramatic -- he just didn't notice Lazarus. Amos names the same blindness: luxury that ignores collapse. The chasm between them after death was created by the chasm the rich man maintained in life. We are invited to close that gap while we still can.

ordinary-time-27-a.logline: God planted a vineyard and expected good fruit; when the tenants killed his son, the vineyard was given to others who would produce.
ordinary-time-27-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The parable of the wicked tenants -- one of Jesus's final confrontations with the religious leaders before his passion.

first.synopsis: Isaiah sings a love song about a vineyard. A friend planted the choicest vines on a fertile hillside, built a watchtower, hewed a wine press. But the vineyard yielded only wild grapes. So the owner will tear it down, let it be trampled. The reveal: the vineyard of the Lord is the house of Israel. He looked for justice but found bloodshed, for righteousness but heard cries of distress.

second.synopsis: Have no anxiety about anything. In everything, by prayer and petition with thanksgiving, make your requests known to God. Then the peace of God that surpasses all understanding will guard your hearts. Finally: whatever is true, honorable, just, pure, lovely, gracious -- think about these things. Paul's prescription for peace is both prayerful and practical -- a discipline of attention.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells the chief priests: a landowner planted a vineyard, leased it to tenants, and went away. At harvest, the tenants beat, killed, and stoned his servants. Finally he sent his son, thinking they'd respect him. They killed the son too. Jesus asks: what will the owner do? They answer: put those wretches to death and lease the vineyard to tenants who will produce. Jesus: 'The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. The kingdom of God will be taken from you and given to a people that will produce its fruit.'

ordinary-time-27-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to ask what fruit we're producing. God has given us everything -- the vineyard, the watchtower, the wine press. The question is whether we're tending it for his purposes or for our own. The parable's warning is clear: stewardship that refuses the owner's claim will eventually be taken away and given to those who will bear fruit.

ordinary-time-27-b.logline: From the beginning, God made them male and female -- Jesus teaches on the permanence of marriage and blesses the children the disciples try to turn away.
ordinary-time-27-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Jesus teaches on marriage and divorce, then welcomes children -- two scenes about vulnerability, fidelity, and receiving the kingdom like a child.

first.synopsis: God says it's not good for the man to be alone and sets out to make a suitable partner. No animal fits. So God casts a deep sleep on the man, takes a rib, and builds it into a woman. The man exclaims: 'This one, at last, is bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh!' That is why a man leaves father and mother and clings to his wife, and the two become one flesh. The foundational text on human partnership.

second.synopsis: Jesus was made lower than the angels for a little while so that by God's grace he might taste death for everyone. It was fitting that God should make the leader of salvation perfect through suffering. He who consecrates and those being consecrated all have one origin. He's not ashamed to call them brothers. Christ's solidarity with humanity is total.

gospel.synopsis: Pharisees test Jesus on divorce. He asks what Moses commanded. They cite the permission to write a bill of divorce. Jesus says that was because of hardness of heart -- but from the beginning, God made them male and female, and the two become one flesh. 'What God has joined together, no human being must separate.' Then people bring children to Jesus; the disciples rebuke them. Jesus is indignant: 'Let the children come to me. Whoever does not accept the kingdom of God like a child will not enter it.' He embraces and blesses them.

ordinary-time-27-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to receive the kingdom with the trust of a child and the fidelity of a covenant. Jesus points back to creation's original design for marriage and forward to the humility needed for the kingdom. Both scenes ask the same question: can you be vulnerable enough -- with a spouse, with God -- to give yourself without holding back?

ordinary-time-27-c.logline: Faith the size of a mustard seed can uproot a tree -- but faithful servants don't expect applause for doing what they were obliged to do.
ordinary-time-27-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The apostles ask for more faith; Jesus says even a little faith is powerful, then teaches that servants shouldn't expect special credit for doing their duty.

first.synopsis: Habakkuk cries out: 'How long, O Lord? I cry for help but you do not listen!' Violence and destruction surround him.
