God answers: write down the vision, because it presses on to fulfillment and will not disappoint. If it delays, wait for it. 'The just one, because of his faith, shall live.' This dialogue between the frustrated prophet and the patient God is a masterclass in honest prayer.

second.synopsis: Paul reminds Timothy to stir into flame the gift of God received through the laying on of hands. God gave us a spirit not of cowardice but of power, love, and self-control. Don't be ashamed of your testimony to the Lord. Bear your share of hardship for the gospel. Guard the rich trust with the help of the Holy Spirit. A mentor's urgent encouragement to a younger leader.

gospel.synopsis: The apostles say, 'Increase our faith.' Jesus answers: if you had faith the size of a mustard seed, you could tell a mulberry tree to uproot itself and plant itself in the sea, and it would obey. Then an unexpected parable: when a servant comes in from plowing, does the master say 'Come sit down'? No -- he says 'Prepare my supper.' The servant doesn't get thanked for doing what was commanded. 'So should it be with you. When you have done all you have been commanded, say: We are unprofitable servants; we have done what we were obliged to do.'

ordinary-time-27-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to stop measuring our faith and start using it. Even mustard-seed faith can do impossible things. But Jesus also deflates spiritual ego: don't expect a trophy for doing what you were supposed to do. Habakkuk waited; Timothy was told to endure. Faithfulness isn't heroic -- it's just doing what you were made for.

ordinary-time-28-a.logline: God prepares a feast of rich food and wipes away every tear -- but when the king holds a wedding banquet, some invited guests refuse and one shows up without a garment.
ordinary-time-28-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The parable of the wedding feast -- God's invitation is universal, but acceptance requires transformation.

first.synopsis: On this mountain, the Lord will provide for all peoples a feast of rich food and choice wines. He will destroy the veil over all nations and death itself forever. God will wipe away the tears from every face. 'Behold our God, to whom we looked to save us! Let us rejoice and be glad that he has saved us!' One of the most beautiful eschatological visions in all of Scripture.

second.synopsis: Paul has learned the secret of contentment in every circumstance -- plenty or hunger, abundance or need. 'I can do all things in him who strengthens me.' God will fully supply whatever you need, according to his glorious riches in Christ Jesus. This passage isn't about self-help; it's about finding sufficiency in Christ alone, whatever the external conditions.

gospel.synopsis: A king gives a wedding feast for his son. The invited guests refuse to come -- some go to their farms, others mistreat and kill the servants. The king destroys them and sends servants into the streets to invite everyone they find, bad and good alike. The hall fills. But the king spots a man without a wedding garment and has him thrown out. 'Many are invited, but few are chosen.' The invitation is wide open, but showing up unchanged is not enough.

ordinary-time-28-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to RSVP to God's feast -- and to dress for the occasion. The invitation goes out to everyone, but acceptance means transformation. The man without the wedding garment represents showing up without letting the invitation change you. God's generosity is extravagant, but it's not indifferent to our response.

ordinary-time-28-b.logline: A rich man walks away sad because he has many possessions -- Jesus says it's easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for the rich to enter the kingdom.
ordinary-time-28-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): The rich young man and the camel/needle saying. The journey to Jerusalem deepens the question of what we must give up to follow.

first.synopsis: The author prayed for prudence and the spirit of wisdom came. He preferred wisdom to scepter and throne, deemed riches nothing in comparison. All gold beside her is a little sand; silver is mire. He loved her beyond health and beauty, because her splendor never yields to sleep. All good things came to him in her company. Wisdom as the true treasure -- everything else pales.

second.synopsis: The word of God is living and effective, sharper than any two-edged sword, penetrating between soul and spirit, joints and marrow, able to discern reflections and thoughts of the heart. No creature is concealed from him -- everything is naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must render an account. Two verses that capture the total transparency we have before God.

gospel.synopsis: A man kneels before Jesus: 'What must I do to inherit eternal life?' He has kept all the commandments since his youth. Jesus looks at him with love and says: 'You are lacking one thing. Go, sell what you have, give to the poor, and come follow me.' The man's face falls and he walks away sad -- he had many possessions. Jesus tells the astonished disciples: 'It is easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for one who is rich to enter the kingdom of God.' They ask: 'Then who can be saved?' 'For human beings it is impossible, but not for God.'

ordinary-time-28-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to name what we can't let go of. The rich man kept every commandment but couldn't part with his wealth. Jesus looked at him with love -- not judgment -- and named the one thing standing between him and freedom. The question for each of us: what is our 'one thing'? And are we willing to hear Jesus name it?

ordinary-time-28-c.logline: Ten lepers are healed, but only one returns to give thanks -- and he's the outsider, the Samaritan.
ordinary-time-28-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The healing of the ten lepers -- a story about gratitude, faith, and the surprising identity of the one who comes back.

first.synopsis: Naaman the Syrian general plunges into the Jordan seven times at Elisha's word and is healed of leprosy. His flesh becomes like a child's. He returns to Elisha and declares: 'Now I know there is no God in all the earth except in Israel.' He asks for two mule-loads of Israelite soil so he can worship the Lord on it back home. A foreigner recognizes what insiders miss.

second.synopsis: Paul writes from chains: remember Jesus Christ, raised from the dead. The word of God is not chained, even if Paul is. A faithful saying: if we have died with him, we shall live with him; if we persevere, we shall reign with him. If we deny him, he will deny us. But if we are unfaithful, he remains faithful -- for he cannot deny himself. God's fidelity outlasts our failures.

gospel.synopsis: On the way to Jerusalem, ten lepers call out from a distance: 'Jesus, Master, have pity on us!' He tells them to show themselves to the priests. As they go, they are cleansed. One of them, seeing he's healed, returns glorifying God and falls at Jesus's feet in thanks. He was a Samaritan. Jesus asks: 'Were not ten cleansed? Where are the other nine? Has no one returned to give thanks except this foreigner?' Then to him: 'Stand up and go; your faith has saved you.' Gratitude completes the healing.

ordinary-time-28-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to come back and say thank you. All ten were healed, but only one returned -- and he was the outsider, the Samaritan. Naaman was an outsider too. Both stories suggest that those on the margins sometimes recognize God's gifts more clearly than those who take them for granted. Gratitude is not automatic; it's a choice that deepens faith.

ordinary-time-29-a.logline: Repay to Caesar what belongs to Caesar, and to God what belongs to God -- Jesus refuses to be trapped and reframes the whole question.
ordinary-time-29-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The question about paying taxes to Caesar -- Jesus's masterful response exposes the real issue: everything bears God's image.

first.synopsis: God addresses Cyrus, the pagan Persian king, as his 'anointed' -- the only non-Israelite to receive that title. God grasps Cyrus's right hand, calls him by name, gives him a title, though Cyrus doesn't know God. Why? So that people from east to west may know there is no God besides the Lord. God works through unlikely instruments, even foreign kings.

second.synopsis: Paul opens his letter to the Thessalonians with thanksgiving, remembering their work of faith, labor of love, and endurance in hope. He knows they are loved by God and chosen, because the gospel came to them not in word alone but in power, the Holy Spirit, and much conviction. The first letter Paul ever wrote -- joy and affection from the very first line.

gospel.synopsis: The Pharisees and Herodians try to trap Jesus: 'Is it lawful to pay the census tax to Caesar?' If he says yes, he's a collaborator. If no, he's a rebel. Jesus asks for the coin: 'Whose image is this?' Caesar's. 'Then repay to Caesar what belongs to Caesar, and to God what belongs to God.' The answer is devastatingly clever: Caesar can have his coin back -- but everything that bears God's image (which is everything) belongs to God.

ordinary-time-29-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to recognize whose image we bear. The coin had Caesar's image; we bear God's. Jesus doesn't separate the sacred from the secular -- he subordinates it. Yes, fulfill your civic obligations, but never forget: you belong to God, and that claim supersedes every other. The real question isn't about taxes; it's about allegiance.

ordinary-time-29-b.logline: James and John ask for glory; Jesus offers them the cup of suffering -- 'The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve and to give his life as a ransom for many.'
ordinary-time-29-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Third and final passion prediction. The disciples still don't get it. Jesus redefines leadership as service and sacrifice.

first.synopsis: The fourth Suffering Servant Song reaches its conclusion: the Lord was pleased to crush him in infirmity. If he gives his life as an offering for sin, he shall see his descendants in a long life. Through his suffering, the servant shall justify many and bear their guilt. Redemptive suffering -- the innocent one bearing the cost for others. Christians read this as a direct prophecy of Jesus on the cross.

second.synopsis: We have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens -- Jesus, the Son of God. He's not unable to sympathize with our weaknesses; he was tested in every way, yet without sin. So let us confidently approach the throne of grace to receive mercy and find grace for timely help. Jesus's priesthood is grounded in his experience of human struggle.

gospel.synopsis: James and John ask Jesus to let them sit at his right and left in glory. Jesus: 'You don't know what you're asking. Can you drink the cup I drink?' They say they can. Jesus says they will -- but the seats aren't his to give. The other ten are indignant (they wanted those seats too). Jesus gathers them: 'Whoever wishes to be great among you will be your servant. The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.' The definitive statement on Christian leadership.

ordinary-time-29-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to redefine what we mean by greatness. James and John wanted thrones; Jesus offered them a cup of suffering. The Suffering Servant gives his life so others can live. Christian authority isn't a position to occupy but a service to render. 'The Son of Man came to serve' -- and so must anyone who follows him.

ordinary-time-29-c.logline: The persistent widow won't stop demanding justice -- Jesus tells us to pray always and never lose heart.
ordinary-time-29-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The parable of the persistent widow and the unjust judge -- Jesus's most vivid teaching on relentless prayer.

first.synopsis: Amalek attacks Israel. Moses sends Joshua to fight while he stands on the hilltop with the staff of God raised. As long as Moses holds up his hands, Israel prevails; when he drops them, Amalek gains ground. When Moses's arms grow tired, Aaron and Hur put a rock under him and support his hands until sunset. Joshua wins the battle. Perseverance -- and community support -- make the difference.

second.synopsis: Paul charges Timothy: remain faithful to what you've learned. All Scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching. Proclaim the word -- be persistent whether it's convenient or inconvenient. Convince, reprimand, encourage through all patience and teaching. The charge of a dying apostle to the next generation: don't stop preaching, no matter the circumstances.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells a parable 'about the necessity for them to pray always without becoming weary.' A widow keeps coming to a corrupt judge demanding justice. He neither fears God nor respects anyone, but eventually gives in: 'Because this widow keeps bothering me, I shall deliver a just decision for her.' Jesus's argument runs from lesser to greater: if even a dishonest judge responds to persistence, how much more will God secure justice for his chosen ones who call out day and night? The question that lingers: 'When the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on earth?'

ordinary-time-29-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to keep praying, especially when it feels pointless. The widow's power isn't eloquence -- it's persistence. Moses needed Aaron and Hur to hold up his arms. Paul tells Timothy to keep preaching in season and out. Prayer isn't a single transaction; it's a sustained posture of trust. Jesus's final question -- 'will he find faith on earth?' -- is aimed at us.

ordinary-time-30-a.logline: Love God with all your heart and love your neighbor as yourself -- Jesus names the two commandments on which everything else hangs.
ordinary-time-30-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): The Great Commandment -- Jesus distills the entire law and prophets into two inseparable commands of love.

first.synopsis: God's law protects the vulnerable: do not oppress aliens, for you were once aliens in Egypt. Do not wrong widows or orphans -- if they cry out to God, he will hear them, and his wrath will flare up. If you take your neighbor's cloak as a pledge, return it by sunset -- it's his only covering. 'If he cries out to me, I will hear him, for I am compassionate.' The law of Israel is rooted in memory and mercy.

second.synopsis: Paul reminds the Thessalonians how they became imitators of the Lord, receiving the word in great affliction with joy from the Holy Spirit. They became a model for all believers, turning from idols to serve the living God and to await his Son from heaven -- Jesus, who delivers us from the coming wrath. A young church on fire with conversion.

gospel.synopsis: A scholar of the law tests Jesus: which commandment is the greatest? Jesus answers: love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, and mind -- that's the first and greatest. The second is like it: love your neighbor as yourself. 'The whole law and the prophets depend on these two commandments.' Jesus doesn't choose between the two; he welds them together. You can't have one without the other.

ordinary-time-30-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to simplify. All the complexity of moral and religious life reduces to two commands: love God totally, love your neighbor as yourself. But 'simplify' doesn't mean 'make easy.' Exodus shows that loving your neighbor means protecting aliens, widows, and the poor. The simplicity of the command is matched by the totality of its demand.

ordinary-time-30-b.logline: Blind Bartimaeus throws off his cloak and cries out 'Master, I want to see' -- the last healing before Jerusalem, a model of bold faith.
ordinary-time-30-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): The healing of Bartimaeus -- the final scene on Mark's journey to Jerusalem. A blind beggar becomes a model disciple.

first.synopsis: Shout with joy for Jacob! God will bring his people back from the north, gathering them from the ends of the world -- the blind and the lame, mothers with child, an immense throng. They departed in tears, but God will console them and lead them to brooks of water on a level road. 'For I am a father to Israel.' A vision of restoration where the most vulnerable are not left behind.

second.synopsis: Every high priest is taken from among mortals and represents them before God. He can deal patiently with the ignorant because he himself is beset by weakness. No one takes this honor on himself -- he's called by God, just as Aaron was. Christ didn't glorify himself in becoming high priest but was appointed by the Father: 'You are a priest forever according to the order of Melchizedek.' Christ's priesthood is both humble and eternal.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus is leaving Jericho when blind Bartimaeus cries out: 'Jesus, son of David, have pity on me!' People tell him to be quiet, but he shouts louder. Jesus stops: 'Call him.' Bartimaeus throws off his cloak, springs up, and comes to Jesus. 'What do you want me to do for you?' 'Master, I want to see.' Jesus: 'Go your way; your faith has saved you.' Immediately he receives his sight and follows Jesus on the way -- toward Jerusalem and the cross. The model disciple: cry out, throw off everything, follow.

ordinary-time-30-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to name what we need from Jesus and to throw off whatever holds us back. Bartimaeus didn't let the crowd silence him. He threw away his cloak -- his most valuable possession -- to get to Jesus. When asked what he wanted, he didn't hedge: 'I want to see.' Sometimes the bravest prayer is the most direct one.

ordinary-time-30-c.logline: The Pharisee congratulates himself while the tax collector beats his breast -- 'O God, be merciful to me a sinner.' Guess which one goes home justified.
ordinary-time-30-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The parable of the Pharisee and the tax collector -- Jesus contrasts self-righteous prayer with the prayer of genuine humility.

first.synopsis: The Lord is a God of justice who knows no favorites. Though not partial toward the weak, he hears the cry of the oppressed. He's not deaf to the wail of the orphan or the widow's complaint. The prayer of the lowly pierces the clouds and does not rest till it reaches its goal. God will not delay in responding. The lowly have a direct line to God's attention.

second.synopsis: Paul's farewell: 'I am already being poured out like a libation. I have competed well; I have finished the race; I have kept the faith.' The crown of righteousness awaits him. At his first defense, everyone deserted him, but the Lord stood by him and gave him strength. The Lord will rescue him from every evil threat and bring him safe to his heavenly kingdom. A dying man's extraordinary confidence.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus addresses those convinced of their own righteousness. Two men go to the temple to pray. The Pharisee thanks God that he's not like the rest of humanity -- greedy, dishonest, adulterous, or like that tax collector. He fasts twice a week and pays tithes on everything. The tax collector stands far off, won't even raise his eyes, beats his breast: 'O God, be merciful to me a sinner.' Jesus says the tax collector went home justified, not the Pharisee. 'Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled; the one who humbles himself will be exalted.'

ordinary-time-30-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine how we pray -- and why. The Pharisee's prayer is really a self-congratulatory monologue addressed to God. The tax collector's prayer is seven honest words. Sirach says the prayer of the lowly pierces the clouds. Paul, at the end of his life, trusts not in his achievements but in God's faithfulness. The only prayer that opens heaven's door is the one that knows it doesn't deserve to.

ordinary-time-31-a.logline: The scribes and Pharisees preach but don't practice -- they pile burdens on others and love places of honor while neglecting what matters.
ordinary-time-31-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Jesus's public denunciation of religious hypocrisy -- 'Do and observe all things they tell you, but do not follow their example.'

first.synopsis: God confronts the priests: you have turned aside from the way and caused many to falter by your instruction. You have made void the covenant of Levi. God has made them contemptible because they don't keep his ways and show partiality. Then the piercing question: 'Have we not all one Father? Has not one God created us? Why then do we break faith with one another?' Failed religious leadership betrays the whole community.

second.synopsis: Paul recalls how he and his companions were gentle among the Thessalonians, like a nursing mother caring for her children. They shared not just the gospel but their very selves. They worked night and day so as not to burden anyone. And the community received Paul's word not as a human message but as the word of God, which is at work in those who believe. Ministry as self-giving love.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus tells the crowds: the scribes and Pharisees sit on the chair of Moses. Do what they say, but don't follow their example -- they preach but don't practice. They pile heavy burdens on people's shoulders without lifting a finger to help. Everything they do is for show. They love places of honor, titles, and being called 'Rabbi.' Jesus says: don't be called Rabbi or Master -- you have one Teacher, one Father, one Master. The greatest among you must be your servant. Whoever exalts himself will be humbled.

ordinary-time-31-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to close the gap between what we say and what we do. Jesus doesn't dismiss the teaching of the scribes -- he condemns the disconnect between their words and their lives. Malachi says the same to the priests. The antidote isn't less teaching; it's more integrity. Greatness in the kingdom is measured by service, not by titles or seating charts.

ordinary-time-31-b.logline: Love the Lord your God with all your heart, and your neighbor as yourself -- a scribe gets it right and Jesus says 'You are not far from the kingdom of God.'
ordinary-time-31-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): The Great Commandment in Mark -- a rare moment of agreement between Jesus and a scribe, placing love above all sacrifice.

first.synopsis: Moses speaks to the people: fear the Lord your God and keep his commandments all the days of your life. 'Hear, O Israel! The Lord is our God, the Lord alone!' Love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, and strength. Take these words to heart. The Shema -- the foundational prayer of Israel, recited daily. Love of God is the bedrock of everything.

second.synopsis: The levitical priests were many because death prevented them from continuing in office. But Jesus remains forever and has a priesthood that doesn't pass away. He is always able to save those who approach God through him, since he lives forever to intercede for them. The perfect high priest: holy, innocent, undefiled. He offered himself once for all. His priesthood is permanent.

gospel.synopsis: A scribe asks Jesus which commandment is the first of all. Jesus quotes the Shema: love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength. The second: love your neighbor as yourself. No commandment is greater. The scribe agrees and adds: this is worth more than all burnt offerings and sacrifices. Jesus sees that he answered with understanding and says, 'You are not far from the kingdom of God.' No one dared ask him any more questions. A rare moment of mutual recognition.

ordinary-time-31-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to center everything on love -- love of God and love of neighbor. The scribe understood that these two commandments matter more than all the rituals in the world. Jesus affirmed him: 'You are not far from the kingdom.' The question for us: do we know this intellectually, or do we live it? The gap between 'not far' and 'inside' is the distance between knowing and doing.

ordinary-time-31-c.logline: Zacchaeus climbs a tree to see Jesus, and salvation comes to his house -- 'The Son of Man has come to seek and to save what was lost.'
ordinary-time-31-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): Zacchaeus -- the last encounter before Jerusalem. A short, corrupt tax collector discovers that Jesus sees him and everything changes.

first.synopsis: The whole universe before God is like a grain from a balance or a drop of morning dew. But God has mercy on all because he can do all things. He overlooks people's sins so they may repent. He loves all things that exist and loathes nothing he has made -- for he would not have created it if he hated it. He corrects little by little, reminding people where they sin, so they may be freed from wickedness and trust in him.

second.synopsis: Paul prays that God may make the Thessalonians worthy of his calling and powerfully bring to fulfillment every good purpose and work of faith. He urges them not to be shaken or alarmed by claims that the day of the Lord has already come, whether by spirit, spoken word, or letter allegedly from Paul. Steady on -- don't be rattled by false reports about the end times.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus enters Jericho. Zacchaeus, a chief tax collector and wealthy man, can't see Jesus because he's too short, so he climbs a sycamore tree. Jesus looks up and says, 'Zacchaeus, come down quickly, for today I must stay at your house.' Zacchaeus receives him with joy. The crowd grumbles -- he's a sinner. But Zacchaeus stands and declares: half my possessions I give to the poor, and if I've extorted anyone, I'll repay four times over. Jesus: 'Today salvation has come to this house. The Son of Man has come to seek and to save what was lost.'

ordinary-time-31-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to let ourselves be seen by Jesus -- even in our most compromised state. Zacchaeus was curious, not pious. Jesus didn't wait for him to clean up his life; he invited himself to dinner. The encounter with Jesus produced immediate, concrete generosity. Salvation isn't an abstract concept -- it's what happens when Jesus enters your house and you respond.

ordinary-time-32-a.logline: The ten virgins wait for the bridegroom -- five are ready with oil, five are not. 'Stay awake, for you know neither the day nor the hour.'
ordinary-time-32-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Eschatological parables begin. The parable of the ten virgins -- the liturgical year tilts toward watchfulness and the end times.

first.synopsis: Wisdom is resplendent and unfading, readily perceived by those who love her, found by those who seek her. She hastens to make herself known before you even ask. Whoever watches for her at dawn won't be disappointed -- she'll be sitting by his gate. She makes her own rounds, seeking those worthy of her. Wisdom isn't passive; she's looking for us even as we look for her.

second.synopsis: Paul reassures the Thessalonians about those who have died: we do not want you to grieve like those who have no hope. If Jesus died and rose, God will bring with him those who have fallen asleep. The Lord himself will come down from heaven with a word of command, and the dead in Christ will rise first. Then those alive will be caught up with them to meet the Lord in the air. 'Console one another with these words.' Early Christian hope about death and resurrection.

gospel.synopsis: Ten virgins take their lamps and go out to meet the bridegroom. Five are wise and bring extra oil; five are foolish and bring none. The bridegroom is delayed; they all fall asleep. At midnight: 'Behold, the bridegroom! Come out to meet him!' The foolish ask to borrow oil, but the wise say there won't be enough. While the foolish go to buy oil, the bridegroom arrives. Those who are ready enter the feast; the door is locked. The latecomers knock: 'Lord, Lord, open the door!' He says: 'I do not know you.' The message: stay awake, be prepared.

ordinary-time-32-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to prepare now for what we can't schedule. The bridegroom's delay is the point -- you can't borrow readiness at the last minute. Wisdom makes her rounds seeking us; the question is whether we're paying attention. As the liturgical year nears its end, these readings push us toward vigilance: the life of faith isn't a sprint but a long, sustained watch.

ordinary-time-32-b.logline: Two widows give everything they have -- Elijah's widow shares her last meal, and the poor widow puts her last two coins into the treasury.
ordinary-time-32-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): The widow's mite -- Jesus's last teaching in the temple before the eschatological discourse. Total self-giving contrasted with showy religion.

first.synopsis: During a severe drought, Elijah meets a widow at Zarephath gathering sticks for her last meal. She has only a handful of flour and a little oil -- enough for one cake for herself and her son, and then death. Elijah asks her to make him a cake first, promising God will provide. She does it. The jar of flour never empties, the jug of oil never runs dry. She risked everything on the word of God and was sustained.

second.synopsis: Christ didn't enter a man-made sanctuary but heaven itself, to appear before God on our behalf. He doesn't need to offer himself repeatedly like the high priest entering the sanctuary yearly. He appeared once at the end of the ages to take away sin by his sacrifice. He was offered once to take away the sins of many and will appear a second time -- not to deal with sin but to bring salvation to those who eagerly await him.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus warns: beware of scribes who parade in long robes, love front seats, and devour widows' houses under cover of lengthy prayers. Then he sits opposite the treasury and watches. Many rich people put in large sums. A poor widow puts in two small coins — everything she had, her whole livelihood. Jesus calls his disciples: 'This poor widow put in more than all the others. They gave from their surplus; she gave from her poverty — all she had to live on.' Note the interpretive tension: is Jesus praising the widow's extraordinary faith, or lamenting that a temple system run by scribes who 'devour widows' houses' has just consumed her last coins? Both readings are present in the text, and the juxtaposition is deliberate.

ordinary-time-32-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to examine the difference between giving from surplus and giving from need. Two widows -- centuries apart -- risk everything on trust in God. The scribes give from their abundance to be noticed. The widow gives her last two coins and probably goes unnoticed by everyone except Jesus. God measures giving not by the amount but by the cost.

ordinary-time-32-c.logline: The Maccabean martyrs die rather than abandon God's law; Jesus teaches that God is not God of the dead but of the living.
ordinary-time-32-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The liturgical year nears its end with resurrection hope. The Sadducees try to trap Jesus, and he reveals that God's relationship with us survives death.

first.synopsis: Seven brothers and their mother are arrested and tortured to force them to eat pork against God's law. One by one they refuse, choosing death over betrayal of their faith. 'The King of the world will raise us up to live again forever.' Another says, 'It was from Heaven that I received these hands; from him I hope to receive them again.' Their courage comes from faith in resurrection -- a belief still developing in Judaism. They die trusting that death is not the end.

second.synopsis: Paul prays that God may encourage the community's hearts and strengthen them in every good deed and word. He asks for their prayers too -- that the word of the Lord may spread and that they be delivered from wicked people. But the Lord is faithful and will strengthen and guard them from the evil one. May the Lord direct your hearts to the love of God and the endurance of Christ.

gospel.synopsis: The Sadducees, who deny resurrection, pose a trick question: if a woman marries seven brothers, whose wife is she in the resurrection? Jesus says they misunderstand: in the age to come, people neither marry nor die -- they are like angels, children of God. Then the decisive argument: even Moses called the Lord 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.' He is not God of the dead, but of the living, for to him all are alive. Relationship with God doesn't end at death.

ordinary-time-32-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to ground our hope in the God of the living. The Maccabean martyrs faced death with extraordinary courage because they believed God could raise them up. Jesus teaches that God's relationship with us survives death itself -- 'to him all are alive.' As the liturgical year draws to a close, resurrection hope is the foundation under everything.

ordinary-time-33-a.logline: The parable of the talents: what you've been given is meant to be used, multiplied, and risked -- burying your gift out of fear is the one unacceptable response.
ordinary-time-33-a.trajectory: Year A (Matthew): Eschatological parables continue. The parable of the talents -- the penultimate Sunday before Christ the King. Faithfulness means risk, not safety.

first.synopsis: The woman of valor: her value is far beyond pearls. She works with loving hands, obtains wool and flax, plies the spindle, reaches out her hands to the poor. Charm is deceptive and beauty fleeting, but the woman who fears the Lord is to be praised. Let her works praise her at the city gates. This isn't just about wives -- it's about the kind of industrious, generous life that merits honor.

second.synopsis: The day of the Lord will come like a thief at night. When people say 'Peace and security,' sudden disaster strikes. But you are not in darkness -- you are children of the light. So don't sleep as the rest do; stay alert and sober. Paul's counsel for the end times isn't fear -- it's wakefulness. If you're already living in the light, the thief's arrival doesn't catch you off guard.

gospel.synopsis: A man going on a journey entrusts his possessions to three servants: five talents to one, two to another, one to the third. The first two double their money. The third buries his in the ground out of fear. When the master returns: 'Well done, good and faithful servant! Come, share your master's joy' to the first two. To the third: 'You wicked, lazy servant!' He should have at least put it in the bank. The talent is taken from him and given to the one with ten. The message: God gives gifts not to be hoarded or buried but to be invested and multiplied. Fear-based inaction is the real failure.

ordinary-time-33-a.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to stop playing it safe with what God has given us. The master didn't expect all three servants to produce equally -- he gave each 'according to his ability.' The sin wasn't failing; it was refusing to try. The woman of Proverbs works with loving hands and opens them to the poor. Paul says stay awake. The penultimate week of Ordinary Time says: use what you've been given before the Master returns.

ordinary-time-33-b.logline: The sun will be darkened and the stars will fall -- but the Son of Man will come in glory to gather his elect. The end of the liturgical year mirrors Advent's beginning.
ordinary-time-33-b.trajectory: Year B (Mark): Mark's 'little apocalypse.' Cosmic imagery of the end times gives way to the promise of the Son of Man's coming. The liturgical year circles back toward Advent's themes of watchfulness.

first.synopsis: The archangel Michael will arise, guardian of the people, during a time of unprecedented distress. Those written in the book will escape. Many who sleep in the dust shall awake -- some to everlasting life, others to horror and disgrace. 'The wise shall shine brightly like the splendor of the firmament, and those who lead many to justice shall be like the stars forever.' One of the clearest Old Testament visions of resurrection and final judgment.

second.synopsis: Every priest stands daily offering the same sacrifices that can never take away sins. But Christ offered one sacrifice for sins and took his seat forever at God's right hand. By one offering he has made perfect forever those who are being consecrated. Where there is forgiveness, there is no longer need for sin offerings. The definitive sacrifice has already been made.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus describes the end: the sun will be darkened, the moon will not give its light, stars will fall, the powers of heaven will be shaken. Then 'they will see the Son of Man coming in the clouds with great power and glory.' He will send his angels to gather his elect from the four winds. Learn from the fig tree: when its branches sprout leaves, summer is near. 'Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away.' But of that day or hour, no one knows -- not even the Son, only the Father.

ordinary-time-33-b.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to hold cosmic upheaval and personal hope together. The apocalyptic imagery can feel alarming, but the core message is reassurance: the Son of Man comes to gather, not to destroy. Daniel's vision says the wise will shine like stars. Mark's Jesus says his words outlast heaven and earth. As the liturgical year ends, we're reminded that the same Christ we await at Advent is the one who comes at the end of all things.

ordinary-time-33-c.logline: The day is coming, blazing like an oven -- but for those who fear God's name, the sun of justice will rise with healing rays. Persevere and secure your lives.
ordinary-time-33-c.trajectory: Year C (Luke): The final Ordinary Time Sunday in Luke. Jesus predicts the destruction of the temple and speaks of persecution, but promises divine wisdom and ultimate vindication. The liturgical year comes full circle to meet Advent.

first.synopsis: The day is coming, blazing like an oven, when the proud and evildoers will be stubble set on fire -- no root, no branch. But for those who fear God's name, the sun of justice will arise with its healing rays. Two lines that capture the whole arc of judgment and mercy: destruction for arrogance, healing for those who reverence the Lord.

second.synopsis: Paul models the work ethic he expects: he worked night and day so as not to burden anyone. If anyone is unwilling to work, neither should that one eat. He's heard some are conducting themselves in a disorderly way, not working but minding others' business. His instruction: work quietly and eat your own food. Waiting for the Lord's return doesn't mean sitting idle.

gospel.synopsis: People admire the temple's beauty. Jesus says the days are coming when not one stone will be left on another. They ask when. He warns: don't be deceived by those who say 'I am he' or 'The time has come.' Wars, earthquakes, famines, and plagues will come, but 'it will not immediately be the end.' Before all this, they'll be persecuted and brought before authorities -- but this will be their chance to testify. 'I myself shall give you a wisdom your adversaries cannot resist.' Even betrayal by family won't destroy them. 'By your perseverance you will secure your lives.' The end of the gospel's journey narrative looks forward to the end of all things.

ordinary-time-33-c.invitesUsTo: This Sunday invites us to persevere with confidence as the liturgical year draws to its close. Malachi promises the sun of justice with healing rays for those who remain faithful. Jesus doesn't hide the cost -- persecution, betrayal, hatred -- but he promises wisdom and ultimate vindication. The last word isn't destruction; it's life, secured through patient endurance. Next week we celebrate Christ the King, and the cycle begins again.

solemnity-christ-the-king-a.logline: The King who judges nations by how they treated the hungry, the sick, and the forgotten.
solemnity-christ-the-king-a.trajectory: The last Sunday of the liturgical year. Everything the Church has proclaimed since Advent leads here: Christ reigns, and his throne is set among the least of these. Next week, the cycle begins again.

first.synopsis: God announces that he will personally shepherd his scattered flock. He will seek the lost, bring back the strayed, bind up the injured, and heal the sick. But this is not sentimental — God also promises to judge between sheep, separating rams from goats. The good shepherd is also the just judge.

second.synopsis: Paul lays out the endgame of history: Christ, raised from the dead, is the firstfruits of resurrection. He must reign until every enemy — including death itself — is destroyed. Then the Son hands the kingdom over to the Father, so that God may be all in all. The arc of salvation bends toward completion.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus describes the final judgment in vivid detail. The Son of Man separates the nations like a shepherd separates sheep from goats. The criterion is startlingly concrete: did you feed the hungry, welcome the stranger, clothe the naked, visit the sick and imprisoned? The twist — both the righteous and the condemned are surprised to learn that whatever they did or failed to do for the least, they did or failed to do for Christ himself.

solemnity-christ-the-king-a.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to see the face of Christ in every person who is hungry, displaced, sick, or forgotten. The King who will judge all nations does not ask about our theological credentials — he asks whether we noticed the person right in front of us. As the liturgical year closes, we are confronted with Christianity's most unsettling claim: that love for the vulnerable is not optional charity but the measure by which everything else is weighed.

solemnity-christ-the-king-b.logline: Before Pilate, Jesus redefines kingship as witness to the truth rather than worldly power.
solemnity-christ-the-king-b.trajectory: The last Sunday of the liturgical year. Year B's Christ the King turns from a cosmic throne room to a Roman courtroom, where the real King stands bound before an empire that cannot comprehend him. Advent begins next week.

first.synopsis: Daniel's night vision reveals one like a Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven. He is presented before the Ancient One and receives dominion, glory, and kingship over all peoples, nations, and languages. His dominion is everlasting and will never be destroyed. This is royalty on a cosmic scale.

second.synopsis: The Book of Revelation identifies Jesus as the faithful witness, the firstborn of the dead, and the ruler of kings. He has freed us by his blood and made us into a kingdom of priests. He is coming amid the clouds, and every eye will see him. He is the Alpha and the Omega — the beginning and the end of everything.

gospel.synopsis: Standing before Pilate, Jesus is asked point-blank: are you a king? Jesus responds that his kingdom does not belong to this world — if it did, his followers would be fighting. Pilate presses: so you are a king? Jesus answers that he was born to testify to the truth, and everyone who belongs to the truth listens to his voice. Power meets truth, and power does not know what to do with it.

solemnity-christ-the-king-b.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to reconsider what real authority looks like. Jesus stands bound and powerless before the Roman governor, yet he is the one asking the harder questions. His kingdom runs on truth and witness, not force. In a world that confuses power with greatness, this feast asks us whose voice we are actually listening to — and whether we belong to the truth.

solemnity-christ-the-king-c.logline: A dying criminal recognizes the King that everyone else is mocking — and receives paradise.
solemnity-christ-the-king-c.trajectory: The last Sunday of the liturgical year. Year C gives us the most unlikely throne room of all: two crosses on either side of Jesus, and a convicted criminal who sees what the religious leaders cannot. Next week, Advent begins the story again.

first.synopsis: The tribes of Israel come to David at Hebron, acknowledging that even under Saul, it was David who truly led them. They recognize his calling — the Lord had said David would shepherd Israel. The elders anoint him king. It is a simple, almost understated coronation for Israel's greatest king.

second.synopsis: Paul offers one of the New Testament's highest descriptions of Christ: the image of the invisible God, firstborn of all creation, the one in whom all things were created and in whom all things hold together. He is the head of the Church, the firstborn from the dead, and through his cross he reconciles all things — on earth and in heaven.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus hangs on the cross while rulers sneer and soldiers mock: if you are the King, save yourself. One criminal joins the taunting, but the other rebukes him and makes an astonishing request: 'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom.' Jesus replies with the most generous sentence in Scripture: 'Today you will be with me in Paradise.' The King's first royal act from his throne is mercy.

solemnity-christ-the-king-c.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to see that Christ's kingship is most visible at its most vulnerable. While everyone mocks and demands proof, one dying man simply asks to be remembered — and receives everything. It is never too late, and no one is too far gone. The Kingdom is not earned by achievement but entered through honest need.

solemnity-most-holy-trinity-a.logline: God reveals his name to Moses as merciful and gracious — and then sends his Son because he loved the world.
solemnity-most-holy-trinity-a.trajectory: The Sunday after Pentecost. The Easter season is complete, the Spirit has been poured out, and the Church pauses to name what it has experienced: one God in three persons. This marks the transition into Ordinary Time.

first.synopsis: Moses climbs Sinai carrying the stone tablets. God descends in a cloud and proclaims his own name — not as a title of power but as a self-description: 'The Lord, a merciful and gracious God, slow to anger and rich in kindness and fidelity.' Moses bows to the ground and asks God to travel with his stiff-necked people. God reveals his deepest identity as mercy.

second.synopsis: Paul closes his letter with a brief, packed farewell: rejoice, mend your ways, encourage one another, live in peace. Then comes the formula that shaped Christian prayer ever since — 'The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with all of you.' Three names, one blessing.

gospel.synopsis: The most quoted verse in Christianity: God so loved the world that he gave his only Son, so that everyone who believes might not perish but have eternal life. God did not send his Son to condemn the world but to save it. The Trinity is not an abstract doctrine — it is the shape of a love that gives everything away.

solemnity-most-holy-trinity-a.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to stop treating the Trinity as a theological puzzle and start recognizing it as the shape of God's love. A merciful Father reveals himself on a mountain. A Son is given because the world is loved. A Spirit binds us together in fellowship. We do not need to explain the mystery — we are invited to live inside it.

solemnity-most-holy-trinity-b.logline: The God who spoke from fire adopts us as children through the Spirit and sends us to baptize all nations.
solemnity-most-holy-trinity-b.trajectory: The Sunday after Pentecost. Year B approaches the Trinity through experience — Israel's encounter with God, the Spirit's witness that we are God's children, and the risen Jesus commissioning his disciples. The transition into Ordinary Time begins here.

first.synopsis: Moses challenges the people to search all of history: has any nation heard the voice of God speaking from fire and survived? Has any god rescued a people the way yours did in Egypt? The conclusion is direct — the Lord is God in the heavens above and on earth below, and there is no other. Fix it in your heart.

second.synopsis: Paul describes the Spirit's role in the Trinity's relationship with us. We did not receive a spirit of slavery but a Spirit of adoption, through whom we cry 'Abba, Father.' The Spirit bears witness that we are children of God — and if children, then heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ. The cost: we suffer with him so that we may also be glorified with him.

gospel.synopsis: The risen Jesus meets the eleven on a mountain in Galilee. They worship, though some doubt. Jesus claims all authority in heaven and earth and sends them to make disciples of all nations, baptizing in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. His final promise: 'I am with you always, until the end of the age.' The Trinitarian formula becomes the Church's mission.

solemnity-most-holy-trinity-b.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to recognize that the Trinity is not distant theology — it is the God who spoke from fire, the Spirit who calls us children, and the Son who promises to stay with us until the end. We are not spectators of this mystery. Through baptism, we are drawn into the life of the Trinity itself, adopted into the family and sent out to share it.

solemnity-most-holy-trinity-c.logline: Wisdom plays at God's side from before creation, and the Spirit guides us into all truth.
solemnity-most-holy-trinity-c.trajectory: The Sunday after Pentecost. Year C takes the most poetic approach to the Trinity — divine Wisdom delighting in creation, suffering producing hope through the Spirit's love, and Jesus promising that the Spirit will declare what belongs to the Father and the Son.

first.synopsis: Wisdom speaks in the first person, describing her presence before creation itself. She was there when God established the heavens, marked out the vault of the deep, and set the sea its limits. She was beside God as his craftsman, his delight day by day, playing before him. And she found her own delight in the human race. This is a portrait of God's creative joy.

second.synopsis: Paul traces a chain reaction: justified by faith, we have peace with God through Christ. But we even boast in our afflictions, because affliction produces endurance, endurance produces character, and character produces hope. And hope does not disappoint, because the love of God has been poured into our hearts through the Holy Spirit. The Trinity is Father, Son, and Spirit working together to transform suffering into hope.

gospel.synopsis: At the Last Supper, Jesus tells his disciples he has more to say but they cannot bear it yet. When the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide them to all truth. The Spirit will take from what belongs to Jesus and declare it to them — and everything the Father has belongs to Jesus. Three persons, one flow of truth and love.

solemnity-most-holy-trinity-c.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to approach the Trinity not as a doctrine to be solved but as a relationship to be experienced. Wisdom delights in creation. The Spirit pours love into our hearts. The Son shares everything the Father has given him. We are caught up in a divine exchange of truth, creativity, and love — one that has been going on since before the world was made.

solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-a.logline: The God who fed Israel with mysterious bread in the desert now gives his own flesh as food for eternal life.
solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-a.trajectory: The Sunday after Trinity Sunday. The Church moves from contemplating who God is to celebrating how God feeds us. Year A connects the manna in the wilderness to the Bread of Life discourse — the Eucharist as God's ongoing provision.

first.synopsis: Moses reminds Israel of their forty years in the desert. God let them hunger, then fed them with manna — a food unknown to them or their ancestors — to teach them that people do not live by bread alone but by every word from God's mouth. The desert was a classroom: hunger first, then provision, then understanding.

second.synopsis: Paul asks a direct question: the cup we bless, is it not participation in the blood of Christ? The bread we break, is it not participation in the body of Christ? Because the bread is one, we who are many are one body. The Eucharist does not just connect us to Christ — it connects us to each other.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus makes his most startling claim: 'I am the living bread that came down from heaven. Whoever eats this bread will live forever, and the bread I will give is my flesh for the life of the world.' The crowd is scandalized. Jesus doubles down: unless you eat my flesh and drink my blood, you have no life in you. Note the shift from John 3 (where 'believing' gives eternal life) to John 6 (where 'eating' does) — Jesus moves from mental assent to bodily participation. This is not metaphor. Whoever feeds on him will live because of him.

solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-a.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to take the Eucharist as seriously as Jesus does. He is not offering a symbol or a nice idea — he is offering himself as real food. The manna in the desert kept people alive for a day. This bread is meant to sustain eternal life. And because we all share one loaf, the Eucharist also makes us responsible for one another.

solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-b.logline: The blood of the old covenant sprinkled on the altar becomes the blood of Christ poured out for many.
solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-b.trajectory: The Sunday after Trinity Sunday. Year B traces the Eucharist through the lens of covenant — from Moses sealing the covenant with animal blood at Sinai to Jesus offering his own blood as the seal of a new and eternal covenant.

first.synopsis: Moses relays God's words and the people respond with one voice: we will do everything the Lord has said. Moses builds an altar, young men offer sacrifices, and he takes the blood — half splashed on the altar, half sprinkled on the people — saying, 'This is the blood of the covenant the Lord has made with you.' It is a raw, physical sealing of the relationship between God and his people.

second.synopsis: The Letter to the Hebrews draws the connection: if animal blood could purify the flesh, how much more will the blood of Christ — who offered himself through the eternal Spirit — cleanse our consciences? Christ entered the heavenly sanctuary not with the blood of animals but with his own, obtaining eternal redemption. He is the mediator of a new covenant.

gospel.synopsis: On the first day of Unleavened Bread, the disciples prepare the Passover. During supper, Jesus takes bread, blesses it, breaks it, and says, 'This is my body.' He takes the cup and says, 'This is my blood of the covenant, which will be shed for many.' Then they sing a hymn and go to the Mount of Olives. The Last Supper in Mark is spare and stunning — every word carries weight.

solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-b.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to understand the Eucharist as covenant — a binding relationship sealed in blood. At Sinai, the people were sprinkled with blood and pledged their faithfulness. At the Last Supper, Jesus offers his own blood as the seal of a new and better covenant. Every time we receive the cup, we are renewing that covenant and pledging ourselves again.

solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-c.logline: From Melchizedek's ancient bread and wine to five thousand fed — the Eucharist has always been about abundance.
solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-c.trajectory: The Sunday after Trinity Sunday. Year C connects the Eucharist to both its ancient roots and its missionary future — a mysterious priest-king's offering, Paul's account of the Last Supper, and a crowd fed with almost nothing.

first.synopsis: Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest of God Most High, brings out bread and wine and blesses Abram. It is one of the most mysterious moments in Genesis — a priest who appears from nowhere, offering the same elements that will one day become the Eucharist. Abram gives him a tenth of everything. The pattern of bread, wine, and blessing is older than Israel itself.

second.synopsis: Paul hands on what he received from the Lord: on the night he was handed over, Jesus took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and said, 'This is my body that is for you. Do this in remembrance of me.' The cup is the new covenant in his blood. As often as we eat this bread and drink this cup, we proclaim the death of the Lord until he comes. This is the earliest written account of the Eucharist.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus has been teaching and healing all day. As evening approaches, the disciples suggest sending the crowd away to find food. Jesus says, 'Give them some food yourselves.' With only five loaves and two fish, he blesses, breaks, and gives — and five thousand are fed with twelve baskets left over. The Eucharistic pattern is unmistakable: he took, blessed, broke, and gave.

solemnity-body-blood-of-christ-c.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to see the Eucharist as an act of impossible abundance. Melchizedek's bread and wine, Jesus' broken bread for thousands, Paul's account of the Last Supper — the thread is always the same: God takes what is small, blesses it, and makes it more than enough. When Jesus says 'give them some food yourselves,' he is telling us the Eucharist is not just received but shared.

solemnity-nov-1-all-saints-abc.logline: A countless multitude in white robes, the Beatitudes as their resume, and the promise that we shall see God as he is.
solemnity-nov-1-all-saints-abc.trajectory: All Saints falls near the end of the liturgical year, between OT 30 and OT 31, when the readings turn toward eschatological themes — last things, final judgment, the communion of saints. It serves as both a celebration of those who have completed the journey and a bridge to All Souls Day (November 2), when the Church prays for all the faithful departed.

first.synopsis: John sees an angel sealing the servants of God, then a vision shifts to a vast multitude that no one can count — from every nation, race, people, and tongue — standing before the throne in white robes with palm branches. They cry out that salvation comes from God and the Lamb. Who are they? Those who survived the great distress and washed their robes white in the blood of the Lamb.

second.synopsis: John marvels at what the Father has given us: we are called children of God — and we actually are. The world does not recognize us because it did not recognize him. What we will ultimately become has not yet been revealed, but we know this: when it is revealed, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is. Everyone who holds this hope makes themselves pure.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus goes up the mountain, sits down, and delivers the Beatitudes — the manifesto of the Kingdom. Blessed are the poor in spirit, those who mourn, the meek, those who hunger for righteousness, the merciful, the clean of heart, the peacemakers, the persecuted. These are not rules to follow but descriptions of the people God calls blessed. The saints lived this way. Rejoice and be glad — your reward is great in heaven.

solemnity-nov-1-all-saints-abc.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to look up from our own small struggles and see the enormous company we belong to. The saints are not distant superheroes — they are the countless ordinary people who lived the Beatitudes in their own time and place. We are already God's children. What we will become is still unfolding. All Saints is both a celebration of those who have finished the race and an encouragement to those of us still running it.

ash-wednesday.logline: Rend your hearts, be reconciled to God, and practice your faith in secret — Lent begins now.
ash-wednesday.trajectory: Ash Wednesday opens the Lenten season. The three readings establish the three pillars of Lent — fasting, prayer, and almsgiving — and set the tone for forty days of conversion. Everything between here and the Easter Triduum flows from this starting point.

first.synopsis: God speaks through Joel with an urgent invitation: return to me with your whole heart, with fasting, weeping, and mourning. But the key line reframes everything — 'Rend your hearts, not your garments.' This is not about performance. God is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, rich in kindness, and he may relent. The trumpet sounds in Zion calling a sacred assembly. The priests weep between the porch and the altar, pleading: spare your people, Lord.

second.synopsis: Paul delivers one of the most direct appeals in the New Testament: 'We implore you on behalf of Christ, be reconciled to God.' The one who did not know sin became sin for us, so that we might become the righteousness of God in him. Then comes the line that gives Ash Wednesday its urgency — 'Now is the acceptable time. Now is the day of salvation.' Not later. Now.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus lays out the three Lenten practices — almsgiving, prayer, and fasting — and gives the same instruction for each: do it in secret. Do not blow a trumpet when you give alms. Do not pray on street corners to be seen. Do not look gloomy when you fast. Your Father who sees in secret will repay you. The irony of hearing this gospel while wearing ashes on your forehead is not lost on the tradition — and it is worth sitting with.

ash-wednesday.invitesUsTo: This day invites us to begin again — honestly, quietly, and without pretense. Joel's call to rend our hearts rather than our garments strips away external performance and asks for interior conversion. Jesus reinforces this by insisting that our prayer, fasting, and generosity happen in secret, between us and God. Paul removes every excuse for delay: now is the time. Ash Wednesday does not ease us into Lent. It drops us into the deep end and trusts that God meets us there.

feast-abc-feb-2-presentation-of-the-lord.logline: An elderly man holds an infant in the temple and sees in him the salvation of the world — a light for all nations.

first.synopsis: The Lord announces that he is sending his messenger to prepare the way — and then the Lord himself will suddenly come to his temple. But who can endure the day of his coming? He is like a refiner's fire and like fullers' lye. He will purify the sons of Levi until they offer sacrifice in righteousness. The language is both thrilling and unsettling — God's arrival is not gentle.

second.synopsis: Jesus shared fully in human flesh and blood so that through death he might destroy the one who holds the power of death — the devil — and free those who lived in fear of death all their lives. He had to become like his brothers and sisters in every way, so he could be a merciful and faithful high priest. He was tested through suffering, which is exactly what qualifies him to help those who are being tested.

gospel.synopsis: Mary and Joseph bring the infant Jesus to the temple to present him to the Lord and offer sacrifice according to the law. There they meet Simeon, a righteous man who had been promised he would not die before seeing the Messiah. He takes the child in his arms and prays the Nunc Dimittis: 'Now, Master, you may let your servant go in peace — my eyes have seen your salvation, a light for revelation to the Gentiles and glory for your people Israel.' He also warns Mary that a sword will pierce her soul. Anna, an 84-year-old prophetess, gives thanks and speaks about the child to all who await redemption.

feast-abc-feb-2-presentation-of-the-lord.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to see what Simeon saw — salvation arriving not in power but in the arms of an old man holding a baby. The Presentation is about recognition: two elderly people at the end of their lives recognize in this infant the fulfillment of everything they have been waiting for. Malachi warned that the Lord would come to his temple suddenly. He did — as a six-week-old child. For music ministers, the Nunc Dimittis is one of the great canticles of the tradition, and this is its day.

feast-of-st-monica.logline: A mother's relentless faith, a life of prayer and mercy, and Jesus' radical call to love without limits.

first.synopsis: God redefines what fasting really means. It is not sackcloth and ashes — it is releasing the unjustly bound, sharing your bread with the hungry, sheltering the homeless, and clothing the naked. When you do this, 'your light shall break forth like the dawn, and your wound shall quickly be healed.' If you remove oppression and satisfy the afflicted, you will be like a watered garden, like a spring whose water never fails.

second.synopsis: Peter writes with end-times urgency: be serious and sober for prayers. Above all, let your mutual love be intense, because love covers a multitude of sins. Be hospitable without complaining. Use whatever gift you have received to serve one another as stewards of God's varied grace. Whoever serves should do so with the strength God supplies, so that God may be glorified in all things through Jesus Christ.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus delivers some of his most demanding teaching: love your enemies, do good to those who hate you, bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you. If someone strikes your cheek, offer the other. Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful. Stop judging. Stop condemning. Forgive. Give — and gifts will be given to you: a good measure, packed together, shaken down, and overflowing will be poured into your lap.

feast-of-st-monica.invitesUsTo: This memorial invites us to consider what a life of persistent, sacrificial love actually looks like. Monica is remembered for praying relentlessly for her son Augustine through decades of his wandering and resistance. These readings mirror her life — Isaiah's insistence that real devotion means caring for others, Peter's call to intense love and faithful service, and Jesus' radical demand that we love even those who resist us. Monica never gave up. This feast asks whether we have that kind of endurance.

feast-our-lady-of-guadalupe-abc.logline: God comes to dwell among his people, a woman clothed with the sun appears, and Mary sings that the mighty are cast down.

first.synopsis: The most common option is Zechariah's joyful prophecy: 'Sing and rejoice, O daughter Zion! See, I am coming to dwell among you, says the Lord.' Many nations will join themselves to the Lord on that day. The Revelation option offers the cosmic vision of a woman clothed with the sun, with the moon at her feet and a crown of twelve stars — a sign of both Israel and Mary, pursued by a dragon yet protected by God.

second.synopsis: Paul states it plainly: when the fullness of time had come, God sent his Son, born of a woman, born under the law, to ransom those under the law so that we might receive adoption as children. Because you are children, God sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying 'Abba, Father!' You are no longer a slave but a child, and if a child then also an heir. The incarnation makes us family.

gospel.synopsis: Mary travels in haste to visit her cousin Elizabeth in the hill country. When Elizabeth hears Mary's greeting, the infant leaps in her womb and Elizabeth is filled with the Holy Spirit. She cries out: 'Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb! How does this happen to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?' Mary responds with the Magnificat — 'My soul proclaims the greatness of the Lord, my spirit rejoices in God my Savior' — a song that praises God for scattering the proud and lifting up the lowly.

feast-our-lady-of-guadalupe-abc.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to see God choosing to dwell among ordinary people in unexpected places. Guadalupe is the story of Mary appearing to Juan Diego — an indigenous man on the margins — and through him shaking the foundations of a colonial church. The readings echo this pattern: Zechariah promises God will dwell among the nations, Paul says we are adopted as God's own children, and the Magnificat insists that God lifts the lowly and fills the hungry. This is a feast about divine closeness to those the world overlooks.

feast-the-exaltation-sep-14-of-the-holy-.logline: A bronze serpent lifted in the desert, a hymn of self-emptying obedience, and the Son of Man lifted up so the world might live.

first.synopsis: The Israelites grow impatient in the desert and complain against God and Moses — why did you bring us out of Egypt to die? God sends fiery serpents among them, and many are bitten and die. The people confess their sin and beg Moses to pray. God tells Moses to make a bronze serpent and mount it on a pole: anyone who is bitten and looks at it will live. The remedy comes in the very form of the affliction — a strange foreshadowing of the cross.

second.synopsis: The great kenosis hymn. Though Christ was in the form of God, he did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped. He emptied himself, taking the form of a slave, born in human likeness. He humbled himself, becoming obedient to the point of death — even death on a cross. Therefore God greatly exalted him and bestowed on him the name above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bend and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus draws the connection himself: 'Just as Moses lifted up the serpent in the desert, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, so that everyone who believes in him may have eternal life.' Then comes the verse that holds the entire gospel in one sentence: 'God so loved the world that he gave his only Son, so that everyone who believes in him might not perish but might have eternal life.' God did not send his Son to condemn the world, but to save it.

feast-the-exaltation-sep-14-of-the-holy-.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to look at the cross and see not defeat but the fullest expression of God's love. The bronze serpent in the desert is the prototype — healing comes by looking at the very thing that wounds. Paul's hymn traces the downward arc of Christ's self-emptying all the way to death on a cross, then the dramatic reversal of exaltation. John 3:16-17 explains why: God loved the world that much. For music ministers, this feast calls for music that holds both the cost and the glory of the cross together.

memorial-abc-feast-of-st-francis-oct-4.logline: Boasting only in the cross, bearing the marks of Jesus, and finding rest in meekness and humility of heart.

first.synopsis: Paul makes a declaration that became the motto of Francis of Assisi: 'May I never boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, through which the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world.' Neither circumcision nor uncircumcision matters — only a new creation. Paul closes by noting the marks of Jesus he bears on his body. For Francis, who received the stigmata, these words became literally embodied.

second.synopsis: Not typically proclaimed at memorial rank. When this feast is celebrated as a memorial, only two readings (first reading and gospel) are used.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus praises the Father for hiding the mysteries of the kingdom from the wise and learned and revealing them to little ones — 'for such has been your gracious will.' Then he issues the most tender invitation in the gospels: 'Come to me, all you who labor and are burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am meek and humble of heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden light.'

memorial-abc-feast-of-st-francis-oct-4.invitesUsTo: This memorial invites us to consider what it means to let go of everything the world values and boast only in the cross. Francis stripped himself of wealth, status, and ambition to follow a crucified Lord — and found freedom and joy in the letting go. Jesus' invitation to the burdened is Francis' whole program: learn meekness, embrace humility, take the lighter yoke. This feast asks whether our burdens are ones God actually gave us, or ones we picked up on our own.

solemnity-immaculate-conception.logline: From Eden's fall to the angel's greeting — God chose Mary before the foundation of the world to be holy and without blemish.

first.synopsis: After the Fall, God comes looking: 'Where are you?' Adam blames Eve, Eve blames the serpent. God curses the serpent and delivers the protoevangelium — the first hint of the gospel: 'I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and hers; he will strike at your head, while you strike at his heel.' The Church reads this as the first promise of redemption, with the woman understood as a figure of Mary.

second.synopsis: Paul opens with a cascade of blessing: 'Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us in Christ with every spiritual blessing in the heavens.' God chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world to be holy and without blemish before him. He destined us for adoption through Jesus Christ, in accord with the favor of his will, for the praise of his glory. Every phrase here applies to Mary in a particular way on this feast.

gospel.synopsis: The angel Gabriel is sent to Nazareth, to a virgin named Mary. His greeting is unique in all of Scripture: 'Hail, full of grace! The Lord is with you.' Mary is deeply troubled. Gabriel reassures her: 'Do not be afraid. You will conceive and bear a son, and you shall name him Jesus.' Mary asks the practical question: 'How can this be, since I have no relations with a man?' The answer: 'The Holy Spirit will come upon you.' Mary's response is the hinge of salvation history: 'Behold, I am the handmaid of the Lord. May it be done to me according to your word.'

solemnity-immaculate-conception.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to see Mary's 'yes' against the backdrop of humanity's original 'no.' Genesis gives us the Fall — the grasping, the blame, the broken trust. Ephesians reveals that God had a plan before the world began: to choose and set apart. Luke gives us the moment when a young woman, prepared by grace from her conception, says the word that opens the door to redemption. The Immaculate Conception is not just about Mary's privilege — it is about God's long, patient preparation for the moment when everything could begin again.

solemnity-nov-1-all-souls-abc.logline: The souls of the just are in God's hand, baptism unites us to Christ's death and resurrection, and no one who comes to him will be lost.

first.synopsis: The souls of the just are in the hand of God, and no torment shall touch them. They seemed, in the view of the foolish, to be dead — their passing looked like destruction, their going forth like annihilation. But they are in peace. Though punished in the sight of people, their hope is full of immortality. Chastised a little, they shall be greatly blessed, because God tried them and found them worthy of himself.

second.synopsis: Paul asks a question that reframes death itself: are you unaware that we who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried with him through baptism so that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might live in newness of life. If we have died with Christ, we believe we shall also live with him. Christ, raised from the dead, dies no more — death no longer has power over him.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus makes a promise that speaks directly to the grieving: 'Everything that the Father gives me will come to me, and I will not reject anyone who comes to me.' He came down from heaven not to do his own will but the will of the one who sent him. And what is that will? 'That I should not lose anything of what he gave me, but that I should raise it on the last day.' Everyone who sees the Son and believes in him shall have eternal life, and Jesus himself will raise them up.

solemnity-nov-1-all-souls-abc.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to pray for the dead with confidence and grief held together. The readings do not minimize the reality of death — Wisdom acknowledges that the just appeared to be destroyed, and Paul says plainly that we were baptized into death. But the promise running through all three readings is that death does not have the final word. The souls of the just are in peace. We are buried with Christ so we can rise with him. Jesus will not lose anyone the Father has given him. All Souls is the Church's annual act of trust that love does not end at the grave.

ss-peter-paul-apostles-jun-29-at-the-vig.logline: Peter heals in Jesus' name, Paul receives the gospel through revelation, and the risen Christ asks Peter three times: do you love me?

first.synopsis: Peter and John go up to the temple at the hour of prayer. A man crippled from birth, carried daily to the Beautiful Gate to beg, asks them for alms. Peter looks at him intently and says: 'I have neither silver nor gold, but what I do have I give you: in the name of Jesus Christ the Nazorean, rise and walk.' He takes the man by the hand, pulls him up, and immediately his feet and ankles grow strong. The man enters the temple walking, leaping, and praising God.

second.synopsis: Paul insists that the gospel he preaches did not come from any human source but through a revelation of Jesus Christ. He recounts his former life — his violent persecution of the Church, his advancement in Judaism beyond his peers. But God, who set him apart from his mother's womb, chose to reveal his Son to Paul so that he might proclaim him to the Gentiles. He went to Arabia, then to Damascus, then finally to Jerusalem to confer with Cephas — Peter — staying fifteen days.

gospel.synopsis: After the resurrection, Jesus shares breakfast with his disciples on the shore. Then he turns to Peter and asks three times: 'Simon, son of John, do you love me?' Each time Peter says yes. Each time Jesus responds: 'Feed my lambs.' 'Tend my sheep.' 'Feed my sheep.' The threefold question mirrors Peter's threefold denial. Peter is grieved by the third asking, but Jesus is restoring him — and commissioning him. The final word: 'Follow me.'

ss-peter-paul-apostles-jun-29-at-the-vig.invitesUsTo: This vigil invites us to see the Church built on two very different men who shared one calling. Peter, the fisherman who denied Jesus three times, is restored by love and entrusted with the flock. Paul, the persecutor who breathed murderous threats, is knocked flat by a revelation and sent to the nations. Neither earned his apostleship. Both were given it by a Lord who specializes in unlikely choices. This feast celebrates the foundation stones of the Church — broken, rebuilt, and sent.

ss-peter-paul-apostles-jun-29-mass-durin.logline: An angel breaks Peter's chains at midnight, Paul finishes the race, and Jesus builds his Church on the rock of Peter's confession.

first.synopsis: King Herod arrests Peter and throws him in prison, intending to bring him before the people after Passover. The church prays fervently for him. The night before his trial, Peter sleeps chained between two soldiers. An angel appears, strikes Peter's side to wake him, and his chains fall off. The angel leads him past the guards and through the iron gate, which opens by itself. Peter, thinking it is a vision, finally comes to himself and says: 'Now I know for certain that the Lord sent his angel and rescued me from the hand of Herod.'

second.synopsis: Paul writes from prison, likely near the end of his life: 'I am already being poured out like a libation, and the time of my departure is at hand.' Then comes one of the most stirring summaries in Scripture: 'I have competed well; I have finished the race; I have kept the faith. From now on the crown of righteousness awaits me.' The Lord stood by him and gave him strength so that the proclamation might be completed. He trusts the Lord will rescue him for the heavenly kingdom.
