gospel.synopsis: At Caesarea Philippi, Jesus asks his disciples the question that defines the Church: 'Who do you say that I am?' Peter answers: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.' Jesus responds: 'Blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah — flesh and blood has not revealed this to you, but my heavenly Father.' Then he makes the declaration: 'You are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my Church, and the gates of the netherworld shall not prevail against it.' He gives Peter the keys of the kingdom.

ss-peter-paul-apostles-jun-29-mass-durin.invitesUsTo: This solemnity invites us to celebrate the two pillars of the Church and what their lives cost them. Peter is freed from chains by an angel — not because his life would be easy, but because his work was not yet done. Paul, at the end of the road, has no regrets: the race is finished, the faith is kept. And the gospel gives us the moment it all began — Peter's confession and Jesus' promise to build on that rock. This feast reminds us that the Church is founded on faith spoken aloud, tested by suffering, and sustained by a Lord who does not abandon his own.

the-dedication-of-nov-9-the-lateran-basi.logline: Living water flows from the temple, you are the temple of God, and Jesus speaks of the temple of his body.

first.synopsis: Ezekiel sees water flowing from beneath the threshold of the temple, trickling at first, then becoming a river too deep to cross. Wherever the river flows, it brings life — even the Dead Sea is made fresh, and fish swarm in its waters. Trees on both banks bear fresh fruit every month and their leaves never wither, because the water comes from the sanctuary. The temple is not just a building but a source of life for the whole world.

second.synopsis: Paul shifts the temple image from stone to people: 'Do you not know that you are the temple of God, and that the Spirit of God dwells in you?' He warns that God's temple is holy — and you are that temple. No one can lay a foundation other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ. The building metaphor is deliberate: we are God's building, and every person builds on the one foundation.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus enters the temple during Passover, finds it full of merchants and money changers, and drives them all out with a whip of cords: 'Take these out of here, and stop making my Father's house a marketplace.' The Jews demand a sign. Jesus answers: 'Destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up.' They think he means the building that took forty-six years to construct. But he was speaking about the temple of his body.

the-dedication-of-nov-9-the-lateran-basi.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to think about what a temple really is — and to discover that the answer keeps expanding. Ezekiel's temple is a source of living water that heals everything it touches. Paul says we ourselves are God's temple, the dwelling place of the Spirit. Jesus identifies his own body as the true temple that will be destroyed and raised. The Lateran Basilica is the pope's cathedral and the mother church of Christendom, but this feast uses it as an occasion to proclaim that God's dwelling place is not a building — it is a body, a community, a river of life.

pentecost-vigil-abc.logline: Creation groans, the Spirit intercedes with sighs too deep for words, and Jesus promises rivers of living water to all who thirst.
pentecost-vigil-abc.trajectory: The Pentecost Vigil anticipates the great feast by recalling the Old Testament moments when God's Spirit was at work — scattering, gathering, breathing life, pouring out. The vigil format uses one Old Testament reading (parish's choice) before the epistle and gospel.

first.citation: Genesis 11:1-9 or Exodus 19:3-8a, 16-20b or Ezekiel 37:1-14 or Joel 3:1-5 (one OT reading selected)
first.synopsis: The vigil offers four Old Testament options, each revealing the Spirit's work from a different angle. Genesis 11: at Babel, God scattered humanity by confusing their languages — Pentecost will reverse this. Exodus 19: at Sinai, God descends in fire and thunder to form a covenant people — Pentecost will write the law on hearts. Ezekiel 37: the valley of dry bones receives breath and lives again — Pentecost will breathe life into the Church. Joel 3: God promises to pour out his Spirit on all flesh, young and old, slave and free — Pentecost fulfills this literally.

second.synopsis: Paul says all creation groans in labor pains together until now — and we groan within ourselves, awaiting the redemption of our bodies. We were saved in hope, and hope that is seen is not hope. In our weakness, we do not know how to pray as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with inexpressible groanings. The one who searches hearts knows the mind of the Spirit. Even our inability to pray is covered by the Spirit's intercession.

gospel.synopsis: On the last and greatest day of the feast, Jesus stands up and cries out: 'Let anyone who thirsts come to me and drink. Whoever believes in me, as Scripture says, rivers of living water will flow from within him.' John explains that Jesus said this about the Spirit, which those who came to believe in him were to receive — for the Spirit had not yet been given, because Jesus had not yet been glorified. Pentecost is the fulfillment of this promise.

pentecost-vigil-abc.invitesUsTo: This vigil invites us to wait with expectation on the eve of the Spirit's arrival. The Old Testament readings recall the long history of God scattering and gathering, descending in fire, breathing life into the dead, and promising to pour out his Spirit on all flesh. Paul acknowledges that we groan and do not even know how to pray — but the Spirit is already interceding for us beneath our awareness. Jesus' cry — 'Let anyone who thirsts come to me' — is the invitation that Pentecost answers. The vigil is about thirst before it is about fire.

pentecost-ext-vigil-abc.logline: The full sweep of the Spirit's work — from Babel's scattering to Sinai's fire to dry bones rising to the promise poured out on all flesh.
pentecost-ext-vigil-abc.trajectory: The Extended Vigil of Pentecost uses all four Old Testament readings, tracing the full arc of the Spirit's activity through salvation history before arriving at the epistle and gospel. This is the most expansive liturgical preparation for Pentecost — a miniature Vigil in the style of the Easter Vigil.

first.citation: Genesis 11:1-9, Exodus 19:3-8a, 16-20b, Ezekiel 37:1-14, and Joel 3:1-5 (all four OT readings)
first.synopsis: The extended vigil proclaims all four Old Testament readings in sequence, building a cumulative narrative of the Spirit's work. Genesis: at Babel, human pride leads God to scatter the nations and confuse their speech — a rupture Pentecost will heal. Exodus: at Sinai, God descends in thunder, lightning, and fire to give the law and form a covenant people — Pentecost will internalize that covenant. Ezekiel: God commands the prophet to speak to a valley of dry bones, and breath enters them and they live — Pentecost will breathe the Church into existence. Joel: God promises to pour out his Spirit on all flesh, sons and daughters will prophesy, old men will dream dreams — Pentecost fulfills this to the letter.

second.synopsis: Paul says all creation groans in labor pains together until now — and we groan within ourselves, awaiting the redemption of our bodies. We were saved in hope, and hope that is seen is not hope. In our weakness, we do not know how to pray as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with inexpressible groanings. The one who searches hearts knows the mind of the Spirit. Even our inability to pray is covered by the Spirit's intercession.

gospel.synopsis: On the last and greatest day of the feast, Jesus stands up and cries out: 'Let anyone who thirsts come to me and drink. Whoever believes in me, as Scripture says, rivers of living water will flow from within him.' John explains that Jesus said this about the Spirit, which those who came to believe in him were to receive — for the Spirit had not yet been given, because Jesus had not yet been glorified. Pentecost is the fulfillment of this promise.

pentecost-ext-vigil-abc.invitesUsTo: This extended vigil invites us to experience the full sweep of salvation history as a single story leading to Pentecost. Babel scattered — Pentecost gathers. Sinai gave the law on stone — Pentecost writes it on hearts. Ezekiel's bones were dead — the Spirit gives them breath and life. Joel promised the outpouring — Pentecost delivers it. By hearing all four Old Testament readings in sequence, the assembly experiences the cumulative weight of what the Spirit has been doing since the beginning of creation. Romans acknowledges the groaning that continues even now, and Jesus' cry — 'Let anyone who thirsts come to me' — stands as the final invitation before the fire falls.

nativity.logline: Christmas Day — four distinct liturgies trace the arc from anticipation to incarnation to cosmic revelation.
nativity.trajectory: Christmas offers four Mass formularies: Vigil (anticipation/genealogy), Midnight (the birth/angels/shepherds), Dawn (the shepherds respond), and Day (the cosmic Word-made-flesh from John 1). Each has its own readings and theological emphasis.

first.synopsis: "The people who walked in darkness have seen a great light." Isaiah announces the birth of a royal child whose titles are staggering: Wonder-Counselor, God-Hero, Father-Forever, Prince of Peace. The yoke of oppression is smashed, the boots and bloodied cloaks of war are burned. This is the great messianic prophecy — a child whose dominion is vast and forever peaceful, sustained by justice from David's throne.

second.synopsis: Paul tells Titus that "the grace of God has appeared, saving all." This grace trains us to reject godless ways and live with temperance, justice, and devotion while we wait for the "blessed hope" — the full appearance of Christ's glory. Jesus gave himself to deliver us from lawlessness and to form a people eager to do good. It is a compact theology of Christmas: grace appeared, grace transforms, grace promises more.

gospel.synopsis: Luke sets the scene with a census under Caesar Augustus that sends Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem. There, in the most ordinary of circumstances — no room at the inn — Mary gives birth, wraps the baby in swaddling clothes, and lays him in a manger. Shepherds keeping watch in the fields are terrified by an angel who proclaims "good news of great joy" — a savior born in the city of David. Then the sky fills with angels singing, "Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace to those on whom his favor rests."

nativity.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to see the contrast at the heart of Christmas: the almighty God entering the world as a vulnerable infant, laid in a feeding trough. Isaiah's promise of a great light piercing the darkness is fulfilled not in a palace but in a stable, announced not to kings but to shepherds. We are invited to find God in the small, overlooked, and humble places — and to join the angels in singing glory.

abc-jan-04-2026-sun.logline: Wisdom pitches her tent among God's people, and the eternal Word becomes flesh to dwell among us.
abc-jan-04-2026-sun.trajectory: The 2nd Sunday after Christmas lingers in the Incarnation before Epiphany arrives. The readings refuse to rush past Christmas — Sirach's Wisdom, Paul's cosmic adoption plan, and John's Prologue all insist we sit with the mystery of God choosing to live among us.

first.synopsis: Wisdom speaks in the first person, boasting before the assembly of the Most High. The Creator commanded her to pitch her tent in Jacob and put down roots in Israel. "Before all ages, in the beginning, he created me, and through all ages I shall not cease to be." She ministers in the holy tent and fixes her abode in Zion. This is a portrait of divine Wisdom choosing to dwell with a particular people — a foreshadowing of the Incarnation itself.

second.synopsis: Paul blesses God for blessing us "with every spiritual blessing in the heavens." Before the foundation of the world, God chose us to be holy and destined us for adoption through Jesus Christ. Paul then prays that God would give the community "a Spirit of wisdom and revelation" so that "the eyes of your hearts" may be enlightened. Key imagery: being chosen before creation, adopted as children, and given inner sight to see what that means.

gospel.synopsis: John's Prologue returns on this Sunday as the theological capstone of the Christmas season. "In the beginning was the Word" — present with God, identical with God, the agent of all creation. Life and light entered the world, but the world did not recognize him. To those who accepted him, he gave the power to become children of God. The turning point: "The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us." John the Baptist testifies, and from Christ's fullness we receive "grace in place of grace." No one has seen God, but the Son has revealed him.

abc-jan-04-2026-sun.invitesUsTo: This feast invites us to linger in the mystery of the Incarnation before the season passes. Sirach's Wisdom choosing to dwell among God's people is the Old Testament preview of John's staggering claim: the Word became flesh. We are invited to let Paul's prayer become our own — that the eyes of our hearts would be enlightened to grasp the hope, the riches, and the glory of being chosen and adopted before the world began.

independence-day.logline: A nation pauses to give thanks for freedom — and the Church asks what freedom is truly for.

first.synopsis: Isaiah envisions a time when the Spirit is poured out from on high and the wilderness becomes a garden. Justice will dwell in the desert and righteousness in the orchard. The fruit of justice will be peace; the effect of righteousness, calm and security. God's people will live in peaceful dwellings, secure homes, undisturbed resting places. It is a vision where true national security comes not from military power but from justice. Other readings may be selected, including Romans 13:1-7 on civic responsibility or Galatians 5:1, 13-18 on freedom ordered toward love.

second.synopsis: Paul declares, "For freedom Christ set us free" — then immediately warns against using that freedom as license for the flesh. Instead, serve one another through love. The whole law is summed up in one command: love your neighbor as yourself. If you bite and devour one another, you will be consumed. Freedom without love is just another kind of bondage. The parish may also choose 1 Corinthians 12:12-27 on the body of Christ or James 2:14-17 on faith and works.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus opens the Sermon on the Mount with the Beatitudes — nine declarations that overturn every expectation of who is blessed. The poor in spirit, the mourning, the meek, those hungering for righteousness, the merciful, the clean of heart, the peacemakers, the persecuted — these are the citizens of the kingdom. On a day that celebrates national ideals, the Beatitudes offer a different standard of greatness. The parish may also proclaim John 15:9-12 on remaining in Christ's love.

independence-day.invitesUsTo: This celebration invites us to hold gratitude and responsibility together. We give thanks for the freedoms we enjoy while acknowledging that Scripture never lets freedom stand alone — Isaiah ties security to justice, Paul ties freedom to love of neighbor, and Jesus ties blessing to poverty of spirit and peacemaking. We are invited to ask not only what our country has given us, but what we owe one another.

memorial-day.logline: The nation remembers those who died in service, and the Church entrusts them to a God who conquers death.

first.synopsis: "The souls of the just are in the hand of God, and no torment shall touch them." To the foolish, their death seemed like destruction — but they are at peace. Though punished in the eyes of mortals, their hope is full of immortality. God tested them like gold in the furnace and found them worthy. Those who trust in him will understand truth and abide with him in love. It is a passage that meets grief with unshakable confidence. The parish may also choose Isaiah 25:6a, 7-9 on God destroying death forever.

second.synopsis: Paul builds to one of Scripture's great crescendos: if God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own Son — how will he not give us everything else? Nothing can separate us from the love of Christ — not tribulation, distress, persecution, famine, nakedness, peril, or the sword. "In all these things we conquer overwhelmingly through him who loved us." Neither death nor life, neither angels nor principalities, neither present nor future — nothing in all creation can cut us off from God's love.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus speaks a single sentence that has echoed through every memorial service since: "No one has greater love than this, to lay down one's life for one's friends." In its original context, Jesus is speaking of his own coming sacrifice, but the Church has always heard in it a recognition of all who give their lives for others. The fuller passage (John 15:9-17) grounds this sacrifice in friendship with Christ and in the command to love one another. The parish may also choose John 14:1-6 on the Father's house with many rooms.

memorial-day.invitesUsTo: This observance invites us to honor the dead not with sentiment alone but with the boldest claim the Church can make: that death does not have the last word. The Book of Wisdom assures us that the just are in God's hands. Paul insists that nothing — not even death — can separate us from God's love. And Jesus names the ultimate act of love as laying down one's life. We are invited to remember with gratitude, to grieve with hope, and to recommit ourselves to the peace for which so many gave everything.

mlk-day.logline: The Church honors Dr. King's legacy by returning to the prophets who fueled his dream — justice, equality, beloved community.

first.synopsis: God rejects Israel's worship in the harshest terms: "I hate, I despise your feasts. Your burnt offerings do not please me." Take away the noise of your songs — God will not listen. Then the thunderclap: "Let justice surge like water, and goodness like an unfailing stream." This is the passage Dr. King quoted on the steps of the Lincoln Memorial. God is uninterested in religious performance disconnected from justice. The parish may also choose Isaiah 58:6-9, where God defines the fast he desires as releasing the oppressed and sheltering the homeless.

second.synopsis: Paul declares that through faith and baptism, all are clothed in Christ. "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free person, there is not male and female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus." The divisions that human societies build and defend — ethnic, economic, gendered — are dismantled at the baptismal font. If you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's descendants and heirs according to the promise. The beloved community is not a metaphor. It is baptismal reality.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus returns to Nazareth, enters the synagogue, and unrolls the scroll of Isaiah. He reads: "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to bring glad tidings to the poor. He has sent me to proclaim liberty to captives and recovery of sight to the blind, to let the oppressed go free, to proclaim a year acceptable to the Lord." He rolls up the scroll, sits down, and says, "Today this Scripture passage is fulfilled in your hearing." It is a mission statement for liberation. The parish may also proclaim Matthew 5:1-12a, the Beatitudes.

mlk-day.invitesUsTo: This observance invites us to hear the prophetic tradition that shaped Dr. King's vision and to recognize it as our own. Amos demands that justice flow like water — not trickle, not drip, but surge. Paul insists that in Christ there is no room for the divisions we cling to. And Jesus declares that his mission begins with the poor, the captive, the blind, and the oppressed. We are invited to move beyond admiring Dr. King's dream and to do the difficult, daily work of making the beloved community real.

thanksgiving.logline: One leper out of ten returns to give thanks — and Jesus asks where the other nine went.

first.synopsis: Sirach calls the assembly to bless the God of all, "who has done wondrous things on earth, who fosters people's growth from their mother's womb, and fashions them according to his will." The prayer asks for joyful hearts, lasting peace in Israel, and the assurance that God's mercy remains with us. It is a brief, full-throated blessing — the kind of prayer that belongs at a table surrounded by family. The parish may also choose Deuteronomy 8:7-18, Moses' warning to remember God when the harvest is plentiful and the houses are fine.

second.synopsis: Paul opens his letter to Corinth with thanksgiving: "I give thanks to my God always on your account for the grace of God bestowed on you in Christ Jesus." The community has been enriched in every way — in speech, in knowledge, in every spiritual gift. They lack nothing as they wait for the Lord's coming. And the anchor: "God is faithful, and by him you were called to fellowship with his Son." Gratitude here is not just for material blessings but for the gift of community and calling. The parish may also choose Colossians 3:12-17, which instructs us to let the word of Christ dwell in us and to do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus heals ten lepers on his way to Jerusalem. All ten are cleansed, but only one — a Samaritan, an outsider — turns back, falls at Jesus's feet, and gives thanks. Jesus asks the devastating question: "Were not ten made clean? Where are the other nine? Has none but this foreigner returned to give thanks to God?" He tells the man, "Stand up and go; your faith has saved you." The healing was given to all ten. The salvation belonged to the one who came back.

thanksgiving.invitesUsTo: This celebration invites us to be the one who turns back. Sirach blesses the God who fashions us from the womb. Paul gives thanks not for comfort but for grace, community, and calling. And Jesus makes clear that gratitude is not optional politeness — it is the response that completes the gift. We are invited to give thanks not merely for abundance on the table but for the God who provides, sustains, and calls us into fellowship. The question lingers: are we the one who returned, or the nine who kept walking?

veteran-s-day.logline: The parish honors living veterans — their courage, their sacrifice, and the peace we are called to build together.

first.synopsis: Isaiah envisions the Spirit poured out from on high, turning the wilderness into a garden. Justice will dwell in the desert, and the fruit of justice will be peace — calm and security forever. God's people will live in peaceful dwellings and undisturbed resting places. The vision is striking on a day that honors those who served in war: the ultimate goal of their sacrifice is not victory but peace. The parish may also choose Micah 4:1-4, the vision of swords beaten into plowshares.

second.synopsis: Paul lays out the marks of genuine Christian life: love without pretense, devotion to one another in mutual affection, patience in affliction, generosity to those in need, hospitality to strangers. He calls the community to rejoice with those who rejoice and weep with those who weep. The passage builds toward a challenge especially resonant for veterans and their families: "If possible, on your part, live at peace with all." The parish may also choose Ephesians 6:10-18 on the armor of God.

gospel.synopsis: Jesus commands his disciples: "Love one another as I love you. No one has greater love than this, to lay down one's life for one's friends." He then redefines their relationship — they are no longer servants but friends, because he has shared everything the Father told him. He chose them, not the other way around. On this day, the passage honors those who have risked or given their lives in service, while grounding all sacrifice in the love of Christ. The parish may also proclaim Matthew 5:1-12a, the Beatitudes.

veteran-s-day.invitesUsTo: This observance invites us to honor the men and women who have served with more than words — with a commitment to the peace and justice their service was meant to protect. Isaiah's vision of security rooted in righteousness reminds us that lasting peace is built, not merely won. Paul calls us to weep with those who weep — to accompany veterans in the wounds they carry home. And Jesus names the deepest truth: that laying down one's life for others is the highest expression of love. We are invited to give thanks for their courage and to work for the world they fought to make possible.

holy-thursday-lords-supper.logline: Holy Thursday — The night Jesus redefines love as service and gives himself as food: the Last Supper, the washing of feet, and the beginning of the Triduum.
holy-thursday-lords-supper.trajectory: The Triduum is one continuous liturgy across three days: Holy Thursday opens with the institution of the Eucharist and priesthood; Good Friday continues with the Passion and death; the Easter Vigil completes the movement through baptism and resurrection.

first.synopsis: The Lord gives Moses and Aaron the detailed instructions for the Passover meal — the lamb, the blood on the doorposts, the unleavened bread, the belt fastened, sandals on. This meal must be eaten in haste; it is the Lord's Passover. The blood on the doorposts will be a sign of protection when the destroyer passes through Egypt. And this day will be a memorial to keep as a perpetual feast. The entire Passover ritual that Jesus fulfills at the Last Supper has its roots in these precise, urgent instructions.

second.synopsis: Paul delivers what is almost certainly the earliest written account of the Lord's Supper, predating the Gospels. He received this from the Lord himself: on the night he was handed over, Jesus took bread, broke it, and said 'This is my body that is for you.' Then the cup: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood.' Paul adds the interpretive key: every time you eat and drink this, you proclaim the death of the Lord until he comes. This is not a memorial in the sense of merely remembering — it is a proclamation, an announcement of the Paschal Mystery to the world.

gospel.synopsis: John's account of the Last Supper has no bread-breaking or cup-sharing — instead it centers entirely on Jesus washing the feet of his disciples. Knowing that his hour has come, knowing who he is and where he is going, Jesus gets up from dinner, ties a towel around his waist, and kneels before his disciples. Peter resists violently; Jesus insists. When Jesus finishes, he asks: do you know what I have done to you? You call me Teacher and Lord, and rightly so. Then he gives them the model: if I, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you ought to wash one another's feet. I have given you an example.

holy-thursday-lords-supper.invitesUsTo: Holy Thursday invites us into the paradox at the heart of the faith: the one who is Lord becomes servant, the one who gives life gives his body as food. John's gospel and Paul's letter together frame the Eucharist not as passive reception but as commissioning — to be broken and poured out for others. The foot-washing is the enacted parable of the meal: both say the same thing about what love looks like when it has no agenda. The Passover reading grounds it all in salvation history — this is not a new thing God is doing, but the fulfillment of the oldest thing: liberation purchased through sacrifice. We are invited to receive this gift with open hands and to take seriously the towel Jesus has placed in ours.

good-friday-passion.logline: Good Friday — The servant who suffers in silence, the high priest who has been through every trial, and the Passion of John: how the death of Jesus transforms everything it touches.
good-friday-passion.trajectory: Good Friday is the second movement of the one Triduum liturgy — no Mass, no consecration, just the long listening to the Passion, the solemn intercessions for the whole world, and the slow, silent veneration of the Cross.

first.synopsis: The fourth Servant Song is one of the most haunting texts in all of scripture. Isaiah describes a figure disfigured beyond recognition, despised and rejected, a man of sorrows. He bore our infirmities, carried our sorrows, and was pierced for our offenses. Upon him was the punishment that makes us whole; by his wounds we were healed. The servant does not protest — he is led like a lamb to slaughter and opens not his mouth. But the conclusion is startling: the Lord's will shall be accomplished through him, and he shall see his descendants and justify many. Suffering is not the end of this story — vindication is.

second.synopsis: The Letter to the Hebrews makes a claim that would have sounded radical: Jesus is our great high priest — not because he was untouched by human weakness, but because he was tested in every way that we are, yet without sin. In the days of his flesh, he offered prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears, and he was heard. He learned obedience through what he suffered. This is a God who has been through it. The invitation that flows from this is extraordinary: let us approach the throne of grace with confidence, so that we may receive mercy and find grace in our time of need.

gospel.synopsis: John's Passion is written by someone who wants you to see clearly who is in charge. Jesus steps forward to identify himself to the soldiers — and they fall to the ground. He tells Pilate that Pilate would have no power if it had not been given from above. He carries his own cross to Calvary. From the cross he gives his mother and the beloved disciple to each other. He says 'I thirst' — and then 'It is finished.' He bows his head and hands over the spirit. John's Passion is not a story of victimhood but of sovereign love choosing to go all the way. The burial is careful, costly, and regal — anointed with a hundred pounds of myrrh and aloes.

good-friday-passion.invitesUsTo: Good Friday invites us into the hardest mystery: that God chose the cross. Isaiah's servant absorbs violence without retaliating and becomes, paradoxically, the instrument of healing for those who wounded him. Hebrews tells us this suffering was not incidental but formative — Jesus learned through it, became complete through it, and as a result can accompany us through ours. John's Passion refuses to let us pity Jesus; instead it asks us to see the cross as the moment of exaltation, the completion of love's logic. We are invited to come forward — slowly, in silence — and venerate the wood on which the salvation of the world has hung.

easter-vigil.logline: Easter Vigil — The longest liturgy of the year: creation, exodus, covenant, baptism, and resurrection, all gathered into one holy night that changes everything.
easter-vigil.trajectory: The Easter Vigil is the third and culminating movement of the Triduum. It moves from darkness to light, from silence to the first Alleluia, from the old covenant to the new, and from death to life — often anchored by the reception of new members of the Church.

first.synopsis: The Vigil offers up to seven Old Testament readings; the minimum is three, including Exodus 14. The Creation account (Gen 1–2) opens with God speaking the world into existence — light from darkness, order from chaos, goodness from nothing. Each day ends: it was good. The Exodus account is the Passover reading in motion — Moses stretches out his hand, the sea parts, Israel walks through on dry ground, and the Egyptians are swallowed. Then the Song of Moses: I will sing to the Lord, glorious in triumph! Isaiah 55 is the invitation of the covenant: come to the water, come without cost, come and eat. My word shall not return to me empty but shall achieve what I will.

second.synopsis: This is the baptismal epistle — the reading that makes explicit what the water means. Paul asks: do you not know that all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried with him through baptism into death, so that just as Christ was raised from the dead, we too might live in newness of life. The logic is compressed and stunning: if we have grown into union with him through a death like his, we shall also be united with him in a resurrection like his. The old self was crucified with him. The life he lives, he lives for God. This is the night that makes this true for the newly baptized.

gospel.synopsis: Luke's resurrection account belongs to the women. At dawn on the first day of the week, they come to the tomb with the spices they had prepared — and find the stone rolled away, the body gone. Two men in dazzling garments appear and ask the question that will echo through every Easter: why do you seek the living among the dead? He is not here; he has been raised. Remember what he told you in Galilee. The women remember. They go and tell the eleven and all the others. Peter runs to the tomb, sees only the burial cloths, and goes home amazed at what had happened.

easter-vigil.invitesUsTo: The Easter Vigil invites us to inhabit the entire arc of salvation history in a single night — to feel the weight of darkness before the first flame is struck, to hear creation pronounced good, to stand with Israel at the sea, to receive the invitation of the covenant. Then, at the Epistle, the turn: baptism is death and resurrection. The first Alleluia breaks open everything that has been held in silence since Ash Wednesday. The Gospel is spare and luminous — women with spices, an empty tomb, two shining men with the greatest question ever asked. This night invites us to be amazed, to remember, to believe that what happened then is happening now: the living Christ, not among the dead.

easter-sunday-resurrection.logline: Easter Sunday — The tomb is empty, Mary Magdalene is running, and Peter sees only burial cloths and wonder: the resurrection has begun.
easter-sunday-resurrection.trajectory: Easter Sunday launches the 50-day Easter season. The readings move from Peter's proclamation of the resurrection to the baptismal ethics of Colossians to the empty tomb in John — three angles on the same inexhaustible mystery.

first.synopsis: Peter is speaking to Cornelius — a Gentile, the first non-Jewish person to receive the Gospel — and he gives the earliest kerygmatic summary in the New Testament. You know what happened: Jesus of Nazareth was anointed with the Holy Spirit and power; he went about doing good and healing all those oppressed by the devil. They killed him, hanging him on a tree. But God raised him on the third day and granted that he be visible — not to all, but to witnesses chosen beforehand, to us who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead. He commanded us to testify. Everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness. This is the proclamation at the heart of the Easter season.

second.synopsis: Four crisp verses with an enormous claim: if you have been raised with Christ — and you have — then seek what is above, where Christ is seated at the right hand of God. Set your mind on things that are above, not on things that are on earth. For you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God. When Christ who is your life appears, then you also will appear with him in glory. Paul is writing to people who were baptized, telling them that their resurrection is not a future event but a present reality already underway. The life they are living now is already hidden in God.

gospel.synopsis: John's Easter morning is intimate and precise. Mary Magdalene comes to the tomb while it is still dark — and finds the stone removed. She runs to Peter and the beloved disciple: they have taken the Lord. The two men run; the beloved disciple arrives first but does not go in. Peter arrives and goes straight in: burial cloths lying there, the cloth that covered Jesus' head rolled up separately. Then the other disciple goes in, sees, and believes — though as yet they did not understand the scripture that he had to rise from the dead. John invites us to linger in the details: the folded cloth, the darkness giving way, two people running toward something they cannot yet explain.

easter-sunday-resurrection.invitesUsTo: Easter Sunday invites us to stand at the entrance of the empty tomb and make a decision: to believe, as the beloved disciple believed, even before fully understanding. Peter's speech in Acts tells us what the church has been saying since the beginning — not an abstraction but a testimony, from people who ate and drank with the risen Christ. Colossians tells us that this resurrection is not only past and future but present: our life is already hidden with Christ in God. John's gospel asks us to notice what is absent — not the body but the grave clothes, neatly folded, as if to say: he had all the time in the world. We are invited to run toward the impossible and find it true.
